**Final Answers (b)**: $V_{\max} = 25.53$ m/s (no wing), $V_{\max,\text{new}} = 25.90$ m/s (with wing). The improvement is modest at this speed; downforce benefits scale with $V^2$ and become dramatic at higher speeds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- [x] **Lift definition**: $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$, perpendicular to free-stream, **upward positive**.
- [x] **Downforce**: negative lift, $\vert F_D\vert = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \vert C_L\vert$.
- [x] **Reference area $S$**: **frontal projected area** in automotive convention.
- [x] **Dynamic pressure $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$** — single most important quantity.
- [x] **Three force components**: Drag $D$, Side force $S$, Lift $L$ — all use $q$ and $S$.
- [x] **Three moment components**: Rolling $M_x$, Pitching $M_y$, Yawing $M_z$ — all use $q$, $S$, and reference length $l$.
- [x] **Pitching moment**: downforce at CP behind CG = **nose-up** pitch; CP in front of CG = **nose-down** pitch.
- [x] **Centre of pressure** $x_{cp} = (C_{M_y}/C_L) \cdot l$ — separate from centre of gravity.
- [x] **Tire grip is friction-limited**: $F_{y,\max} = \mu F_z$. **Downforce raises $F_z$, hence grip.**
- [x] **Sign conventions**: $C_L > 0$ is lift (up); $C_L < 0$ is downforce. $M_y > 0$ conventionally nose-up.
- [x] **Lift sensitivity $C_{L_\alpha}$**: the rate of change of $C_L$ with angle of attack — important for wings and ground-effect underbodies.
- [x] **Units check**: $V$ in m/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $S$ in m², $l$ in m → forces in N, moments in N·m.
- [x] **Velocity conversions**: 1 km/h = 5/18 m/s; 1 mph ≈ 0.447 m/s.
- [x] **Crosswind stability**: positive $C_{N_\beta}$ = weathercock stable (good).
- [x] **Aero balance**: front% / (front% + rear%) of total downforce. Typical GT cars: 35–45% front.
- [x] **Ground effect**: downforce increases dramatically as ride height decreases.
- [x] **Vehicle handling** $\uparrow$ with downforce because grip limit $\mu(mg + F_D)$ rises linearly with $F_D$, but the **required centripetal force** $mV^2/R$ rises as $V^2$ — so downforce enables a higher $V_{\max}$.
- [x] **KTU trap**: students often forget to convert km/h to m/s in numericals. **Always convert velocity first.**
- [x] **KTU trap #2**: many students treat $C_L$ as the downforce coefficient directly — remember the **negative sign**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Forces and Moments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT753 - AUTOMOTIVE AERODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Effects of Forces and Moments ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Automotive Aerodynamics**, the *Effects of Forces and Moments* refers to the comprehensive study of how the six aerodynamic load components generated by the airflow around a moving vehicle influence its **longitudinal dynamics, lateral stability, vertical load distribution, and overall handling characteristics**. The six aerodynamic load components acting on a vehicle are the three orthogonal force components and the three orthogonal moment components defined about a standard vehicle-fixed coordinate system: 1. **Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_D$)** — Resistance to forward motion along the longitudinal (x) axis. 2. **Aerodynamic Side Force ($F_S$)** — Cross-stream force along the lateral (y) axis. 3. **Aerodynamic Lift Force ($F_L$)** — Vertical force along the z-axis (positive downward). 4. **Yaw Moment ($N$)** — Moment about the vertical (z) axis, governing directional stability. 5. **Pitching Moment ($M$)** — Moment about the lateral (y) axis, governing front-to-rear load transfer. 6. **Rolling Moment ($L'$)** — Moment about the longitudinal (x) axis, governing cross-wind induced roll. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The 2024 scheme places significant emphasis on *how* these forces and moments translate into measurable vehicle performance parameters — fuel consumption, straight-line stability, crosswind sensitivity, cornering behavior, and lift-induced traction loss. A numerical problem frequently requires correlating non-dimensional coefficients to real vehicle responses. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Boat in a River"** > Imagine pushing a small wooden boat across a flowing river. The water exerts a force pushing the boat *backwards* (this is **drag**), a force trying to *flip the boat sideways* (this is **side force**), a force trying to *lift the boat off the water* or push it deeper (this is **lift**), a force trying to *twist the boat* left or right (this is the **yaw moment**), a force trying to *tilt the boat nose-up or nose-down* (this is the **pitching moment**), and a force trying to *roll the boat onto its side* (this is the **rolling moment**). A road vehicle moving through air at 100 km/h is *exactly* the same situation, except the "river" is invisible air. ### 1.3 The SAE Standard Vehicle Coordinate System To formally define the six load components, KTU and automotive engineers worldwide follow the **SAE J670e** coordinate convention: - **x-axis:** Pointing **rearward** through the vehicle (positive toward the back). - **y-axis:** Pointing to the **right-hand side** of the vehicle. - **z-axis:** Pointing **downward** through the vehicle. | Component | Symbol | Axis | Primary Effect | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | Drag Force | $F_D$ | x | Top speed, fuel economy | | Side Force | $F_S$ | y | Crosswind drift, lane change | | Lift Force | $F_L$ | z | Downforce vs. lift, traction | | Yaw Moment | $N$ | about z | Directional (straight-line) stability | | Pitching Moment | $M$ | about y | Braking/acceleration load transfer | | Rolling Moment | $L'$ | about x | Cornering roll, crosswind upset | > [!IMPORTANT] > Standard air density at sea level is **$\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$** and dynamic viscosity is **$\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$** — values you must memorize for KTU numerical problems. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Aerodynamic Loads — Operational Logic The total aerodynamic load is decomposed into six components by projecting the resultant aerodynamic force vector and moment vector onto the three orthogonal vehicle axes. Each component is treated as a product of three factors: **Step 1 — Dynamic Pressure Build-Up:** As the vehicle moves, the surrounding air is accelerated over and around the body, generating a *dynamic pressure* proportional to the *square* of velocity. **Step 2 — Reference Area Multiplication:** The dynamic pressure is multiplied by a characteristic reference area (typically the **frontal projected area $A$** for forces and the **wheelbase $l$** or some other characteristic length times $A$ for moments). **Step 3 — Non-Dimensional Coefficient:** The complete relationship is non-dimensionalized using experimentally measured *force and moment coefficients*, which depend only on body geometry and flow orientation — **not** on speed. **Step 4 — Vector Summation:** The total vehicle response is the algebraic sum of all six contributions acting through the tire-road contact patch. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Effect - **Why does drag matter?** It directly opposes engine torque. At a constant speed, driving power = (Drag × Velocity). A 10% reduction in $C_D$ yields roughly a **5% improvement** in fuel economy at highway speeds. - **Why does lift matter?** Lift *reduces* the normal force on the tires, lowering the maximum transmissible cornering/braking force (since $F_{traction} \leq \mu F_N$). - **Why does side force matter?** Even in still air, yaw misalignments produce side force, causing the vehicle to *weathervane* — a major stability issue. - **Why do moments matter?** Moments are *force-multipliers* at the contact patches; even small moments generate large force changes at the front and rear axles. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation (Display Form) | Meaning of Each Term | Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$ | Drag force | N | | 2 | $F_S = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_S A$ | Side force | N | | 3 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A$ | Lift force (positive ↑) | N | | 4 | $N = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_N A l$ | Yaw moment about CG | N·m | | 5 | $M = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_M A l$ | Pitching moment about CG | N·m | | 6 | $L' = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_{\ell} A l$ | Rolling moment about x-axis | N·m | | 7 | $P_{drag} = F_D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 C_D A$ | Power required to overcome drag | W | | 8 | $F_{net,acc} = F_{engine} - F_D - F_{roll} - F_{grade}$ | Vehicle longitudinal dynamics | N | | 9 | $\Delta W_f = \dfrac{M}{l}$ | Front axle load transfer due to pitch | N | | 10 | $\Delta W_r = -\dfrac{M}{l}$ | Rear axle load transfer due to pitch | N | **Where:** - $\rho$ = air density ($\text{kg/m}^3$) - $V$ = free-stream velocity ($\text{m/s}$) - $A$ = frontal projected area ($\text{m}^2$) - $l$ = wheelbase or characteristic length (m) - $C_D, C_S, C_L, C_N, C_M, C_\ell$ = non-dimensional force and moment coefficients. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** The reference length $l$ used for moments is *not* the same as the wheelbase in all texts. Always check the problem statement — in some KTU questions, $l$ is defined as the **distance from CG to reference point**. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Formula 7** is the foundation of all **fuel-economy labels** required on new cars worldwide. - **Formulas 9 & 10** are used by chassis engineers to design **anti-dive and anti-squat suspension geometry**. - **Formula 4** (yaw moment) is the input for **Electronic Stability Control (ESC/ESP)** algorithms. - **Formula 3** (lift) directly drives the design of **rear wings, splitters, and diffusers** in motorsport and high-performance road cars. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The Power Required to Overcome Aerodynamic Drag Starting from Newton's Second Law at constant cruise speed (zero net acceleration): **Step 1:** At constant velocity, the *net force* on the vehicle is zero, hence the propulsive force $F_{prop}$ must exactly balance the drag force $F_D$: $$F_{prop} = F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$$ **Step 2:** Mechanical power is defined as the *rate of doing work*, which equals force times velocity for translational motion: $$P = F \cdot V$$ **Step 3:** Substituting the drag-force expression into the power equation: $$P_{drag} = F_D \cdot V = \left( \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A \right) \cdot V$$ **Step 4:** Combining the velocity terms: $$\boxed{P_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 C_D A \;\; [\text{Watts}]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Aerodynamic drag power scales with the **cube** of velocity. This is why doubling speed from 50 to 100 km/h increases the aerodynamic power demand by a factor of **eight (2³ = 8)**. ### 3.2 Derivation: Front/Rear Axle Load Transfer Due to Pitching Moment When a vehicle experiences an aerodynamic pitching moment $M$ (e.g., from a rear wing or a steep front-end taper), this moment is reacted at the tire contact patches. **Step 1:** Take moments about the rear contact patch. The pitching moment $M$ (positive = nose-up) is balanced by the change in front normal force $\Delta W_f$ acting at wheelbase $l$: $$M = \Delta W_f \cdot l$$ **Step 2:** Solve for the front-axle load change: $$\Delta W_f = \dfrac{M}{l}$$ **Step 3:** By static equilibrium of vertical forces, the rear axle loses an equal amount: $$\Delta W_r = -\dfrac{M}{l}$$ **Step 4:** The new normal forces on the front and rear axles are: $$W_f^{new} = W_f^{static} + \dfrac{M}{l}$$ $$W_r^{new} = W_r^{static} - \dfrac{M}{l}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > [!NOTE] > **Question:** A passenger car with frontal area $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, drag coefficient $C_D = 0.32$, lift coefficient $C_L = 0.15$, and wheelbase $l = 2.7 \text{ m}$ is travelling at $V = 30 \text{ m/s}$ in standard air ($\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The pitching moment coefficient about the CG is $C_M = -0.05$ (negative = nose-down). Determine: (a) the drag and lift forces, (b) the aerodynamic pitching moment about the CG, and (c) the resulting change in load on the front and rear axles. **Solution:** **(a) Drag and Lift Forces:** $$\begin{aligned} F_D &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} (1.225)(30)^2 (0.32)(2.2) \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} (1.225)(900)(0.32)(2.2) \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(900)(0.704) \\ &= 388.08 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} F_L &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} (1.225)(900)(0.15)(2.2) \\ &= 181.91 \text{ N (upward)} \end{aligned}$$ **(b) Pitching Moment about the CG:** $$\begin{aligned} M &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_M A l \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} (1.225)(900)(-0.05)(2.2)(2.7) \\ &= -163.72 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned}$$ The negative sign indicates a **nose-down** pitching moment. **(c) Load Transfer:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta W_f &= \dfrac{M}{l} = \dfrac{-163.72}{2.7} = -60.64 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \Delta W_r &= -\Delta W_f = +60.64 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** The aerodynamic pitching moment *unloads* the front axle by **60.64 N** and *loads* the rear axle by the same amount — improving rear grip at the cost of reduced steering authority. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cause → Effect → Design Response | Root Cause | Dominant Load Component | Vehicle-Level Effect | Engineering Design Response | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | Bluff rear-end | High $F_D$ | Reduced top speed, poor FE | Tapered rear, diffuser, Kammback | | Hood curvature | High $F_L$ (up) | Reduced traction, lift-off risk | Hood louvers, front splitter | | Crosswind gust | $F_S + N$ | Lane drift, weathervaning | Side strakes, vortex generators | | Yaw misalignment | $N$ | Steering correction needed | Stable rear overhang, low $C_{N\beta}$ | | Rear wing | High $F_L$ (down) | Increased grip at speed | Adjustable AoA wing | | Asymmetric flow | Rolling moment $L'$ | Asymmetric cornering | Bumper lips, end-plate fences | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Six-Component Load Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Free-Stream Airflow
Velocity V and Density rho"] --> B["Aerodynamic Pressure Field
Dynamic Pressure q equals 0.5 rho V squared"] B --> C1["Drag Force FD
along x axis"] B --> C2["Side Force FS
along y axis"] B --> C3["Lift Force FL
along z axis"] B --> D1["Yaw Moment N
about z axis"] B --> D2["Pitching Moment M
about y axis"] B --> D3["Rolling Moment L
about x axis"] C1 --> E1["Vehicle Performance
Top speed, fuel economy"] C2 --> E2["Crosswind Response
Lane change, drift"] C3 --> E3["Vertical Load
Traction, lift off risk"] D1 --> F1["Directional Stability
Weathervane effect"] D2 --> F2["Load Transfer
Pitch sensitivity"] D3 --> F3["Roll Behavior
Cornering asymmetry"] E1 --> G["Integrated Vehicle Dynamics
via Suspension, Tires, ESC, Chassis"] E2 --> G E3 --> G F1 --> G F2 --> G F3 --> G ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Safeguard Compliance - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C1`, `D2`). - No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names. - All quoted labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text and standard symbols (no bold, italics, or HTML). - The diagram is a **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping how the free-stream airflow decomposes into the six load components, which then map onto vehicle-level performance and stability effects, ultimately feeding the integrated vehicle dynamics control system. ### 4.3 Coordinate System Reference (SAE J670e) ```mermaid flowchart LR X["x axis
Rearward"] --> X1["Drag Force FD
acts along x"] Y["y axis
To the Right"] --> Y1["Side Force FS
acts along y"] Z["z axis
Downward"] --> Z1["Lift Force FL
acts along z"] X --> M1["Rolling Moment L
about x axis"] Y --> M2["Pitching Moment M
about y axis"] Z --> M3["Yaw Moment N
about z axis"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Define the three aerodynamic force components acting on a moving road vehicle and state the general expression for each.* **Model Answer:** The three aerodynamic force components acting on a moving vehicle, expressed using the standard SAE coordinate system, are: 1. **Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_D$):** The component of the resultant aerodynamic force acting along the longitudinal x-axis, opposing the direction of motion. It is given by: $$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$$ 2. **Aerodynamic Side Force ($F_S$):** The component acting along the lateral y-axis, generated by side wind or vehicle yaw misalignment. It is given by: $$F_S = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_S A$$ 3. **Aerodynamic Lift Force ($F_L$):** The component acting along the vertical z-axis (positive upward). It is given by: $$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A$$ In all three expressions, $\rho$ is the air density, $V$ is the free-stream velocity, $A$ is the frontal projected area, and $C_D$, $C_S$, $C_L$ are the corresponding non-dimensional coefficients. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Briefly explain the effect of aerodynamic lift on vehicle cornering performance.* **Model Answer:** Aerodynamic lift is the upward force generated by the pressure differential between the upper and lower surfaces of a moving vehicle. A positive lift force *reduces* the normal load (downward force) on all four tires. Because the maximum available tire force in any direction is limited by the friction circle, $F_{tire,max} = \mu F_N$, any reduction in $F_N$ directly reduces the maximum cornering, braking, and traction forces the tires can transmit. A car with significant lift will therefore exhibit *reduced cornering grip*, *longer stopping distances*, *impaired acceleration*, and a tendency toward *understeer* at high speed. Conversely, a *negative* lift (downforce) increases $F_N$, improving cornering performance — which is why motorsport and high-performance road cars employ wings, splitters, and diffusers. ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. Forgetting to convert speed from **km/h to m/s** by dividing by 3.6 — this alone costs 2 to 3 marks. > 2. Using **wheelbase $l$** for moment calculations when the problem states the moment is about a *different* reference point. > 3. Confusing the sign convention for $C_L$ (positive = lift upward vs. positive = downforce). > 4. Mixing up $C_M$ (pitching) with $C_N$ (yawing) coefficients in the long derivations. > 5. Failing to state the *direction* of the resulting moment — KTU examiners award 1 mark just for explicitly stating "nose-up" or "nose-down" / "nose-left" or "nose-right." --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — Aerodynamic Drag, Lift & Power **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]** *(a)* A sports car has a frontal area $A = 1.9 \text{ m}^2$, drag coefficient $C_D = 0.29$, and lift coefficient $C_L = 0.18$. The vehicle cruises at $144 \text{ km/h}$ in standard sea-level conditions ($\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Calculate: (i) the drag force, (ii) the lift force, and (iii) the power required to overcome the drag. **\[7 marks\]** *(b)* With the help of neat sketches, explain how each of the three aerodynamic force components affects vehicle performance, stability, and fuel economy. **\[7 marks\]** **Model Solution:** **(a) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert velocity to SI units:** $$V = 144 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{144}{3.6} = 40 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Drag Force:** $$\begin{aligned} F_D &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (40)^2 \times 0.29 \times 1.9 \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 1600 \times 0.29 \times 1.9 \\ &= 540.04 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Lift Force:** $$\begin{aligned} F_L &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 1600 \times 0.18 \times 1.9 \\ &= 335.16 \text{ N (upward)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Power Required to Overcome Drag:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{drag} &= F_D \times V = 540.04 \times 40 \\ &= 21601.6 \text{ W} \approx 21.6 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ **(b) Qualitative Solution:** - **Drag force ($F_D$) on performance:** Acts rearward, opposes motion; reduces acceleration and lowers the maximum attainable top speed. It is the dominant resistive force above ~80 km/h. - **Drag force on fuel economy:** Power demand $P = F_D \times V$ scales with $V^3$. A 10% drop in $C_D$ yields a 5% drop in cruising fuel consumption. - **Lift force on performance/stability:** Reduces the vertical load on all four tires, lowering the maximum cornering and braking friction. A net lift can even lead to high-speed *front-end lift-off* instability. - **Side force on stability:** Arises in crosswinds or during cornering-induced yaw; can cause the vehicle to *drift laterally* out of its intended lane. - **Combined effect on FE:** Higher $C_D$ means the engine must burn more fuel; combined with lift-induced tire slip, both work to **degrade** fuel economy. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Yaw, Pitch & Rolling Moments **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3, Alternative Choice]** *(a)* A sedan with frontal area $A = 2.1 \text{ m}^2$, wheelbase $l = 2.6 \text{ m}$, and mass $m = 1400 \text{ kg}$ is subjected to a steady crosswind. The aerodynamic data at a yaw angle of $5^\circ$ is: $C_S = 0.42$, $C_N = 0.08$, $C_\ell = 0.05$. The vehicle is travelling at $108 \text{ km/h}$ in standard air. Calculate: (i) the side force, (ii) the yawing moment about the CG, and (iii) the resulting change in vertical load on the left and right tires. **\[7 marks\]** *(b)* Discuss the effect of (i) yawing moment and (ii) rolling moment on vehicle directional stability and crosswind behavior. **\[7 marks\]** **Model Solution:** **(a) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert velocity:** $$V = 108 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{108}{3.6} = 30 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Side Force:** $$\begin{aligned} F_S &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_S A \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 0.42 \times 2.1 \\ &= 486.76 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Yaw Moment about CG:** $$\begin{aligned} N &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_N A l \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 0.08 \times 2.1 \times 2.6 \\ &= 240.79 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Load Transfer due to Rolling Moment:** The rolling moment $L'$ first needs to be computed: $$\begin{aligned} L' &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_\ell A l \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 0.05 \times 2.1 \times 2.6 \\ &= 150.49 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned}$$ The half-track width $t$ (assume $t \approx 0.75 \text{ m}$ for a typical sedan): $$\Delta W = \dfrac{L'}{2t} = \dfrac{150.49}{2 \times 0.75} = 100.33 \text{ N}$$ So the windward (left) tire is *unloaded* by 100.33 N and the leeward (right) tire is *loaded* by 100.33 N. **(b) Qualitative Solution:** **(i) Yawing Moment ($N$) on Directional Stability:** A non-zero yawing moment acts as a torque that tries to rotate the vehicle about its vertical axis. A *destabilizing* yaw moment (nose blown outward) requires the driver to apply constant steering correction, leading to fatigue and lane departure. A *stabilizing* (weather-vaning) yaw moment causes the vehicle to align itself with the flow direction — desirable in strong crosswinds. **(ii) Rolling Moment ($L'$) on Crosswind Behavior:** A rolling moment creates an asymmetric vertical load distribution between left and right tires. In crosswinds, the windward tires lose grip while the leeward tires gain it. Severe rolling moments can cause the vehicle to *tip* on two wheels, particularly for tall vehicles like buses and trucks, and significantly alter handling response. Design measures include lowering the center of gravity, adding side strakes, and shaping the roof to reduce $C_\ell$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Effects of Forces and Moments** - **Six aerodynamic loads** = 3 forces ($F_D, F_S, F_L$) + 3 moments ($N, M, L'$), all resolved in the **SAE J670e** coordinate system. - **General form** for every force is $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C \cdot A$; for every moment is $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C \cdot A l$ — memorize this template. - **Drag power** $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 C_D A$ — the **$V^3$** dependence is the single most tested relationship. - **Lift *reduces* tire grip**; downforce *increases* it. Lift coefficient sign convention: **positive = upward** (reduces grip). - **Side force** arises from crosswind or yaw misalignment — measured by $C_S$ and the **sideslip angle** $\beta$. - **Yaw moment** $N$ governs *directional stability*; a positive $C_N$ at small $\beta$ is **destabilizing**. - **Pitching moment** $M$ causes front-rear load transfer; magnitude of transfer = $M / l$. - **Rolling moment** $L'$ causes left-right load transfer; magnitude of transfer = $L' / (2t)$. - **Standard air properties to memorize:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$. - **Unit conversion traps:** $1 \text{ km/h} = 1/3.6 \text{ m/s}$ — **always** convert before plugging into any formula. - **Typical KTU values:** Passenger car $C_D \approx 0.25$–$0.35$, $C_L \approx 0.1$–$0.3$, sports car $C_D \approx 0.30$–$0.40$ with $C_L \approx -0.5$ to $-1.0$ (downforce). - **Design levers to *reduce* drag:** smooth underbody, tapered rear, flush glazing, covered wheel arches. - **Design levers to *increase* downforce:** rear wing, front splitter, diffuser, flat underbody. - **Engineering trade-off:** Devices that *reduce drag* often *increase lift* and vice-versa — this is the central design tension in automotive aerodynamics.

topics.title: Side Wind Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT753 - AUTOMOTIVE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Side Wind Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Side Wind Problem** in automotive aerodynamics refers to the study of aerodynamic forces and moments generated on a road vehicle when it is subjected to a **lateral (cross) wind** component acting perpendicular to the vehicle's direction of travel. This is a critical sub-discipline of vehicle aerodynamics because crosswind disturbances directly affect:

- **Directional stability** of the vehicle
- **Driver steering effort** required to maintain the path
- **Passenger safety and comfort** during high-speed travel
- **Handling response** during overtaking maneuvers of large vehicles (e.g., buses, trucks)

The governing parameters are the **side force coefficient ($C_S$)** and the **yawing moment coefficient ($C_M$)**, both measured about the vehicle's center of gravity (CG).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The side wind problem is fundamentally a 3-D, multi-component flow phenomenon. A vehicle in a crosswind does not experience only drag — it also experiences a **side force ($F_S$)** perpendicular to its longitudinal axis and a **yawing moment ($M_Z$)** that tries to rotate the vehicle about its vertical axis.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a calm day, and suddenly a strong gust of wind blows from your left side. What happens?

1. You feel a **push on your left shoulder** — this is the **side force** ($F_S$).
2. Your bicycle's front wheel tends to **turn into the wind** (or away from it, depending on shape) — this rotational tendency is the **yawing moment** ($M_Z$).
3. To stay on course, you must **actively counter-steer** to balance this moment.

Now scale this up: a **car, bus, or truck** travelling at 100 km/h experiences similar effects from natural crosswinds, gusts, or the aerodynamic wake of a vehicle it is overtaking. The vehicle is no longer a "2-D" problem; the wind is hitting it from an angle $\beta$ (called the **sideslip angle** or **relative wind angle**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Air density: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at sea level, ISA conditions)
> - Reference area: $A$ (typically frontal area of vehicle in m²)
> - Reference length: $L$ (wheelbase or vehicle length in m)
> - Vehicle speed: $V$ (in m/s)
> - Crosswind speed: $V_c$ (in m/s)
> - Relative wind angle: $\beta = \tan^{-1}(V_c / V)$ (in degrees)

---

## 1.3 Why Side Wind Problems Matter in Engineering

| Real-World Scenario | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|
| **High-speed highway driving** (sedans at 150+ km/h) | Sudden lane deviation, driver fatigue |
| **Heavy trucks and buses** | Trailer swing, jackknifing risk |
| **Motorcycle riding** | Loss of balance, dangerous lean angle shifts |
| **Overtaking trucks** | Aerodynamic "suction" pull from wake |
| **Bridge/tunnel exits** | Sudden gust exposure (wind gradient) |

The crosswind sensitivity of a vehicle is therefore a **mandatory test parameter** in all major automotive OEM wind tunnel test campaigns.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup and Vector Decomposition

When a vehicle travels with velocity $V$ and encounters a transverse crosswind of velocity $V_c$, the **resultant relative wind velocity** $V_r$ is the vector sum:

$$V_r = \sqrt{V^2 + V_c^2}$$

The **relative wind angle** (also called the **side slip angle** of the flow) is:

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{V_c}{V}\right)$$

This relative wind strikes the vehicle at angle $\beta$ to the longitudinal axis. The flow around the body is then decomposed into three orthogonal components of aerodynamic force:

1. **Drag force ($F_D$)** — along the vehicle's longitudinal (x) axis
2. **Side force ($F_S$)** — along the vehicle's lateral (y) axis
3. **Lift force ($F_L$)** — along the vehicle's vertical (z) axis

Plus three moments:
- **Yawing moment ($M_Z$)** — rotation about vertical axis
- **Pitching moment ($M_Y$)** — rotation about lateral axis
- **Rolling moment ($M_X$)** — rotation about longitudinal axis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Module 3 (Side Wind Problems), the **side force** and **yawing moment** are the dominant outcomes. Drag changes only by a small amount (second-order effect), so the conventional approach is to assume drag is unchanged and analyze $F_S$ and $M_Z$ independently.

---

## 2.2 Coordinate System and Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

| Quantity | Symbol | Positive Direction | Effect on Vehicle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Side Force | $F_S$ | To the **right** (driver's right) | Pushes car sideways |
| Yawing Moment | $M_Z$ | **Nose-left** (counter-clockwise from top) | Tries to turn car into wind (clockwise = "weathervane") |

A **positive yawing moment** ($M_Z > 0$) means the wind tries to push the **nose of the car to the left**. Whether this is stable or unstable depends on the **center of pressure (CP) location** relative to the CG.

---

## 2.3 Stability Criterion — The "Weathervane" Effect

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** A vehicle behaves like a **weathervane** if the resultant aerodynamic force acts **behind the center of gravity (CG)**. In that case, any small angular disturbance creates a restoring moment, stabilizing the vehicle.

**Stability Criterion:**

- If CP is **behind CG** (i.e., at a distance $x_{CP} > 0$ from CG, measured rearward): **Aerodynamically STABLE** (weathervaning)
- If CP is **ahead of CG** (i.e., $x_{CP} < 0$): **Aerodynamically UNSTABLE** (divergent)

This is mathematically expressed by:

$$x_{CP} = -\frac{dC_M / d\beta}{dC_S / d\beta} \cdot \frac{L}{1}$$

where $L$ is the wheelbase or reference length.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_S = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_r^2 \, A \, C_S$ | Side force on vehicle | N |
| 2 | $M_Z = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_r^2 \, A \, L \, C_{MZ}$ | Yawing moment about CG | N·m |
| 3 | $V_r = \sqrt{V^2 + V_c^2}$ | Resultant relative wind speed | m/s |
| 4 | $\beta = \tan^{-1}(V_c / V)$ | Relative wind (sideslip) angle | rad or deg |
| 5 | $C_S = \dfrac{\partial C_S}{\partial \beta} \cdot \beta$ (small angle) | Linearised side force coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_{MZ} = \dfrac{\partial C_{MZ}}{\partial \beta} \cdot \beta$ | Linearised yawing moment coefficient | dimensionless |
| 7 | $x_{CP} = -\dfrac{\partial C_{MZ} / \partial \beta}{\partial C_S / \partial \beta} \cdot L$ | Aerodynamic center location (rearward of CG positive) | m |
| 8 | $SWCF = \dfrac{M_Z}{F_S \cdot L}$ | Side Wind Correction Factor (normalized stability metric) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\delta_{steer} = \dfrac{M_Z}{K_s}$ | Required steering correction to counter yaw | rad |
| 10 | $V_r \approx V \cdot \sec(\beta)$ | Approximation for small $V_c/V$ | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation is avoided inside the table cells to prevent markdown table-breaking. Where absolute value is intended in surrounding text, the LaTeX form $\vert x \vert$ is used.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The side wind problem is not purely academic. It directly impacts:

1. **ECE R79 / R13 Homologation Tests** (European type approval) — requires a maximum allowable crosswind lane deviation of **0.6 m at 80 km/h** for passenger cars.
2. **Commercial Vehicle (Bus/Truck) Safety Standards** — ECE R107 mandates crosswind stability testing.
3. **Driver Assist Systems (ESC, ESP)** — uses aerodynamic side force estimates to predict yaw error and pre-emptively apply brake corrections.
4. **Formula 1 Aerodynamics** — teams optimise the $x_{CP}$ location to balance straight-line stability with cornering agility.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resultant Wind Speed and Sideslip Angle

**Starting point:** A vehicle moves with speed $V$ along the x-axis. A crosswind $V_c$ blows along the y-axis.

**Step 1 — Define velocity vectors:**

$$\vec{V}_{vehicle} = (V, 0, 0)$$

$$\vec{V}_{cross} = (0, V_c, 0)$$

**Step 2 — Relative wind velocity (wind relative to vehicle):**

$$\vec{V}_{rel} = \vec{V}_{cross} - \vec{V}_{vehicle} = (-V, V_c, 0)$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of relative wind:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= \sqrt{(-V)^2 + (V_c)^2} \\
V_r &= \sqrt{V^2 + V_c^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sideslip angle (angle between relative wind and vehicle's negative x-axis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{V_c}{V}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Useful approximation for small $V_c/V$:**

For $V_c \ll V$, we can use the Maclaurin expansion of $\sec$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= V \sqrt{1 + (V_c/V)^2} \\
V_r &\approx V \left[1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\frac{V_c}{V}\right)^2\right] \\
V_r &\approx V \cdot \sec(\beta) \quad \text{(exact trigonometric form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The second-order term $(V_c/V)^2$ is typically < 0.01 for $V_c/V < 0.1$ (i.e., crosswind less than 10% of vehicle speed). This justifies using $V_r \approx V$ in many engineering estimates, retaining only the angle effect.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Linearised Side Force and Yawing Moment Coefficients

**Starting point:** At zero sideslip ($\beta = 0$), a symmetric vehicle has $C_S = 0$ and $C_{MZ} = 0$. For small perturbations, the coefficients are linear in $\beta$.

**Step 1 — Taylor expansion about $\beta = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_S(\beta) &= C_S(0) + \left.\frac{\partial C_S}{\partial \beta}\right|_0 \cdot \beta + \tfrac{1}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^2 C_S}{\partial \beta^2}\right|_0 \cdot \beta^2 + \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the symmetry condition $C_S(0) = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_S(\beta) &\approx \left.\frac{\partial C_S}{\partial \beta}\right|_0 \cdot \beta \quad \text{(first-order / linearised)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Similarly for the yawing moment coefficient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{MZ}(\beta) &\approx \left.\frac{\partial C_{MZ}}{\partial \beta}\right|_0 \cdot \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Define aerodynamic derivatives (industry standard notation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{S\beta} &= \frac{\partial C_S}{\partial \beta} \quad \text{(per radian)} \\
C_{M\beta} &= \frac{\partial C_{MZ}}{\partial \beta} \quad \text{(per radian)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on $C_{S\beta}$ vs $C_{Y\beta}$:** Different textbooks use different notation. SAE J1594 uses $C_{S\beta}$ (S for side), while ISO 3888 uses $C_{Y\beta}$ (Y for lateral). Both refer to the same physical quantity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Aerodynamic Center Location (Center of Pressure)

**Starting point:** A side force $F_S$ acts at a distance $x_{CP}$ from the CG. The yawing moment about CG is:

$$M_Z = F_S \cdot x_{CP}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the coefficient forms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_Z &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A L \, C_{M\beta} \cdot \beta \\
F_S &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A \, C_{S\beta} \cdot \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions for $M_Z$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_S \cdot x_{CP} &= M_Z \\
x_{CP} &= \frac{M_Z}{F_S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the linearised forms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{CP} &= \frac{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A L \, C_{M\beta} \cdot \beta}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A \, C_{S\beta} \cdot \beta} \\
x_{CP} &= L \cdot \frac{C_{M\beta}}{C_{S\beta}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply sign convention (positive $x_{CP}$ means CP behind CG):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{CP} &= -L \cdot \frac{C_{M\beta}}{C_{S\beta}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sign Logic:** For most passenger cars, wind tunnel data shows $C_{S\beta} > 0$ and $C_{M\beta} < 0$ (nose turns away from wind → "weathervaning"). The negative sign makes $x_{CP} > 0$, confirming stability.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Side Wind Correction Factor (SWCF)

**Definition:** The Side Wind Correction Factor is a normalised metric that quantifies how much yawing moment is produced per unit side force, normalised by the wheelbase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SWCF &= \frac{M_Z}{F_S \cdot L} \\
SWCF &= \frac{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A L \, C_{M\beta} \cdot \beta}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A \, C_{S\beta} \cdot \beta \cdot L} \\
SWCF &= \frac{C_{M\beta}}{C_{S\beta}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A small absolute value of $SWCF$ (e.g., $\vert SWCF \vert < 0.05$) means the vehicle is highly **crosswind-stable** — it produces little yaw for a given side force. A large absolute value means the vehicle is **crosswind-sensitive**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A car travels at $V = 30 \text{ m/s}$ (108 km/h) and encounters a crosswind of $V_c = 6 \text{ m/s}$. The car has frontal area $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, wheelbase $L = 2.6 \text{ m}$, and the following wind tunnel derivatives:

$C_{S\beta} = 1.8 \text{ /rad}, \quad C_{M\beta} = -0.18 \text{ /rad}$

Air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate:
(a) The sideslip angle $\beta$ in degrees.
(b) The side force $F_S$ on the vehicle.
(c) The yawing moment $M_Z$ about the CG.
(d) The aerodynamic center location $x_{CP}$ and state whether the vehicle is stable.
(e) The Side Wind Correction Factor $SWCF$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Sideslip angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \tan^{-1}(V_c / V) = \tan^{-1}(6/30) = \tan^{-1}(0.2) \\
\beta &= 11.31^\circ = 0.1974 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Side force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= \sqrt{V^2 + V_c^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{936} = 30.594 \text{ m/s} \\
F_S &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_r^2 A \, C_{S\beta} \cdot \beta \\
F_S &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (30.594)^2 \times 2.2 \times 1.8 \times 0.1974 \\
F_S &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 935.99 \times 2.2 \times 1.8 \times 0.1974 \\
F_S &= 457.30 \text{ N (lateral push on the car)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Yawing moment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_Z &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_r^2 A L \, C_{M\beta} \cdot \beta \\
M_Z &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 935.99 \times 2.2 \times 2.6 \times (-0.18) \times 0.1974 \\
M_Z &= -108.92 \text{ N·m (nose-left convention)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Aerodynamic center:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{CP} &= -L \cdot \frac{C_{M\beta}}{C_{S\beta}} = -2.6 \cdot \frac{(-0.18)}{1.8} \\
x_{CP} &= -2.6 \times (-0.10) = +0.26 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Stability Verdict:** Since $x_{CP} = +0.26 \text{ m}$ is **positive** (i.e., the center of pressure lies **behind** the CG by 26 cm), the vehicle is **aerodynamically stable**. It exhibits a mild weathervaning tendency.

**(e) Side Wind Correction Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SWCF &= \frac{C_{M\beta}}{C_{S\beta}} = \frac{-0.18}{1.8} = -0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

> $\vert SWCF \vert = 0.10$ is moderate; modern sedans typically achieve $\vert SWCF \vert < 0.05$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Side Wind Force Generation and Vehicle Response

This functional block diagram illustrates how a side wind propagates through the vehicle's aerodynamic system to produce the final driver-observed response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crosswind Vc blows perpendicular to vehicle] --> B[Vector Sum with vehicle speed V]
    B --> C[Resultant Relative Wind Vr at angle beta]
    C --> D[Flow separates around vehicle body]
    D --> E[Asymmetric pressure on left and right surfaces]
    E --> F[Side Force FS generated lateral to vehicle]
    E --> G[Yawing Moment MZ generated about CG]
    F --> H[Aerodynamic Center CP location]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Is CP behind CG?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Aerodynamically STABLE Weathervane effect]
    I -->|No| K[Aerodynamically UNSTABLE Divergent yaw]
    J --> L[Driver Counter-Steers to Maintain Path]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Steering Input delta steer]
    M --> N[Corrected Vehicle Trajectory]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
    style G fill:#ffe1e1
    style J fill:#e1ffe1
    style K fill:#fff4e1
```

---

The following block diagram represents the geometric relationship between the vehicle motion, crosswind, and resultant relative wind. (Adapted to block-level architecture because a precise physical drawing of the velocity triangle is best represented in vector form.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Vehicle Velocity V along x axis] --> SUM[Vector Addition]
    V2[Crosswind Vc along y axis] --> SUM
    SUM --> VR[Resultant Relative Wind Vr]
    SUM --> BETA[Sideslip Angle beta = arctan Vc over V]
    VR --> FS[Side Force FS perpendicular to V]
    VR --> MZ[Yawing Moment MZ about CG]
    style SUM fill:#fff4cc

```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Crosswind Stability Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Vehicle subjected to side wind] --> Q[Measure CS beta and CM beta in wind tunnel]
    Q --> R[Compute SWCF = CM beta divided by CS beta]
    R --> S{SWCF magnitude compared to 0.05}
    S -->|Less than 0.05| T[Class A Excellent Stability]
    S -->|Between 0.05 and 0.10| U[Class B Good Stability]
    S -->|Greater than 0.10| V[Class C Needs Improvement]
    T --> W[Typical of modern sedans and coupes]

    style V fill:#ffe1e1
```

    A2 --> A3[Driver Senses Lateral Pull]
    A3 --> A4[Steering Correction Applied]

    A5 --> A6[Stabilised Trajectory Maintained]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define the term "Side Wind Correction Factor (SWCF)" and explain its significance in evaluating the crosswind stability of road vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Side Wind Correction Factor (SWCF) is a dimensionless aerodynamic parameter used to quantify a vehicle's crosswind sensitivity. It is defined as the ratio of the yawing moment coefficient derivative to the side force coefficient derivative with respect to the sideslip angle $\beta$:
>
> $$SWCF = \frac{C_{M\beta}}{C_{S\beta}}$$
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. It is **independent of wind speed** — purely a property of vehicle geometry.
> 2. A **small absolute value** ($\vert SWCF \vert < 0.05$) indicates a **stable** vehicle (low yaw per unit side force).
> 3. A **negative SWCF** indicates a **weathervaning tendency** (the vehicle naturally aligns with the wind), which is desirable for stability.
> 4. A **positive SWCF** indicates an **unstable** divergent response, requiring design correction.
> 5. It is used by OEMs to compare candidate vehicle designs without requiring full-scale wind tunnel runs at every configuration.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Explain the concept of "aerodynamic center" (or center of pressure) of a vehicle subjected to a crosswind. How does its location relative to the CG determine stability?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **aerodynamic center (AC)** or **center of pressure (CP)** is the point on the vehicle's longitudinal axis where the resultant aerodynamic side force is considered to act, such that the moment about the CG equals the actual yawing moment produced by the distributed pressure field.
>
> Mathematically:
>
> $$x_{CP} = -L \cdot \frac{C_{M\beta}}{C_{S\beta}}$$
>
> **Stability Criterion:**
> - If the CP lies **behind** the CG (i.e., $x_{CP} > 0$), any small yawing disturbance produces a **restoring moment** → vehicle is **aerodynamically stable** (weathervanes).
> - If the CP lies **ahead of** the CG ($x_{CP} < 0$), the side force amplifies the disturbance → vehicle is **aerodynamically unstable**.
>
> Designers push the CP rearward by using steeply raked rear glass, boat-tail rear ends, and rear spoilers — common in modern sedans and sports cars.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A passenger car of frontal area $A = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$ and wheelbase $L = 2.5 \text{ m}$ travels at a speed of $V = 25 \text{ m/s}$. It is suddenly exposed to a steady crosswind of magnitude $V_c = 5 \text{ m/s}$. The aerodynamic derivatives obtained from wind tunnel testing are $C_{S\beta} = 2.0 \text{ /rad}$ and $C_{M\beta} = -0.15 \text{ /rad}$. Take $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.**

#### **Part (a) (7 Marks):**
**Calculate the sideslip angle $\beta$, the resultant relative wind speed $V_r$, the side force $F_S$, and the yawing moment $M_Z$ about the vehicle's CG.**

#### **Part (b) (7 Marks):**
**Determine the location of the aerodynamic center (CP) relative to the CG and state whether the vehicle is aerodynamically stable. Also compute the Side Wind Correction Factor (SWCF) and the additional steering angle required to counteract the yawing moment, given the steering stiffness $K_s = 25000 \text{ N·m/rad}$.**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Sideslip angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{V_c}{V}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{25}\right) \\
\beta &= \tan^{-1}(0.2) = 11.31^\circ = 0.1974 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Resultant relative wind speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= \sqrt{V^2 + V_c^2} = \sqrt{25^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{650} \\
V_r &= 25.495 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Side force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_S &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_r^2 A \, C_{S\beta} \cdot \beta \\
F_S &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (25.495)^2 \times 2.0 \times 2.0 \times 0.1974 \\
F_S &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 650.00 \times 2.0 \times 2.0 \times 0.1974 \\
F_S &= 314.16 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Yawing moment about CG:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_Z &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_r^2 A L \, C_{M\beta} \cdot \beta \\
M_Z &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 650.00 \times 2.0 \times 2.5 \times (-0.15) \times 0.1974 \\
M_Z &= -58.91 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The negative sign means the moment is in the **nose-right** direction (or, in the sign convention used, "nose left" if the convention has $C_{M\beta} < 0$ = nose right). Most texts use the convention: **$M_Z < 0$ implies a nose-right moment** when the crosswind comes from the right (driver's side). For the purpose of KTU valuation, stating the sign clearly with a physical interpretation is sufficient.

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Aerodynamic center location:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{CP} &= -L \cdot \frac{C_{M\beta}}{C_{S\beta}} = -2.5 \times \frac{(-0.15)}{2.0} \\
x_{CP} &= -2.5 \times (-0.075) = +0.1875 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Stability:** $x_{CP} = +0.1875 \text{ m}$ is **positive**, meaning the center of pressure lies **0.1875 m behind the CG**. The vehicle is therefore **aerodynamically stable** (weathervane tendency).

**Step 2 — Side Wind Correction Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SWCF &= \frac{C_{M\beta}}{C_{S\beta}} = \frac{-0.15}{2.0} = -0.075
\end{aligned}
$$

> $\vert SWCF \vert = 0.075$ is **moderate**. The vehicle is reasonably stable but could be improved with rear-end aerodynamic refinements.

**Step 3 — Required steering correction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{steer} &= \frac{M_Z}{K_s} = \frac{-58.91}{25000} \\
\delta_{steer} &= -0.00236 \text{ rad} = -0.135^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

> The negative sign means the driver must apply a **steering correction of 0.135° in the nose-left direction** to counter the wind's nose-right moment.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A sports car moving at $V = 40 \text{ m/s}$ encounters a crosswind of $V_c = 8 \text{ m/s}$. The vehicle has $A = 1.8 \text{ m}^2$, $L = 2.4 \text{ m}$, $C_{S\beta} = 1.6 \text{ /rad}$, $C_{M\beta} = -0.08 \text{ /rad}$. Air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.**

#### **Part (a) (7 Marks):**
**Compute $\beta$, $F_S$, $M_Z$, and the ratio $M_Z / F_S$.**

#### **Part (b) (7 Marks):**
**Compare the crosswind stability of this sports car with a typical sedan (assume sedan has $C_{M\beta} = -0.18$, $C_{S\beta} = 1.8$, $L = 2.6$ m). Use SWCF and $x_{CP}$ metrics. Comment on which is more aerodynamically stable and why.**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Sideslip angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \tan^{-1}(8/40) = \tan^{-1}(0.2) = 11.31^\circ = 0.1974 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Resultant relative wind:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= \sqrt{40^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{1664} = 40.79 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Side force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_S &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (40.79)^2 \times 1.8 \times 1.6 \times 0.1974 \\
F_S &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1663.82 \times 1.8 \times 1.6 \times 0.1974 \\
F_S &= 579.65 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Yawing moment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_Z &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1663.82 \times 1.8 \times 2.4 \times (-0.08) \times 0.1974 \\
M_Z &= -69.31 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Ratio $M_Z / F_S$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{M_Z}{F_S} &= \frac{-69.31}{579.65} = -0.1196 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Sports car SWCF and $x_{CP}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SWCF_{sport} &= \frac{-0.08}{1.6} = -0.050 \\
x_{CP, sport} &= -2.4 \times (-0.050) = +0.120 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sedan SWCF and $x_{CP}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SWCF_{sedan} &= \frac{-0.18}{1.8} = -0.100 \\
x_{CP, sedan} &= -2.6 \times (-0.100) = +0.260 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Stability Comparison Table:**

| Metric | Sports Car | Sedan |
|---|---|---|
| $\vert SWCF \vert$ | 0.050 | 0.100 |
| $x_{CP}$ (m) | +0.120 | +0.260 |

> **Conclusion:** The **sedan is more aerodynamically stable** in crosswind because it has a larger rearward $x_{CP}$ (+0.260 m vs +0.120 m) and a larger $\vert SWCF \vert$ in the **negative** sense, indicating a stronger weathervane restoring moment. The sports car, while still stable, has its center of pressure closer to the CG, making it slightly more **agile** in crosswind (less self-correcting) — which is often **deliberate** for a performance vehicle to allow sharper driver inputs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Many students lose 2–3 marks by confusing the sign of $C_{M\beta}$ and $x_{CP}$. Always state the convention explicitly: "Positive $x_{CP}$ means CP behind CG."
> 2. **Forgetting the $V_r$ computation:** A common mistake is to use $V$ instead of $V_r$ in the side-force and moment formulas. For large $V_c/V$ ratios (>0.2), this introduces a 4–5% error. Always compute $V_r = \sqrt{V^2 + V_c^2}$.
> 3. **Confusing $C_{S\beta}$ with $C_S$:** The aerodynamic derivative $C_{S\beta}$ is the **slope** $dC_S/d\beta$, NOT the value of $C_S$ itself. Multiply it by $\beta$ to get the actual coefficient.
> 4. **Radians vs Degrees:** $\beta$ must be in **radians** when used in the formulas. Convert degrees to radians before substitution.
> 5. **SWCF unit misinterpretation:** Some students write $SWCF$ with units. It is a **dimensionless** ratio.
> 6. **Aerodynamic center formula sign:** A missing negative sign in $x_{CP} = -L \cdot (C_{M\beta}/C_{S\beta})$ will flip the stability conclusion — losing all 7 marks in part (b).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Side Wind Problems":**

- ✅ **Resultant wind speed:** $V_r = \sqrt{V^2 + V_c^2}$; **sideslip angle:** $\beta = \tan^{-1}(V_c/V)$ — always in **radians** for formula use.
- ✅ **Side force:** $F_S = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_r^2 A C_{S\beta} \beta$ — acts **perpendicular** to vehicle's longitudinal axis.
- ✅ **Yawing moment:** $M_Z = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_r^2 A L C_{M\beta} \beta$ — acts about the **vertical axis through CG**.
- ✅ **Aerodynamic center:** $x_{CP} = -L \cdot (C_{M\beta} / C_{S\beta})$ — **positive value = CP behind CG = STABLE**.
- ✅ **SWCF:** $C_{M\beta} / C_{S\beta}$ — **negative value = weathervane stable**; magnitude measures sensitivity.
- ✅ **Steering correction:** $\delta_{steer} = M_Z / K_s$ — tells the driver/Esc system how much to counter-steer.
- ✅ **Stability classification:** $\vert SWCF \vert < 0.05$ → Class A (excellent); 0.05–0.10 → Class B (good); > 0.10 → Class C (improve).
- ✅ **Design improvements** to push CP rearward: **raked rear glass**, **boat-tail rear**, **rear spoiler**, **diffuser**, **low roofline at rear**.
- ✅ **Sign convention is critical:** Always state whether $M_Z < 0$ means nose-left or nose-right — examiners expect this explicitly.
- ✅ **Real-world relevance:** ECE R79 (lane deviation < 0.6 m at 80 km/h), ESC/ESP systems, bus/truck safety, F1 aero balance, motorcycle stability.
- ✅ **Approximation:** For $V_c/V < 0.1$, can use $V_r \approx V$, but **never skip the angle** $\beta$ — it is the driver of all side wind effects.
- ✅ **Linearisation validity:** The linearised formulas ($C_S = C_{S\beta} \cdot \beta$) hold only for **small $\beta$** (< 15°). For larger angles, wind tunnel data or CFD must be used.
- ✅ **Crosswind sensitivity** is a **dimensional property** (depends on $L$, $A$, $C_D$); SWCF is the **pure-shape property** preferred for vehicle-to-vehicle comparison.
- ✅ **Aerodynamic center ≠ Center of Gravity:** The CP is where the resultant side force *effectively* acts; the CG is where the mass is concentrated. Stability requires CP **behind** CG.
- ✅ **The weathervane analogy:** A car whose CP is behind its CG behaves like a flag pole — it tries to align with the wind. This is the **fundamental stability mechanism** of all modern road vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Dynamics Under Side Winds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT753 - AUTOMOTIVE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Dynamics Under Side Winds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Dynamics Under Side Winds** is the branch of automotive aerodynamics that studies the response of a moving vehicle to a **crosswind** (a wind component acting perpendicular, or at an oblique angle, to the vehicle's longitudinal axis of motion). It analyzes the resulting **aerodynamic side force (S)**, **yawing moment (N)** about the vertical axis, and **overturning moment (O)** about the longitudinal axis, and quantifies the **crosswind sensitivity** of the vehicle using dimensionless coefficients and rigid-body dynamic equations.

The governing aerodynamic coefficients are conventionally defined as:

$$C_S \;=\; \dfrac{S}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{rel}^{\,2}\,A} \qquad\qquad C_N \;=\; \dfrac{N}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{rel}^{\,2}\,A\,\ell} \qquad\qquad C_O \;=\; \dfrac{O}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{rel}^{\,2}\,A\,h}$$

where:
- $S$ = side (cross) force [N], $N$ = yawing moment [N·m], $O$ = overturning moment [N·m]
- $\rho$ = air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³** (ISA sea-level standard)
- $V_{rel}$ = relative wind speed [m/s]
- $A$ = frontal projected area [m²]
- $\ell$ = wheelbase [m], $h$ = height of aerodynamic centre of pressure above ground [m]

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine pushing a tall, narrow refrigerator trolley down a corridor while someone stands at the side and shoves it sideways.** The trolley doesn't just slide sideways — it tries to **pivot (yaw)** away from you and also **tip over (roll)** because the push is applied high up. A car in a crosswind behaves the *exact same way*: the wind acts like that sideways shove, applied at the "aerodynamic centre of pressure" (typically near the rear window or roof for a saloon). The driver's main concern is **yaw disturbance** (the car wanders into the adjacent lane) and **roll instability** (especially for tall vans and trucks).

### 1.3 Why Side-Wind Behaviour Matters in KTU Context

| Real-world trigger | Engineering consequence |
|---|---|
| Truck overtaking a car on a highway | Sudden gust → yaw spike → lane deviation |
| Bridge / flyover with cross-flow | Steady side force → driver fatigue (constant correction) |
| Tall vans (Sprinter, Force Traveller) in monsoon | High overturning moment → roll-over risk |
| High-speed trains & F1 cars exiting tunnels | Transient gust → loss of downforce balance |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic** — "**SNO**" = **S**ide force → causes **N**ew lane drift (yaw) → causes **O**verturning (roll). This is the order in which examiners like to test the topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 📐 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Side-Wind Forces

When a vehicle of forward velocity $V$ travels through still air, it experiences only drag along its longitudinal axis. When the air mass itself moves with a velocity $W$ at an angle $\psi$ to the vehicle's centreline (the **yaw angle of the relative wind**), the vehicle sees a **relative wind vector**:

$$V_{rel} \;=\; \sqrt{V^{2} \;+\; W^{2} \;+\; 2\,V\,W\cos\psi}$$

For a **pure crosswind** ($\psi = 90°$), this simplifies to:

$$V_{rel} \;=\; \sqrt{V^{2} \;+\; W^{2}}$$

The yaw angle of the relative wind at the vehicle is:

$$\beta_{aero} \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{V}\right)$$

This effective aerodynamic sideslip angle $\beta_{aero}$ is what the vehicle "feels," and it is what causes the side force and yawing moment.

### 2.2 Three Degrees-of-Freedom (3-DOF) Rigid-Body Model

A passenger car under a crosswind is conventionally modelled as a rigid body with three relevant motions:
1. **Sideslip** (lateral translation, $v$)
2. **Yaw rotation** (about vertical axis, $r$)
3. **Roll rotation** (about longitudinal axis, $\phi$)

The **bicycle model** (2-DOF sideslip + yaw) is the workhorse used in KTU problems:

$$m\,V\,(\dot{\beta} + r) \;=\; F_{y,f} + F_{y,r} + S \tag{Lateral}$$

$$I_z\,\dot{r} \;=\; a\,F_{y,f} - b\,F_{y,r} + N \tag{Yaw}$$

$$I_x\,\ddot{\phi} + c_\phi\,\dot{\phi} + k_\phi\,\phi \;=\; m\,g\,h_{cg}\,\phi + O + F_{z,wind}\cdot h_{cp} \tag{Roll}$$

where:
- $m$ = vehicle mass [kg], $I_z$ = yaw mass moment of inertia [kg·m²], $I_x$ = roll mass moment of inertia [kg·m²]
- $a, b$ = distances of front/rear axle from c.g. [m]
- $k_\phi, c_\phi$ = total roll stiffness and damping [N·m/rad, N·m·s/rad]
- $h_{cg}$ = height of centre of gravity [m], $h_{cp}$ = height of centre of pressure [m]
- $\beta$ = vehicle sideslip angle at c.g.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the canonical reference for solving any KTU Part-A or Part-B question on this topic.** Bookmark it. Memorise the units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Side Force Coefficient | $C_S = \dfrac{S}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^{2} A}$ | dimensionless | Positive = away from wind |
| 2 | Yawing Moment Coefficient | $C_N = \dfrac{N}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^{2} A \ell}$ | dimensionless | Positive = nose-right |
| 3 | Overturning Moment Coefficient | $C_O = \dfrac{O}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^{2} A h}$ | dimensionless | Positive = roll away from wind |
| 4 | Relative Wind Speed | $V_{rel} = \sqrt{V^{2} + W^{2} + 2VW\cos\psi}$ | m/s | $\psi = 90°$ for pure crosswind |
| 5 | Aerodynamic Sideslip Angle | $\beta_{aero} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{V}\right)$ | rad or deg | Small-angle: $\beta \approx W/V$ |
| 6 | Steady-state Yaw Rate | $r_{ss} = \dfrac{N_{w}\,W\,V - Y_{w}\,W\,a\,V}{I_z\,V\,k_r + \dots}$ (see §3.3) | rad/s | Crosswind-induced yaw velocity |
| 7 | Steady-state Sideslip | $\beta_{ss} = -\dfrac{Y_{w}}{Y_\beta}\,W_{eff}$ | rad | Crosswind-induced slip angle |
| 8 | Crosswind Sensitivity Index | $CSI = \dfrac{\Delta a_{lat}}{\sigma_{W}}$ | m/(m/s) | Lateral acceleration per unit gust |
| 9 | Static Margin (aerodynamic) | $SM = -\dfrac{\partial C_N/\partial \beta}{C_S/\beta}\cdot\dfrac{\ell}{V}$ | dimensionless | Stability in yaw (negative = stable) |
| 10 | Critical Gust Speed (rollover) | $W_{crit} = \dfrac{m\,g\,(t/2)}{C_O\,\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A h}$ | m/s | Van/truck rollover threshold |

> Where $Y_\beta, Y_r, N_\beta, N_r, Y_w, N_w$ are the **stability derivatives** (aerodynamic + tyre) used in the linearised equations of motion.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Active Safety:** Modern ESC (Electronic Stability Control) and AYC (Active Yaw Control) systems use exactly the 2-DOF model above in their control law — the same equations you derive here.
- **Passenger Comfort:** Crosswind-induced lateral acceleration $a_y = V \cdot r_{ss}$ must remain below ~**0.05 g** for comfortable highway driving (ISO 2631).
- **Commercial Vehicle Regulation:** ECE R29 and EU GSR2 set minimum crosswind stability for heavy trucks and vans.
- **Motorsport:** F1 teams exploit crosswind gusts at circuits like Silverstone to either *gain* downforce (tailwind component) or suffer massive yaw spikes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 🔬 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Wind Vector to Aerodynamic Sideslip

**Given:** A vehicle of forward speed $V$ travelling in still air. A wind of speed $W$ blows at an angle $\psi$ to the vehicle's longitudinal axis.

**Step 1 — Write the wind velocity vectors in the ground (inertial) frame.**

Let the vehicle's longitudinal axis be the $x$-axis and the lateral axis be the $y$-axis. The wind velocity in the inertial frame is:

$$V_{wind,x} = W\cos\psi \qquad\qquad V_{wind,y} = W\sin\psi$$

**Step 2 — Compute the relative wind seen by the vehicle (i.e., subtract vehicle velocity from the air velocity in inertial frame).**

The relative wind vector in vehicle coordinates is:

$$\vec{V}_{rel} \;=\; \vec{V}_{air} \;-\; \vec{V}_{vehicle} \;=\; \bigl(W\cos\psi - V\bigr)\,\hat{i} \;+\; \bigl(W\sin\psi\bigr)\,\hat{j}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude.**

$$V_{rel} \;=\; \sqrt{(W\cos\psi - V)^{2} \;+\; (W\sin\psi)^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the squared terms.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{rel}^{\,2} &\;=\; W^{2}\cos^{2}\psi - 2VW\cos\psi + V^{2} + W^{2}\sin^{2}\psi \\
&\;=\; W^{2}(\cos^{2}\psi + \sin^{2}\psi) + V^{2} - 2VW\cos\psi \\
&\;=\; V^{2} + W^{2} - 2VW\cos\psi
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final relative wind speed.**

$$\boxed{\,V_{rel} \;=\; \sqrt{V^{2} + W^{2} - 2VW\cos\psi}\,}$$

> **Note on sign convention:** KTU convention uses **headwind positive**, so the minus sign is correct. Some texts write $+2VW\cos\psi$ for a *tailwind* convention. Be careful!

**Step 6 — Special case: pure crosswind ($\psi = 90°$).**

Since $\cos(90°) = 0$:

$$V_{rel} \;=\; \sqrt{V^{2} + W^{2}}$$

**Step 7 — The aerodynamic sideslip angle $\beta_{aero}$.**

$$\beta_{aero} \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{rel,y}}{V_{rel,x}}\right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{W\sin\psi}{V - W\cos\psi}\right)$$

For small $W/V$ (gust much smaller than vehicle speed), linearise using $\tan^{-1}(x) \approx x$ and $\sin\psi \approx 1$, $\cos\psi \approx 0$:

$$\boxed{\,\beta_{aero} \;\approx\; \dfrac{W}{V}\,} \qquad \text{(small-angle crosswind assumption)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linearised Yaw & Sideslip Equations (Bicycle Model)

**Given:** The non-linear vehicle equations of motion in §2.2. Assume small perturbations around straight-line driving ($\beta \approx 0$, $r \approx 0$) so that **all trigonometric non-linearities vanish** and tyre cornering forces are linear in slip: $F_{y,i} = C_{\alpha,i}\,\alpha_i$.

**Step 1 — Linearised tyre slip angles.**

For a bicycle model, the front and rear slip angles are:

$$\alpha_f \;\approx\; \delta - \beta - \dfrac{a\,r}{V} \qquad\qquad \alpha_r \;\approx\; -\beta + \dfrac{b\,r}{V}$$

where $\delta$ is the steering angle (set to 0 for the *uncontrolled* crosswind problem).

**Step 2 — Substitute into the lateral equation.**

$$mV(\dot{\beta} + r) \;=\; C_{\alpha f}\!\left(-\beta - \dfrac{a\,r}{V}\right) + C_{\alpha r}\!\left(-\beta + \dfrac{b\,r}{V}\right) + S$$

**Step 3 — Collect $\beta$ and $r$ terms.**

$$\begin{aligned}
mV\dot{\beta} \;=\; -mV\,r &- (C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r})\,\beta \\
&- \dfrac{a\,C_{\alpha f} - b\,C_{\alpha r}}{V}\,r \;+\; S
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Define the stability derivatives.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_\beta &\;=\; -(C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}) \\
Y_r &\;=\; -\dfrac{a\,C_{\alpha f} - b\,C_{\alpha r}}{V} \\
Y_w &\;=\; \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial W} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A\,\dfrac{\partial C_S}{\partial \beta}\,V \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A\,C_{S\beta}\,V
\end{aligned}$$

(where we used $\beta_{aero} \approx W/V$ and the chain rule.)

So the **linearised lateral equation** becomes:

$$\boxed{\,mV\dot{\beta} \;=\; Y_\beta\,\beta + Y_r\,r + Y_w\,W\,} \tag{Lat-lin}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the same linearisation to the yaw equation.**

$$I_z\,\dot{r} \;=\; a\,C_{\alpha f}\!\left(-\beta - \dfrac{a\,r}{V}\right) - b\,C_{\alpha r}\!\left(-\beta + \dfrac{b\,r}{V}\right) + N$$

**Step 6 — Collect terms.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_z\,\dot{r} &\;=\; -(a\,C_{\alpha f} - b\,C_{\alpha r})\,\beta \;-\; \dfrac{a^{2}C_{\alpha f} + b^{2}C_{\alpha r}}{V}\,r \;+\; N
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Define yaw stability derivatives.**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_\beta &\;=\; -(a\,C_{\alpha f} - b\,C_{\alpha r}) \\
N_r &\;=\; -\dfrac{a^{2}C_{\alpha f} + b^{2}C_{\alpha r}}{V} \\
N_w &\;=\; \dfrac{\partial N}{\partial W} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A\,\ell\,C_{N\beta}\,V
\end{aligned}$$

So the **linearised yaw equation** becomes:

$$\boxed{\,I_z\,\dot{r} \;=\; N_\beta\,\beta + N_r\,r + N_w\,W\,} \tag{Yaw-lin}$$

**Step 8 — Matrix form.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} mV & 0 \\ 0 & I_z \end{bmatrix}\! \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\beta} \\ \dot{r} \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} Y_\beta & Y_r \\ N_\beta & N_r \end{bmatrix}\! \begin{bmatrix} \beta \\ r \end{bmatrix} \;+\; \begin{bmatrix} Y_w \\ N_w \end{bmatrix} W \tag{State Eq.}$$

This is the **state-space form** $\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}W$ that examiners love to ask you to write down.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steady-State Response to a Constant Crosswind

**Given:** A *constant* crosswind $W$ acting on the vehicle for long enough that all transient ($\dot{\beta} = 0$, $\dot{r} = 0$) behaviour has died out. Find the steady-state sideslip $\beta_{ss}$ and yaw rate $r_{ss}$.

**Step 1 — Set time derivatives to zero in the linearised equations.**

$$0 \;=\; Y_\beta\,\beta_{ss} + Y_r\,r_{ss} + Y_w\,W$$

$$0 \;=\; N_\beta\,\beta_{ss} + N_r\,r_{ss} + N_w\,W$$

**Step 2 — Solve the lateral equation for $r_{ss}$.**

$$r_{ss} \;=\; -\dfrac{Y_\beta\,\beta_{ss} + Y_w\,W}{Y_r}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the yaw equation.**

$$0 \;=\; N_\beta\,\beta_{ss} + N_r\!\left(-\dfrac{Y_\beta\,\beta_{ss} + Y_w\,W}{Y_r}\right) + N_w\,W$$

**Step 4 — Multiply through by $Y_r$.**

$$0 \;=\; N_\beta\,Y_r\,\beta_{ss} - N_r\,Y_\beta\,\beta_{ss} - N_r\,Y_w\,W + N_w\,Y_r\,W$$

**Step 5 — Factor $\beta_{ss}$.**

$$\beta_{ss}\,(N_\beta\,Y_r - N_r\,Y_\beta) \;=\; (N_r\,Y_w - N_w\,Y_r)\,W$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\beta_{ss}$.**

$$\boxed{\,\beta_{ss} \;=\; \dfrac{(N_r\,Y_w - N_w\,Y_r)}{(N_\beta\,Y_r - N_r\,Y_\beta)}\,W\,}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back to find $r_{ss}$.**

$$r_{ss} \;=\; -\dfrac{1}{Y_r}\!\left[\,Y_\beta\cdot\dfrac{(N_r\,Y_w - N_w\,Y_r)}{(N_\beta\,Y_r - N_r\,Y_\beta)}\,W + Y_w\,W\,\right]$$

After algebraic simplification (multiply by $N_\beta\,Y_r - N_r\,Y_\beta$ over $N_\beta\,Y_r - N_r\,Y_\beta$ and combine):

$$\boxed{\,r_{ss} \;=\; \dfrac{(Y_\beta\,N_w - N_\beta\,Y_w)}{(N_\beta\,Y_r - N_r\,Y_\beta)}\,W\,}$$

**Step 8 — Physical interpretation.**

- A **positive** $r_{ss}$ means the vehicle yaws *into* the wind (drifts sideways towards it).
- The denominator $(N_\beta Y_r - N_r Y_\beta)$ is the determinant of the A-matrix; if it is **positive**, the system is *dynamically stable* in yaw.
- The **numerator** $Y_\beta N_w - N_\beta Y_w$ is called the **crosswind yaw-sensitivity coefficient**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Most saloons have $C_{S\beta} > 0$ (side force builds up with yaw) and $C_{N\beta} < 0$ (nose-weathers-vane effect). The combination yields a stable response — i.e., the car turns *into* the wind and the driver only needs to apply a small steering correction.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Crosswind Speed for Van Rollover

**Given:** A van of mass $m$, track width $t$, frontal area $A$, and centre-of-pressure height $h$. The overturning moment coefficient is $C_O$.

**Step 1 — Write the overturning moment produced by the wind.**

$$O \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^{2}\,A\,h\,C_O$$

For pure crosswind at constant vehicle speed, $V_{rel} \approx V$ (assuming $W \ll V$), so:

$$O \;\approx\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}\,A\,h\,C_O$$

**Step 2 — Write the restoring moment due to gravity acting at half-track.**

The weight acts at the c.g., which is at the centre between the two wheels. The restoring (righting) moment arm is $t/2$ from the centre line. The restoring moment about the tipping edge is:

$$M_{restore} \;=\; m\,g\,\dfrac{t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the rollover condition $O = M_{restore}$.**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}\,A\,h\,C_O \;=\; m\,g\,\dfrac{t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V$.**

$$V_{crit}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{m\,g\,t}{\rho\,A\,h\,C_O}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{crit} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{m\,g\,t}{\rho\,A\,h\,C_O}}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example for a typical van.**

Take $m = 2500$ kg, $t = 1.7$ m, $A = 4.5$ m², $h = 1.3$ m, $C_O = 0.15$:

$$V_{crit} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.7}{1.225 \cdot 4.5 \cdot 1.3 \cdot 0.15}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{41{,}692.5}{1074.6}} \;=\; \sqrt{38.80} \;\approx\; \boxed{6.23 \text{ m/s (only 22.4 km/h!)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is exactly why tall vans (Tempo Traveller, Sprinter) are notoriously dangerous in monsoon crosswinds in Kerala.** A gust of only 6 m/s (22 km/h) at vehicle standstill is enough to flip them. This is a frequently-tested **Part B scenario**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 🗺️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of a Vehicle Under Crosswind (Schematic Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VEH[Vehicle Coordinate System - Side View]
        CP[Aerodynamic Centre of Pressure - height h_cp above ground]
        CG[Centre of Gravity CG - height h_cg]
        VEC[(Velocity V forward along +x axis)]
    end

    subgraph AERO[Aerodynamic Loads at Centre of Pressure]
        SF[Side Force S - acts along +y axis away from wind]
        YM[Yawing Moment N - about z axis at ground]
        OM[Overturning Moment O - about x axis]
        DR[Drag D - along -x axis opposes motion]
    end

    subgraph TYRE[Ground Reaction at Tyre Contact Patches]
        FYf[Front Lateral Tyre Force F_yf]
        FYr[Rear Lateral Tyre Force F_yr]
        FZf[Front Vertical Load F_zf]
        FZr[Rear Vertical Load F_zr]
    end

    subgraph WIND[Crosswind Vector]
        WV[Wind Velocity W at angle psi to vehicle x axis]
        VREL[Relative Wind V_rel = sqrt V squared + W squared + 2 V W cos psi]
    end

    WV --> VREL
    VREL --> SF
    VREL --> YM
    VREL --> OM
    VREL --> DR
    SF --> CP
    YM --> CP
    OM --> CP
    CP --> CG
    CG --> FYf
    CG --> FYr
    CG --> FZf
    CG --> FZr
```

### 4.2 State-Space Block Diagram of Crosswind Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Inputs]
        WINDIN[Crosswind Gust W of t]
        STEER[Steering Input delta of t - usually zero for uncontrolled case]
    end

    subgraph LATDYN[Lateral Dynamics Block]
        LATINT[Integrator - 1 over mV]
        BETASS[Sideslip Angle beta]
    end

    subgraph YAWDYN[Yaw Dynamics Block]
        YAWINT[Integrator - 1 over I_z]
        YAWRATE[Yaw Rate r]
    end

    subgraph AERO[Aerodynamic Force and Moment Block]
        SWIND[Side Force from Wind S = 0.5 rho V squared A C_S]
        NWIND[Yawing Moment from Wind N = 0.5 rho V squared A l C_N]
    end

    subgraph TYREBLK[Tyre Cornering Force Block]
        FYALPHA[Linear Tyre Model F_y = C_alpha times slip angle]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Vehicle Response Outputs]
        AYAT[Lateral Acceleration a_y = V times r plus v_dot]
        PSIY[Yaw Angle psi_y = integral of r]
    end

    WINDIN --> SWIND
    WINDIN --> NWIND
    STEER --> FYALPHA
    SWIND --> LATINT
    NWIND --> YAWINT
    LATINT --> BETASS
    YAWINT --> YAWRATE
    BETASS --> FYALPHA
    YAWRATE --> FYALPHA
    FYALPHA --> LATINT
    FYALPHA --> YAWINT
    YAWRATE --> AYAT
    BETASS --> AYAT
    YAWRATE --> PSIY
```

### 4.3 Methodology Block Diagram — How to Reduce Crosswind Sensitivity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DESIGN[Design-Time Strategies]
        A1[Lower Centre of Pressure - flatter rear end]
        A2[Rear-biased Side Force - place pressure on rear axle]
        A3[Add Spoilers and Side Skirts]
        A4[Active Aerodynamics - variable flaps]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Control-Time Strategies]
        B1[ESC - Electronic Stability Control]
        B2[Active Rear Steering]
        B3[Active Front Steering Overlay]
        B4[Suspension Crosswind Compensation]
    end

    subgraph TYRE[Tyre and Chassis Strategies]
        C1[Stiffer Anti-Roll Bars]
        C2[Wider Track Width t]
        C3[Lower h_cp]
        C4[Heavier Unsprung Damping]
    end

    GOAL((Target - reduce beta_ss and r_ss to acceptable ISO 2631 levels))

    DESIGN --> GOAL
    CTRL --> GOAL
    TYRE --> GOAL
```

> [!NOTE]
> These **Mermaid architecture diagrams** model the *functional interactions* between aerodynamic loads, tyre forces, and vehicle body motions — the same way an automotive R&D engineer would build the model in MATLAB/Simulink before testing in a wind tunnel.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 🎓 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
Define the following terms as applied to vehicle aerodynamics:
(a) Side force coefficient $C_S$
(b) Yawing moment coefficient $C_N$
(c) Aerodynamic sideslip angle $\beta_{aero}$

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**

**(a)** Side force coefficient is defined as the ratio of the lateral aerodynamic side force $S$ to the product of dynamic pressure $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^{2}$ and the frontal projected area $A$:

$$C_S \;=\; \dfrac{S}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^{2} A} \qquad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**(b)** Yawing moment coefficient is the ratio of the aerodynamic yawing moment $N$ about the vertical axis to the product of dynamic pressure, frontal area and the wheelbase $\ell$:

$$C_N \;=\; \dfrac{N}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^{2} A \ell} \qquad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**(c)** Aerodynamic sideslip angle is the angle between the relative wind vector and the vehicle's longitudinal axis, given by:

$$\beta_{aero} \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{V}\right)$$

For small $W/V$: $\beta_{aero} \approx W/V$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question Paper]**
State **any three** design strategies to reduce the crosswind sensitivity of a passenger car. Justify each with one line.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Lower the centre of pressure $h_{cp}$** — A low $h_{cp}$ reduces the lever arm of the side force, thereby reducing both yawing moment $N$ and overturning moment $O$.
2. **Rearward bias of $C_{S\beta}$ derivative** — Designing the body so that side force is generated mainly at the rear axle produces a nose-into-wind (weather-vane) restoring moment, increasing yaw stability.
3. **Use of side spoilers / air dams** — These control the flow separation on the leeward side, reducing $C_S$ magnitude and shifting the centre of pressure rearward.
4. *(Alternative — accepted)* Wider track and stiffer anti-roll bars reduce roll moment, while active rear steering can cancel crosswind-induced yaw in real-time.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For Part-B questions, students commonly lose marks by:
> - Forgetting to **state assumptions** (small angle, constant $V$, linear tyres).
> - Mixing up **headwind vs tailwind sign convention** in $V_{rel}$ formula.
> - Not **drawing the FBD** before writing equations (–2 marks guaranteed loss).
> - Skipping units in the final numerical answer (–1 mark).
> - Writing the wrong denominator in $\beta_{ss}$ formula — the determinant of the A-matrix, **not** just $Y_\beta$.
>
> **Always:** draw FBD → write equations → substitute numbers → box the final answer with units.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

A saloon car of mass $m = 1500$ kg, yaw moment of inertia $I_z = 2500$ kg·m², wheelbase $\ell = a + b = 2.6$ m with $a = 1.2$ m, $b = 1.4$ m, is travelling at $V = 25$ m/s (90 km/h) on a highway. A steady crosswind of $W = 10$ m/s blows perpendicular to the vehicle. The following aerodynamic stability derivatives have been measured in a wind tunnel:

| Derivative | Value |
|---|---|
| $Y_\beta$ | $-80{,}000$ N/rad |
| $Y_r$ | $-1500$ N·s/rad |
| $N_\beta$ | $-12{,}000$ N·m/rad |
| $N_r$ | $-3500$ N·m·s/rad |
| $Y_w$ | $1200$ N·s/m |
| $N_w$ | $850$ N·m·s/m |

**(a)** Develop the linearised **state-space equations** for the sideslip $\beta$ and yaw rate $r$ in the presence of the crosswind $W$. Write the equations in matrix form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **steady-state** sideslip angle $\beta_{ss}$ and yaw rate $r_{ss}$ induced by the crosswind. Also calculate the **lateral acceleration** $a_y = V \cdot r_{ss}$ experienced by the driver. Comment on whether this is acceptable per ISO 2631. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — State-space derivation [7 marks]**

Assumptions: small sideslip and yaw rate; linear tyres; constant forward speed; pure crosswind.

The lateral and yaw linearised equations of motion are:

$$mV\dot{\beta} \;=\; Y_\beta\,\beta + Y_r\,r + Y_w\,W$$

$$I_z\,\dot{r} \;=\; N_\beta\,\beta + N_r\,r + N_w\,W$$
