<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hatchback Drag — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Hatchback Drag):**
> **Hatchback drag** is the aerodynamic resistance experienced by a hatchback-style passenger car (defined by its rear-hinged tailgate and abrupt vertical rear end without a protruding boot/trunk deck). This drag is dominated by **flow separation at the rear roof edge**, which generates a **low-pressure recirculation wake region** behind the vehicle, producing a net rearward suction force. Hatchbacks are particularly susceptible to high drag because the **sloping rear window** meets the **near-vertical tailgate** at a sharp angle, forcing the boundary layer to detach prematurely and creating a large, turbulent wake.

Mathematically, the drag force on a hatchback is governed by the standard aerodynamic drag equation:

$$F_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot C_D \cdot A$$

Where:
- $F_D$ = Aerodynamic drag force (in **Newtons, N**)
- $\rho$ = Air density (standard value **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, 15°C)
- $V$ = Free-stream velocity of the vehicle (in **m/s**)
- $C_D$ = Drag coefficient (dimensionless; hatchbacks typically fall in the range **0.30 – 0.40**)
- $A$ = Frontal projected area (in **m²**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hatchbacks Suffer More Drag than Sedans:**
> A sedan has a long, gently sloping rear deck that allows the flow to remain attached for a longer distance, producing a smaller wake. A hatchback, in contrast, truncates this deck abruptly. The result: a **larger wake cross-section**, **higher pressure drag**, and typically a **10–20% higher drag coefficient** than an equivalently sized sedan.

---

## 1.2 Effect of Downforce on Vehicle Performance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Downforce Effect):**
> **Downforce** is the vertical aerodynamic load generated by pressure differentials across the vehicle body (typically achieved using inverted-wing spoilers, underbody diffusers, or roof-mounted aerodynamic aids). This downward force increases the **normal load (N)** on the tires, which in turn increases the maximum tractive and cornering force available at the tire–road interface (governed by the friction limit $F \le \mu N$). Thus, downforce **directly improves high-speed cornering grip, braking stability, and acceleration traction**, but at the cost of increased rolling resistance and added drag.

The downforce itself is described by an identical dimensional structure to the drag equation:

$$F_L = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot C_L \cdot A$$

Where:
- $F_L$ = Downforce / Negative lift (in **Newtons, N**)
- $C_L$ = Lift coefficient (negative for downforce; for race cars typically **-1.0 to -3.0**)
- All other terms identical to the drag equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used in Automotive Aerodynamics:**
> A **negative lift coefficient ($C_L < 0$)** indicates that the net aerodynamic force on the vehicle is directed **downward** (i.e., the vehicle is pressed onto the road). A positive $C_L$ indicates lift, which is generally undesirable for road cars at speed.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### Analogy 1: Hatchback Drag — "The Open Suitcase"
> Imagine pulling an **open suitcase** behind you while running. The air enters the suitcase but cannot exit smoothly — it tumbles around inside and creates a **partial vacuum** at the back, which yanks the suitcase backward. A hatchback behaves similarly: the smooth roof flow "enters" the slanted rear window, but when it hits the vertical tailgate, it cannot follow the abrupt 90° turn cleanly. It separates, swirls into a turbulent wake, and the resulting low-pressure zone **sucks the car rearward** — this suction is the dominant component of hatchback pressure drag.

### Analogy 2: Downforce — "The Airplane Wing Flipped Upside Down"
> An airplane wing generates **lift** because its curved upper surface accelerates airflow, lowering pressure above and creating an upward force. Now **flip the wing upside down and stick it to the roof of a car** — the high pressure is now on top and the low pressure is below. The car is literally **pushed toward the road**, just like a suction cup. This is exactly how a rear wing, front splitter, or underbody diffuser generates downforce on a sports or racing car.

### Analogy 3: Downforce Effect on Grip — "More Weight = More Friction"
> Think of pushing a heavy book across a table versus a light notebook. The heavy book grips the table better because of greater normal force. Downforce works identically: it artificially increases the car's "weight" on the tires **without adding actual mass**, increasing the maximum frictional force ($F_{friction} = \mu N$) available for cornering, braking, and accelerating.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Hatchback Aerodynamics

The flow physics around a hatchback can be broken down into the following sequential events:

### Step 1: Stagnation and Front-End Flow Attachment
As the vehicle approaches stationary air, the **stagnation point** forms on the front bumper/nose. The flow is deflected over the hood, around the A-pillars, and along the roof. For a well-designed hatchback, the flow remains **attached (laminar or mild turbulent)** along the hood, windshield, and roof.

### Step 2: The Critical Rear Glass / Roof Edge Region
At the **roof trailing edge** (where the rear window meets the tailgate), the flow encounters a **convex curvature** followed by an **abrupt angle change**. This is the **point of boundary-layer separation**. The adverse pressure gradient here is too strong for the boundary layer to overcome, so it detaches.

### Step 3: Wake Formation (The Drag Generator)
Once separated, the flow rolls up into discrete **vortices** (visible as swirling air masses). This creates a large **recirculation bubble** directly behind the vehicle. The wake has:
- **Low average static pressure** (Bernoulli's principle — fast-moving turbulent air = low pressure)
- **High turbulence intensity**
- **Large cross-sectional area**

The pressure difference between the high-pressure front of the car and the low-pressure wake produces a net rearward force = **pressure drag** (the dominant component, ~60-80% of total drag for a hatchback).

### Step 4: Base Drag and Flow Reattachment
Downstream of the wake, the flow eventually reattaches to the surrounding free stream. The base region behind the tailgate is the primary contributor to **base drag**, which scales roughly with the **base area (A_base)** of the vehicle's rear.

---

## 2.2 Methods to Reduce Hatchback Drag

| Method | Physical Principle | Typical $C_D$ Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| **Rear Spoiler (Roof Edge Lip)** | Delays flow separation by streamlining the roof-to-tailgate transition | 3–7% |
| **Extended Tailgate / Kammback Design** | Maintains attached flow for longer distance; truncates a teardrop shape | 5–12% |
| **Rear Diffuser (Underbody)** | Accelerates underbody flow, reduces rear base pressure (Ejector effect) | 4–8% |
| **Boat-Tail (Tapering Rear)** | Gradually reduces cross-section to allow flow to close smoothly | 8–15% |
| **Underbody Flattening & Wheel Covers** | Reduces underbody turbulent losses and wheel rotation drag | 2–5% |
| **Side Mirrors & A-Pillar Optimization** | Reduces local flow separation at frontal features | 1–3% |

---

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Downforce Effects

### Step 1: Generation of Negative Lift
Downforce is generated by:
1. **Rear Wing / Spoiler** (acts as inverted airfoil)
2. **Front Splitter** (creates high pressure below, low pressure above the splitter plane)
3. **Underbody Diffuser** (Venturi effect — accelerates underbody flow, creating low pressure that "sucks" the car down)
4. **Active Aerodynamics** (DRS, movable flaps — common in modern hypercars)

### Step 2: Effect on Tire Normal Load
Downforce adds to the static weight of the car on each axle:

$$N_{total,front} = W_{static,front} + F_{L,front}$$

$$N_{total,rear} = W_{static,rear} + F_{L,rear}$$

Where $W_{static}$ is the static weight distribution and $F_L$ is the downforce contribution at each axle.

### Step 3: Effect on Maximum Cornering, Braking, and Acceleration Force
The maximum force transferable to the road is governed by:

$$F_{max} = \mu \cdot N_{total}$$

This means downforce **linearly** increases the theoretical maximum cornering, braking, and acceleration force. However, the practical limit is reached when the tire saturates and the friction coefficient $\mu$ begins to decrease.

### Step 4: Trade-off with Drag
Downforce generation is **never free** — it always produces additional drag. The **L/D ratio (lift-to-drag ratio)** is a critical design metric. High-performance vehicles aim for the **highest downforce per unit drag penalty**:

$$L/D_{efficiency} = \frac{-C_L}{C_D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):**
> For a well-designed road car, doubling the downforce typically adds **20–40% more drag**. Race cars accept this trade-off because cornering grip is paramount; road cars typically limit downforce to preserve fuel efficiency.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$ | Aerodynamic Drag Force | N (Newtons) | Hatchback $C_D$ typically 0.30–0.40 |
| 2 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A$ | Aerodynamic Lift / Downforce | N (Newtons) | $C_L < 0$ for downforce |
| 3 | $P_D = F_D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 C_D A$ | Power required to overcome drag | W (Watts) | Cubic in V — drag dominates at high speed |
| 4 | $F_{max} = \mu N_{total}$ | Maximum tractive/cornering force | N | $N_{total} = W_{static} + F_L$ |
| 5 | $a_{max} = \mu g + \tfrac{F_L \cdot \mu}{m}$ | Maximum cornering acceleration | m/s² | Aerodynamic grip adds to mechanical grip |
| 6 | $L/D = -C_L / C_D$ | Aerodynamic efficiency (downforce per drag) | dimensionless | Higher is better for performance |
| 7 | $C_{D,total} = C_{D,front} + C_{D,base} + C_{D,skin}$ | Drag coefficient decomposition | dimensionless | Base drag dominates for hatchbacks |
| 8 | $F_{L,axle} = F_L \cdot (a_{downforce})$ | Downforce distribution front vs rear | N | Designer controls via wing position |
| 9 | $V_{critical} = \sqrt{(2W) / (\rho A C_L)}$ | Speed at which lift equals weight | m/s | Above this, vehicle becomes aerodynamically "weightless" |
| 10 | $\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho (V^2 - V_{wake}^2)$ | Pressure differential across wake | Pa (Pascals) | Drives base drag magnitude |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Constant:**
> Standard sea-level air density $\rho = \mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$ is used unless otherwise specified. Dynamic viscosity $\mu_{air} = \mathbf{1.81 \times 10^{-5} \; kg/(m \cdot s)}$ at 15°C.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Passenger Car Industry:** Hatchback manufacturers (VW Golf, Toyota Corolla hatch, Maruti Swift) invest heavily in **rear spoiler geometry, underbody panels, and active grille shutters** to reduce $C_D$ and improve fuel economy.
2. **Motorsport (Formula 1, WEC, NASCAR):** Downforce is the **single most important performance parameter** — a modern F1 car generates over **3.5 tonnes of downforce** at 250 km/h, allowing cornering forces exceeding 5G.
3. **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** EVs benefit from low drag for range; however, performance EVs (Tesla Model S Plaid, Lucid Air Sapphire) use downforce to enhance high-speed stability.
4. **Trucks and Heavy Vehicles:** Roof fairings and side skirts reduce drag by ~15–25%, saving millions of liters of fuel annually in logistics fleets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bernoulli's Equation to the Drag Equation

We start with Euler's equation for inviscid, incompressible flow along a streamline. Neglecting gravity and integrating from the free stream (point ∞) to a point on the vehicle surface (point s):

$$P_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 = P_s + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_s^2$$

Rearranging to find the surface pressure:

$$P_s = P_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho (V_\infty^2 - V_s^2)$$

The **dynamic pressure** is defined as:

$$q = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2$$

The local pressure coefficient $C_p$ (non-dimensional pressure) is:

$$C_p = \frac{P_s - P_\infty}{q} = 1 - \left(\frac{V_s}{V_\infty}\right)^2$$

For a stagnation point, $V_s = 0$, so $C_p = +1$ (maximum pressure).
For the wake (low pressure region), $C_p \ll 0$ (large negative values).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Drag Force by Pressure Integration

To compute total drag, we integrate the pressure distribution over the entire surface of the vehicle. In the **streamwise (x) direction**:

$$F_D = \oint_S (P_s - P_\infty) \cdot \hat{n} \cdot \hat{i} \; dA + \oint_S \tau_w \cdot dA$$

This simplifies into two distinct components:

$$F_D = F_{D,pressure} + F_{D,skin-friction}$$

For a hatchback:
- $F_{D,pressure}$ dominates (~70%) → comes from front stagnation pressure pushing forward AND wake suction pulling rearward.
- $F_{D,skin-friction}$ is smaller (~20–25%) → comes from viscous shear along the body.

The non-dimensional form gives the drag coefficient:

$$C_D = \frac{F_D}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Required to Overcome Hatchback Drag

The aerodynamic power required to push a vehicle through air at constant velocity $V$ equals the drag force times velocity:

$$P_D = F_D \cdot V$$

Substituting the drag equation:

$$P_D = \left(\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A\right) \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 C_D A$$

This **cubic dependence on velocity** is a critical insight — doubling the speed requires **8× the power** to overcome drag. This is why aerodynamic drag dominates fuel consumption at highway speeds.

---

### Worked Example 1: Hatchback Drag and Fuel Power

**Given:**
- Vehicle: Hatchback with $C_D = 0.35$
- Frontal area: $A = 2.2 \; m^2$
- Velocity: $V = 120 \; km/h = 33.33 \; m/s$
- Air density: $\rho = 1.225 \; kg/m^3$

**Find:** (a) Drag force, (b) Power required to overcome drag, (c) Equivalent fuel energy per hour.

**Solution:**

**(a) Drag Force:**

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$$

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (33.33)^2 \times 0.35 \times 2.2$$

Step-by-step:
- $V^2 = (33.33)^2 = 1111.1 \; m^2/s^2$
- $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1111.1 = 680.6 \; Pa$
- $C_D \cdot A = 0.35 \times 2.2 = 0.77 \; m^2$
- $F_D = 680.6 \times 0.77 = 524.0 \; N$

> ✅ **$F_D \approx 524 \; N$**

**(b) Power Required:**

$$P_D = F_D \cdot V = 524.0 \times 33.33 = 17{,}466 \; W \approx 17.47 \; kW$$

> ✅ **$P_D \approx 17.47 \; kW$**

**(c) Energy per hour:**

$$E = P_D \times t = 17{,}466 \; W \times 3600 \; s = 62.88 \; MJ$$

Assuming petrol energy density ≈ 34 MJ/litre and engine efficiency ≈ 25%:

$$Fuel \; used \approx \frac{62.88}{34 \times 0.25} = 7.4 \; litres/hour$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Downforce Contribution to Maximum Cornering Speed

For a vehicle of mass $m$ in steady-state cornering on a flat road, the centripetal force required equals the available frictional force:

$$\frac{m V^2}{R} = \mu \cdot N_{total} = \mu \cdot (mg + F_L)$$

Where $F_L$ is the total downforce. Substituting $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A$ (with $C_L < 0$):

$$\frac{m V^2}{R} = \mu mg + \mu \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \vert C_L \vert A$$

Rearranging to solve for the maximum cornering speed $V_{max}$:

$$V_{max}^2 \left(\frac{m}{R} - \frac{\mu \rho \vert C_L \vert A}{2}\right) = \mu mg$$

$$V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu m g}{\frac{m}{R} - \frac{\mu \rho \vert C_L \vert A}{2}}}$$

> **Key Observation:** As downforce ($|C_L|$) increases, the denominator shrinks, **increasing $V_{max}$**. This is the engineering justification for high-downforce setups in motorsport.

---

### Worked Example 2: Downforce Effect on Cornering Speed

**Given:**
- Vehicle mass: $m = 1200 \; kg$
- Static weight distribution: 50/50
- $C_L = -1.2$ (downforce coefficient)
- Frontal area: $A = 2.0 \; m^2$
- Corner radius: $R = 100 \; m$
- Tire-road friction: $\mu = 1.0$ (high-performance tires)
- Air density: $\rho = 1.225 \; kg/m^3$

**Find:** (a) Maximum cornering speed **without** downforce, (b) Maximum cornering speed **with** downforce, (c) Percentage improvement.

**Solution:**

**(a) Without Downforce ($C_L = 0$):**

$$V_{max} = \sqrt{\mu g R} = \sqrt{1.0 \times 9.81 \times 100} = \sqrt{981} = 31.32 \; m/s$$

$$V_{max} \approx 112.8 \; km/h$$

**(b) With Downforce ($C_L = -1.2$):**

Computing the downforce speed-squared coefficient:

$$\frac{\mu \rho \vert C_L \vert A}{2} = \frac{1.0 \times 1.225 \times 1.2 \times 2.0}{2} = 1.47 \; kg/m$$

Now:

$$V_{max}^2 = \frac{\mu m g}{\frac{m}{R} - 1.47}$$

$$V_{max}^2 = \frac{1.0 \times 1200 \times 9.81}{\frac{1200}{100} - 1.47} = \frac{11{,}772}{12 - 1.47} = \frac{11{,}772}{10.53} = 1117.95$$

$$V_{max} = \sqrt{1117.95} = 33.43 \; m/s \approx 120.4 \; km/h$$

**(c) Improvement:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{120.4 - 112.8}{112.8} \times 100\% = 6.74\%$$

> ✅ **Downforce increased cornering speed by ~6.7% in this case** — modest because road-car downforce levels are low. Race cars with $C_L = -2.5$ would see **15–25% improvement**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Drag vs Downforce Trade-off Table

| Design Feature | Effect on $C_D$ | Effect on $C_L$ | Net Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Larger rear wing | $\uparrow$ Significantly | $\downarrow\downarrow$ Strongly negative | More grip, more drag |
| Front splitter | $\uparrow$ Slightly | $\downarrow$ Moderately | Modest downforce gain |
| Underbody diffuser | $\downarrow$ (reduces base drag) | $\downarrow$ Significantly | Dual benefit! |
| Roof spoiler (hatchback) | $\uparrow$ Slightly | $\downarrow$ Slightly | Marginal improvement |
| Kammback truncation | $\downarrow\downarrow$ Strongly | Negligible | Best drag-only solution |
| Lower ride height | $\downarrow$ (less underbody air) | $\downarrow$ Slightly | Best balance |
| Wheel covers | $\downarrow$ Moderately | Negligible | Drag-only benefit |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The **underbody diffuser** is the rare feature that **reduces drag AND increases downforce** simultaneously — this is why every modern hypercar (Bugatti Chiron, McLaren 750S) uses an aggressive diffuser.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**Explain why hatchback cars generally have a higher drag coefficient than sedans. Mention two design modifications to reduce hatchback drag.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hatchback has an **abrupt vertical rear tailgate** following the **sloping rear window**. This sharp geometric transition forces the boundary layer to **separate early** at the roof trailing edge, creating a **large turbulent wake** with low base pressure. The high pressure at the front combined with low pressure at the rear produces a large **pressure drag component**, which dominates the total drag. Sedans, having a gently sloping rear deck, maintain attached flow over a longer distance, resulting in a smaller wake and lower $C_D$ (typically 0.25–0.32 vs. 0.32–0.40 for hatchbacks).

**Two design modifications (1 mark each):**
1. **Kammback truncation** — a tapered rear that maintains the attached flow for a longer distance.
2. **Rear roof-edge spoiler** — a small lip that delays flow separation at the trailing edge.
3. (Optional) **Underbody diffuser** — reduces base pressure and drag simultaneously.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**Define downforce. How does it improve vehicle cornering performance? Write the downforce equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Downforce is the **vertical aerodynamic force** acting **downward** on a vehicle, generated by aerodynamic aids such as rear wings, front splitters, or underbody diffusers. It is characterized by a **negative lift coefficient** ($C_L < 0$).

**Effect on cornering (1.5 marks):**
Downforce increases the **normal load (N)** on the tires. Since maximum frictional force $F_{max} = \mu N$, increased N allows **greater lateral (cornering) force** to be transferred to the road. This raises the **maximum cornering speed** ($V_{max} = \sqrt{\mu g R}$ without downforce, modified by $F_L$ term with downforce).

**Equation (0.5 mark):**
$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A$$

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **flow physics around a hatchback car** and identify the **regions of flow separation, wake formation, and pressure drag generation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hatchback car has the following data: $C_D = 0.36$, frontal area $A = 2.15 \; m^2$, $C_L = -0.15$, velocity $V = 100 \; km/h$, air density $\rho = 1.225 \; kg/m^3$. Calculate:
(i) The aerodynamic drag force
(ii) The aerodynamic downforce
(iii) The power required to overcome drag at this speed
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Flow Physics Around a Hatchback (7 marks):**

The flow over a hatchback proceeds through the following stages:

1. **Stagnation (1 mark):** At the front bumper, the oncoming air is brought to rest at the stagnation point, creating maximum pressure ($C_p = +1$).

2. **Front-end Attached Flow (1 mark):** The flow smoothly accelerates over the rounded hood, around the A-pillars, and along the windshield. The boundary layer remains attached due to favorable pressure gradients.

3. **Roof and Rear Glass (1 mark):** Flow continues to accelerate over the curved roof and sloped rear window, with pressure dropping below atmospheric.

4. **Critical Separation at Trailing Edge (2 marks):** At the **roof trailing edge** (junction of rear glass and vertical tailgate), the flow encounters a sharp convex curvature and an adverse pressure gradient that the boundary layer cannot overcome. The flow **separates**, forming a **shear layer** that rolls up into discrete **trailing vortices** (C-pillar vortices) and a **recirculating wake** region directly behind the car.

5. **Wake and Base Drag (1.5 marks):** The wake is a low-pressure region with high turbulence. The pressure difference between the high-pressure front and the low-pressure wake produces **pressure drag (base drag)**, which is the dominant component of total drag (~70%). The remaining ~20-25% is skin friction drag from viscous shear on the body surface, and ~5-10% is induced drag from the vortices.

6. **Reattachment (0.5 mark):** Far downstream, the wake flow eventually reattaches to the freestream and recovers to ambient pressure.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C_D = 0.36$, $A = 2.15 \; m^2$, $C_L = -0.15$, $V = 100 \; km/h = 27.78 \; m/s$, $\rho = 1.225 \; kg/m^3$

**Dynamic pressure calculation:**

$$q = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (27.78)^2$$

$$V^2 = 27.78^2 = 771.6 \; m^2/s^2$$

$$q = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 771.6 = 472.6 \; Pa$$

**(i) Aerodynamic Drag Force (2.5 marks):**

$$F_D = q \cdot C_D \cdot A = 472.6 \times 0.36 \times 2.15$$

$$F_D = 472.6 \times 0.774 = 365.8 \; N$$

> ✅ **$F_D \approx 365.8 \; N$**

**(ii) Aerodynamic Downforce (2.5 marks):**

$$F_L = q \cdot C_L \cdot A = 472.6 \times (-0.15) \times 2.15$$

$$F_L = 472.6 \times (-0.3225) = -152.4 \; N$$

The negative sign confirms the force is **downward**.

> ✅ **$F_L \approx 152.4 \; N$ (downward)**

**(iii) Power Required (2 marks):**

$$P_D = F_D \cdot V = 365.8 \times 27.78 = 10{,}163 \; W$$

> ✅ **$P_D \approx 10.16 \; kW$**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of downforce** and discuss in detail its **effect on vehicle cornering, braking, and acceleration performance**. Include a derivation showing how downforce modifies the maximum cornering speed equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sports car of mass $m = 1500 \; kg$ negotiates a curve of radius $R = 150 \; m$ at a speed of $V = 40 \; m/s$. The tire-road friction coefficient is $\mu = 1.1$. The vehicle produces a downforce coefficient $C_L = -1.5$ and frontal area $A = 2.0 \; m^2$. Take $\rho = 1.225 \; kg/m^3$.
(i) Calculate the downforce generated.
(ii) Calculate the maximum speed the car can negotiate the curve **with** downforce and **without** downforce.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Downforce Concept and Effects (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Downforce is the **net downward vertical aerodynamic force** generated on a vehicle moving through air, characterized by a **negative lift coefficient ($C_L < 0$)**. It is generated by **rear wings (inverted airfoils), front splitters, and underbody diffusers (Venturi effect)**.

**Effect on Cornering (2 marks):**
In a cornering maneuver, centripetal force is provided by tire-road friction. The maximum cornering force is $F_{max} = \mu N_{total}$, where $N_{total} = W_{static} + F_L$. Downforce artificially increases the normal load without adding mass, thereby **raising the maximum lateral force** the tires can transmit. This permits higher cornering speeds for a given radius.

**Effect on Braking (2 marks):**
During braking, the maximum deceleration is $a_{brake,max} = \mu g + \mu F_L / m$. Downforce **increases the maximum braking force** by increasing the normal load on all four tires, reducing stopping distance.

**Effect on Acceleration (1.5 marks):**
Similarly, the maximum tractive force during acceleration is $F_{traction,max} = \mu N_{rear} = \mu(W_{static,rear} + F_{L,rear})$. Downforce on the driven axle (typically the rear) improves acceleration, particularly during launch and high-speed acceleration.

**Derivation of cornering speed (0.5 mark) — outline:**

Equating centripetal force to available friction:

$$\frac{mV^2}{R} = \mu(W + F_L) = \mu(mg + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \vert C_L \vert A)$$

Solving for $V$:

$$V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu m g}{\frac{m}{R} - \frac{\mu \rho \vert C_L \vert A}{2}}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m = 1500 \; kg$, $R = 150 \; m$, $V = 40 \; m/s$, $\mu = 1.1$, $C_L = -1.5$, $A = 2.0 \; m^2$, $\rho = 1.225 \; kg/m^3$

**(i) Downforce Calculation (3 marks):**

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A$$

$$F_L = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (40)^2 \times (-1.5) \times 2.0$$

Step-by-step:
- $V^2 = 1600 \; m^2/s^2$
- $0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1600 = 980$
- $980 \times (-1.5) = -1470$
- $-1470 \times 2.0 = -2940 \; N$

> ✅ **$F_L \approx 2940 \; N$ (downward) — equivalent to ~204 kg of extra "aero-weight"**

**(ii) Maximum Cornering Speed (4 marks):**

**Without downforce ($C_L = 0$):**

$$V_{max,no\; aero} = \sqrt{\mu g R} = \sqrt{1.1 \times 9.81 \times 150}$$

$$V_{max,no\; aero} = \sqrt{1618.65} = 40.23 \; m/s \approx 144.8 \; km/h$$

**With downforce ($C_L = -1.5$):**

First, compute the speed-squared coefficient of downforce:

$$\frac{\mu \rho \vert C_L \vert A}{2} = \frac{1.1 \times 1.225 \times 1.5 \times 2.0}{2} = \frac{4.041}{2} = 2.021 \; kg/m$$

Now substitute:

$$V_{max,aero} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu m g}{\frac{m}{R} - 2.021}}$$

$$V_{max,aero} = \sqrt{\frac{1.1 \times 1500 \times 9.81}{\frac{1500}{150} - 2.021}}$$

$$V_{max,aero} = \sqrt{\frac{16186.5}{10 - 2.021}} = \sqrt{\frac{16186.5}{7.979}}$$

$$V_{max,aero} = \sqrt{2028.7} = 45.04 \; m/s \approx 162.1 \; km/h$$

> ✅ **Improvement: $V_{max}$ increased from 40.23 m/s to 45.04 m/s — a 12% gain in cornering speed due to downforce!**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Many students forget that $C_L$ is **negative for downforce** and incorrectly write $F_L$ as positive lift, then add it as if it were an upward force. This causes catastrophic errors in the cornering speed derivation.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Failing to convert $V$ from km/h to m/s is the **#1 source of calculation errors** in KTU papers. Always check: if $V$ is given in km/h, divide by 3.6 first.
> 3. **Forgetting the cubic dependence:** Power to overcome drag goes as $V^3$, not $V^2$. Writing $P_D = F_D \cdot V^2$ costs full marks.
> 4. **Misinterpreting the wake:** Students often describe the wake as "high pressure" — it is actually **low pressure**, and this is precisely what generates base drag.
> 5. **Omitting the underbody diffuser benefit:** When asked about drag reduction, mentioning only the rear spoiler is incomplete. The **underbody diffuser is the highest-yield answer** for hatchbacks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3, Hatchback Drag & Downforce:**

- ✅ **Hatchback drag** is dominated by **pressure drag** (~70%) arising from flow separation at the **roof trailing edge** and the resulting **low-pressure wake**.
- ✅ Hatchback $C_D$ is typically **10–20% higher** than sedan $C_D$ due to the abrupt rear geometry.
- ✅ Standard sea-level air density: $\rho = \mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$ — use this unless told otherwise.
- ✅ **Drag equation:** $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$ — power required: $P_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 C_D A$ (**cubic** in $V$).
- ✅ **Downforce equation:** $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A$ with $C_L < 0$ for downforce.
- ✅ Downforce **adds to static weight** on tires, increasing $N_{total}$ and therefore $F_{max} = \mu N_{total}$.
- ✅ **L/D ratio** ($L = -C_L$, $D = C_D$) is the key efficiency metric for aero devices.
- ✅ **Kammback design** is the most effective passive drag-reduction technique for hatchbacks.
- ✅ **Underbody diffusers** are the rare aero device that **reduce drag AND generate downforce** simultaneously.
- ✅ Maximum cornering speed with downforce: $V_{max} = \sqrt{\mu m g / (m/R - \mu \rho \vert C_L \vert A / 2)}$.
- ✅ Critical speed at which lift equals weight: $V_{critical} = \sqrt{2W / (\rho A \vert C_L \vert)}$.
- ✅ Downforce **always** increases drag — a fundamental trade-off in aerodynamic design.
- ✅ For race cars, downforce is the **most important performance parameter**; for road cars, low $C_D$ (fuel economy) is prioritized.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT753 - AUTOMOTIVE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vortices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Vortex

In the context of **Automotive Aerodynamics (KTU PEMUT753)**, a **vortex** is defined as a region within a moving fluid (air) where the flow is predominantly **rotational**, with the fluid particles revolving around a common imaginary axis line (the *vortex core*). Mathematically, a vortex is any region of flow where the **vorticity vector ($\vec{\omega}$)** is non-zero.

$$ \vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} \neq 0 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A point in a flow field where vorticity is concentrated is called a *vortex line*. A *vortex tube* is a tubular surface formed by a continuous family of vortex lines passing through a small closed curve. Vortex tubes cannot end inside a fluid — they either form closed loops or terminate at a solid boundary (a fundamental topological result from **Helmholtz's Vortex Theorems**).

The two key kinematic measures of a vortex are:
- **Circulation ($\Gamma$)** — a scalar measure of the total rotational "strength" around a closed loop.
- **Vorticity ($\vec{\omega}$)** — a vector field giving the local rate of rotation of fluid elements.

$$ \Gamma = \oint_{C} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stokes' Theorem Connection (Critical for KTU):** Circulation and vorticity are linked by:
> $$ \Gamma = \iint_{A} (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot d\vec{A} = \iint_{A} \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{A} $$
> This is the single most-examined identity in the Vortices module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **stirring a cup of coffee with a spoon** and then suddenly pull the spoon out. The coffee does not stop moving immediately — a swirling column of liquid keeps rotating about the spoon's vertical axis. That rotating column of fluid is a **vortex**.

In an automobile, vortices are everywhere you look:
- The air that curls up at the **trailing edge of a spoiler**.
- The two spirals of air that peel off the **outer edges of the A-pillars** (or wing mirrors).
- The tiny tornadoes of air that form under the **front bumper** of a moving car (the *front-end ground vortex*).
- The **wing-tip-like vortices** that roll off the roof's rear corners on fast-back and notchback sedans.

Each of these swirling pockets of air has **circulation**, **vorticity**, and a **direction of rotation**, and each one can either **help** (add downforce, reduce drag) or **hurt** (increase drag, induce lift, cause wind buffeting) the car's performance.

## 1.3 Why Vortices Matter in Automotive Aerodynamics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Vortices are **NOT** just academic curiosities — they directly govern:
> - **Aerodynamic drag** (vortices in the wake are the largest source of pressure drag).
> - **Aerodynamic lift/downforce** (organized vortices over wings, diffusers, and rear wings).
> - **Side-force asymmetry** (asymmetric vortex shedding can pull a car sideways at high speed).
> - **Wind noise & buffeting** (unsteady vortex shedding from A-pillars and mirrors).
> - **Cooling & soiling** (vortices over the hood carry dust, water, and insects to the windscreen).

## 1.4 Key Metrics & Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit (SI) | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|-----------|------------------|
| Vorticity | $\vec{\omega}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ (or $\text{rad/s}$) | Local angular velocity of a fluid element (twice the rigid-body rotation) |
| Circulation | $\Gamma$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Total rotational strength around a closed contour |
| Core radius | $r_c$ | $\text{m}$ | Radius of the rotating central region of a vortex |
| Tangential velocity | $V_\theta$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Speed of swirl at a radial distance $r$ |
| Strouhal number | $St$ | dimensionless | $St = \frac{f L}{V_\infty}$ — governs vortex shedding frequency |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Vortices (KTU-Relevant Taxonomy)

### 2.1.1 By Internal Velocity Distribution

**A. Free (Irrotational) Vortex**
- Fluid particles have **no angular velocity of their own** (irrotational everywhere except the axis).
- Tangential velocity **decreases with radius** as $V_\theta = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}$.
- Theoretically extends to $r \to \infty$ with $V_\theta \to 0$ (no singularity in velocity).
- **Example in cars:** The far-field rotational wake behind a vehicle.

**B. Forced (Solid-Body / Rotational) Vortex**
- Fluid particles rotate **as a rigid body** (vorticity is uniform and non-zero).
- Tangential velocity **increases linearly** with radius: $V_\theta = \omega r$.
- This is the form of rotation seen **inside the core** of a real vortex.
- **Example in cars:** The tight rotating core inside the vortex shed from a wing-mirror stay.

**C. Rankine Combined Vortex** ⭐ *KTU High-Yield*
- The **realistic model** — a forced vortex core (radius $r_c$) smoothly transitions into a free vortex outside.
- Most KTU problems on vortices use this model.

**D. Burgers Vortex**
- A vortex with axial stretching (viscous model) — appears in turbulent shear layers.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How a Vortex Forms on a Car

1. **Boundary-layer separation** occurs at a sharp edge (e.g., roof rear edge, A-pillar, wing tip).
2. The separated shear layer **rolls up** on itself due to the instability of the shear.
3. The rolled-up shear layer **concentrates vorticity** into a tight rotating core.
4. The core is convected downstream by the mean flow, leaving behind a **trailing vortex**.
5. For a 3-D wing-like body, the vorticity on the upper and lower surfaces does not cancel at the tip — it rolls up into a **wing-tip vortex** (the same physics as a fixed-wing aircraft, scaled to car geometry).

## 2.3 Helmholtz's Vortex Theorems (1849) — The Three Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are **favourite KTU short-answer questions** (3-mark category).

1. **Theorem 1 — Strength Conservation:** The strength of a vortex tube is constant along its length.
   $$ \Gamma_1 = \Gamma_2 = \text{constant} $$

2. **Theorem 2 — Zero Endings:** A vortex tube cannot end in a fluid — it must form a closed loop or terminate at a solid/fluid boundary.

3. **Theorem 3 — Circulation Conservation (Kelvin's Theorem):** In a barotropic, inviscid fluid with conservative body forces, the circulation around a material contour moving with the fluid is **constant in time**.
   $$ \frac{D\Gamma}{Dt} = 0 $$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for the End Semester Examination (ESE). Memorise the units, the conditions of validity, and the variable meanings.

| # | Formula | Name / Application | Validity / Condition |
|---|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ | Vorticity definition | All Newtonian fluids |
| 2 | $\Gamma = \oint_{C} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l}$ | Circulation around closed loop $C$ | Kelvin's theorem assumes barotropic, inviscid flow |
| 3 | $\Gamma = \iint_{A} \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{A}$ | Stokes' theorem link | Closed loop $C$ bounds surface $A$ |
| 4 | $V_\theta = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}$ | Free (irrotational) vortex | $r > r_c$ in Rankine model |
| 5 | $V_\theta = \omega r$ | Forced (solid-body) vortex | $r \le r_c$ in Rankine model |
| 6 | $V_{\theta,\max} = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi r_c}$ | Peak tangential velocity at Rankine core | At $r = r_c$ |
| 7 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Gamma}{4\pi h V_\infty}$ | Induced angle of attack (single horseshoe) | Used in lifting-line theory |
| 8 | $v_i = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi h}$ | Induced downwash velocity | $h$ = distance from vortex filament |
| 9 | $f = St \cdot \dfrac{V_\infty}{D}$ | Vortex shedding frequency | Bluff body of transverse size $D$ |
| 10 | $C_{D,i} = \dfrac{C_L^2}{\pi e \, AR}$ | Induced-drag coefficient | Elliptical loading assumed |
| 11 | $r_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \nu t}{1 - e^{-2 \omega t}}}$ | Lamb's core-growth (viscous diffusion) | Idealised viscous vortex |
| 12 | $St \approx 0.21$ | Strouhal number for a circular cylinder | $10^3 < Re < 2 \times 10^5$ (sub-critical) |

**Symbols used:** $\nu$ — kinematic viscosity; $t$ — time; $e$ — span-efficiency factor; $AR$ — aspect ratio; $h$ — perpendicular distance; $D$ — body diameter; $V_\infty$ — free-stream velocity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Automotive Application | Role of the Vortex | Performance Effect |
|------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Rear wing / spoiler tip | Tip vortex formation | Produces **induced drag** (loss) AND **circulation-driven lift** (downforce gain) |
| A-pillar / wing mirror | Unsteady shear-layer roll-up | Source of **wind noise** and **buffeting**; can be tamed by vortex generators |
| Front splitter / underbody | Ground vortex ingestion | Can starve the diffuser of air → **loss of downforce** |
| Vortex generators on roof/boot | Forced, controlled streamwise vortices | **Delay separation**; reduce wake size; **reduce drag** by 2–5 % |
| Diffuser exit | Organized longitudinal vortex pair | **Energises** the low-pressure region → **boosts downforce** |
| Wheels & wheel wells | Complex 3-D rotating flow | Major contributor to **aerodynamic drag** (~25–30 % of total $C_D$) |

## 2.6 The Biot–Savart Analogy (KTU-Critical for Downwash Calculations)

Just as a current-carrying wire induces a magnetic field, a straight **vortex filament of strength $\Gamma$** induces a velocity field at point $P$ located perpendicular distance $h$ from the (semi-infinite) filament:

$$ V_\theta = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi h} $$

For a **finite** straight filament running from point $A$ to point $B$:

$$ V_\theta = \frac{\Gamma}{4\pi h} \left( \cos\theta_1 + \cos\theta_2 \right) $$

where $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are the angles subtended at $P$ by the two ends of the filament. This is **mandatory** for solving Part-B problems on downwash/induced drag of car rear wings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are the **work-horse problems** in KTU Module-3 past papers. Work through every algebraic step on paper until you can reproduce them cold.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Velocity Field of a Free Vortex

**Problem:** Show that a free vortex with circulation $\Gamma$ has tangential velocity $V_\theta = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}$ and is irrotational everywhere except at the axis.

**Starting setup:** A straight, infinite vortex filament aligned with the $z$-axis. By axial symmetry, the velocity field has only a tangential component $V_\theta(r)$ in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the definition of circulation around a circle of radius $r$ centred on the axis.**

By symmetry, $\vec{V} = V_\theta(r)\,\hat{e}_\theta$ and $d\vec{l} = r\,d\theta\,\hat{e}_\theta$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Gamma &= \oint_{C} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{0}^{2\pi} V_\theta(r)\, r\, d\theta \\
       &= r\, V_\theta(r) \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta = 2\pi r\, V_\theta(r)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_\theta(r)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\pi r\, V_\theta(r) &= \Gamma \\
V_\theta(r) &= \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Prove irrotationality.** The curl in cylindrical coordinates (with only $V_\theta$ non-zero) gives the only non-zero vorticity component:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_z &= \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{\partial (r V_\theta)}{\partial r}\right] - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V_r}{\partial \theta} \\
\omega_z &= \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left[r \cdot \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}\right] = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left[\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\right] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the integrand is independent of $r$, the derivative is zero for any $r > 0$. **Therefore, everywhere except on the axis itself, the flow is irrotational.** On the axis, the velocity is singular (a *line vortex singularity*).

**Physical meaning:** The fluid is "rotating" in the sense of orbital motion (each particle traces a circle), but the particle itself is not spinning on its own axis — a beautifully counter-intuitive result that is a favourite KTU viva question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Rankine Combined Vortex Velocity Profile

**Problem:** Derive the velocity distribution $V_\theta(r)$ for a Rankine combined vortex of circulation $\Gamma$ and core radius $r_c$.

**Step 1 — Define the two regions.** A real vortex has a rotating core (viscous shear region) embedded in an irrotational outer flow. Match the two models at $r = r_c$.

**Step 2 — Inner region (forced vortex, $r \le r_c$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_\theta(r) &= \omega r \\
\text{At } r = r_c:\quad V_{\theta,\max} &= \omega r_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Outer region (free vortex, $r \ge r_c$):**

$$
V_\theta(r) = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply continuity of $V_\theta$ at $r = r_c$ to find the relationship between $\omega$ and $\Gamma$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega r_c &= \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r_c} \\
\omega &= \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r_c^{2}} \\
V_{\theta,\max} &= \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final piecewise expression:**

$$
\boxed{
V_\theta(r) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi r_c^{2}}\, r & \text{for } r \le r_c \\[10pt]
\dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi r} & \text{for } r \ge r_c
\end{cases}
}
$$

**Step 6 — Pressure field (useful for KTU):** Applying Bernoulli's equation along any radial line (irrotational outside the core, so valid there):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} &= p(r) + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\theta^{2}(r) \\
p(r) &= p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\left(V_\infty^{2} - \frac{\Gamma^{2}}{4\pi^{2} r^{2}}\right) \quad (r \ge r_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows a **pressure minimum** at the core — which is why vortex cores are *suction regions* exploited in vortex-based flow-control devices.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Induced Downwash from a Single Horseshoe Vortex

**Problem:** A semi-infinite horseshoe vortex with trailing legs separated by span $b$ moves with free-stream $V_\infty$. Show that the trailing vortex at distance $h$ behind the wing induces a downwash velocity $v_i = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi h}$ and an induced angle of attack $\alpha_i = \dfrac{\Gamma}{4\pi h V_\infty}$.

**Step 1 — Velocity induced by a semi-infinite straight vortex filament.** Using the Biot–Savart analogy (Section 2.6), a semi-infinite filament contributes half the field of a full infinite one:

$$
V_\theta = \frac{\Gamma}{4\pi h}\bigl(\cos\theta_1 + \cos\theta_2\bigr)
$$

For the **trailing leg** of a horseshoe vortex, one end is at infinity ($\cos\theta = 1$) and the other is at the wing tip ($\cos\theta = 0$ at the bound-vortex junction). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_i &= \frac{\Gamma}{4\pi h}\,(1 + 0) = \frac{\Gamma}{4\pi h} \quad \text{(single trailing leg)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — For the symmetric pair of trailing legs at the two wing tips, both contribute downward velocity at a point mid-span, so the total induced velocity is:**

$$
v_{i,\text{total}} = 2 \times \frac{\Gamma}{4\pi h} = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi h}
$$

**Step 3 — The induced angle of attack is the ratio of induced velocity to free-stream velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_i &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{v_i}{V_\infty}\right) \approx \frac{v_i}{V_\infty} = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi h V_\infty}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For an automotive rear wing** of chord $c$ and span $b$, $h$ is typically taken as $\dfrac{\pi b}{4}$ (Trefftz-plane distance) for an elliptical loading distribution, yielding the standard induced-drag formula $C_{D,i} = \dfrac{C_L^2}{\pi\, e\, AR}$ where $AR = b^2/S$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Strouhal Number for Bluff-Body Vortex Shedding

**Problem:** A road-roller-shaped body of diameter $D = 1.2\,\text{m}$ travels at $V_\infty = 30\,\text{m/s}$ in still air. Using $St = 0.21$ (circular-cylinder value, applicable to rounded bluff bodies), find the vortex-shedding frequency.

**Step 1 — Definition of the Strouhal number:**

$$
St = \frac{f\, D}{V_\infty}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $f$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{St \cdot V_\infty}{D} \\
  &= \frac{0.21 \times 30\,\text{m/s}}{1.2\,\text{m}} \\
  &= 5.25\,\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Wavelength of the shed wake:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{V_\infty}{f} = \frac{30}{5.25} \approx 5.71\,\text{m}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** This $5.25\,\text{Hz}$ periodic forcing can excite resonances in tall antennae, thin spoilers, or wing-mirror mounts — a **classic KTU interview question** on automotive buffeting.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Choosing the Right Vortex Model

| Physical Situation | Best Model | Key Equation | Typical $r_c$ |
|--------------------|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| Far-field wake of a car | Free vortex | $V_\theta = \Gamma/(2\pi r)$ | Not applicable (unbounded) |
| Tight tip vortex of a rear wing | Rankine combined | Piecewise (Section 3.2) | 1–10 mm (in full scale) |
| Vortex inside a diffuser | Forced + axial stretch (Burgers) | $V_\theta = \omega r \cdot e^{-r^2/a^2}$ | 5–50 mm |
| Ground vortex under front splitter | Free + ground-image vortex | $V_\theta = \Gamma/(2\pi r) - \Gamma/(2\pi (r+2h))$ | Depends on ride height |
| Vortex shed by A-pillar (unsteady) | Oscillating point vortex / discrete vortex method | $f = St\,V_\infty/D$ | N/A |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid block diagrams** are drawn at the "functional architecture" level — they map how vortices form, evolve, and dissipate in the automotive flow environment. They are validated against Mermaid v10 syntax (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside node labels, no reserved keyword names).

## 4.1 Vortex Formation and Evolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FREESTREAM AIR V_INF] --> B[VEHICLE SOLID SURFACE]
    B --> C[BOUNDARY LAYER GROWTH]
    C --> D[ADVERSE PRESSURE GRADIENT]
    D --> E[BOUNDARY LAYER SEPARATION]
    E --> F[SHEAR LAYER ROLLUP]
    F --> G[PRIMARY VORTEX CORE]
    G --> H{VORTEX STABILITY CHECK}
    H -->|STABLE| I[VORTEX CONVECTED DOWNSTREAM]
    H -->|UNSTABLE| J[VORTEX PAIRING OR BREAKDOWN]
    I --> K[CONTRIBUTES TO DRAG OR DOWNFORCE]
    J --> L[TURBULENT WAKE STRUCTURE]
    L --> M[FINAL DISSIPATION INTO AMBIENT AIR]

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style G fill:#ffe1cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style H fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#999900,color:#000
    style K fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900,color:#000
    style M fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666666,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Rankine Vortex Velocity Profile — Schematic Cross Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORCED_CORE[FORCED VORTEX REGION r LE r_c]
        R1[CENTRE  V_THETA 0] --> R2[CORE EDGE  V_THETA MAX EQUALS GAMMA OVER 2 PI r_c]
    end
    subgraph FREE_OUTER[FREE VORTEX REGION r GE r_c]
        R3[NEAR FIELD  V_THETA DECAYS AS 1 OVER r] --> R4[FAR FIELD  V_THETA APPROACHES 0]
    end
    FORCED_CORE --> FREE_OUTER
    P[PRESSURE PROFILE] --> P1[P MAX AT R 0] --> P2[P MIN AT r r_c SUCTION] --> P3[P RECOVERY TO P_INF AT r INFINITY]
    style FORCED_CORE fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style FREE_OUTER fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style P1 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000
    style P2 fill:#9933ff,stroke:#330066,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#99ccff,stroke:#0066cc
```

## 4.3 Automotive Vortex Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INLET[INLET REGION]
        I1[Front bumper] --> I2[Radiator grille airflow]
        I2 --> I3[Front splitter stagnation]
        I3 --> I4[Front end GROUND VORTEX]
    end
    subgraph TOP[VEHICLE UPPER SURFACE]
        T1[Windscreen] --> T2[Roof curvature separation]
        T2 --> T3[Roof trailing edge vortex]
        T1 --> T4[A PILLAR VORTEX  MIRROR WAKE]
    end

        S1[Wheel rotation] --> S2[Wheel arch ground vortex]
        S2 --> S3[Side rocker vortices]
    end
    subgraph REAR[REAR REGION]
        R1[Boat tail flow] --> R2[C PILLAR SEPARATION]
        R2 --> R3[REAR WING TIP VORTICES]
        R3 --> R4[Diffuser longitudinal vortex pair]
        R4 --> R5[Base wake vortex street]
    end
    INLET --> TOP
    TOP --> REAR
    INLET --> SIDE
    SIDE --> REAR
    style I4 fill:#ff9966,stroke:#cc3300

```

## 4.4 Horseshoe Vortex — Induced Downwash Geometry

```mermaid

    B --> C[TRAILING VORTEX LEG LEFT TIP]
    B --> D[TRAILING VORTEX LEG RIGHT TIP]
    C --> E[DOWNWASH v_i AT TREFFTZ PLANE]
    D --> E
    E --> F[INDUCED ANGLE alpha_i EQUALS v_i OVER V_INF]
    F --> G[INDUCED DRAG C_D_i EQUALS C_L SQUARED OVER PI e AR]
```
