<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define vorticity and circulation. State the relation between them. — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Vorticity** $\vec{\omega}$ is defined as the curl of the velocity field:
$$ \vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} $$
It is a vector quantity representing the **local angular velocity** (twice the rate of rotation) of a fluid element. SI unit: $\text{s}^{-1}$.

**Circulation** $\Gamma$ is the **line integral of velocity** around a closed contour $C$:
$$ \Gamma = \oint_{C} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} $$
It is a **scalar** measure of the total rotational tendency of the fluid. SI unit: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

**Relation (Stokes' Theorem):** They are linked through the surface integral of vorticity over any surface $A$ bounded by $C$:
$$ \Gamma = \iint_{A} (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot d\vec{A} = \iint_{A} \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{A} $$

### Q2. State and explain Helmholtz's vortex theorems. — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Theorem 1 (Strength conservation):** The strength $\Gamma$ of a vortex tube is the same at every cross-section along its length. Vorticity is neither created nor destroyed inside the fluid.

2. **Theorem 2 (Vortex tubes cannot end in a fluid):** A vortex tube must either form a **closed loop** or terminate at a **solid/fluid boundary**. This is why tip vortices trailing a wing persist far downstream — they cannot simply vanish in mid-air.

3. **Theorem 3 (Kelvin's circulation theorem):** In an inviscid, barotropic flow with conservative body forces, the circulation around a closed material curve moving with the fluid is **constant in time**: $\dfrac{D\Gamma}{Dt} = 0$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module-3 Question with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **ESE Question Paper Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module-level choice — either Q(A) or Q(B), each carrying **14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solutions are expected to be analytical with full derivation steps.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Dec 2023 pattern]`

**(a)** Derive the velocity distribution of a **Rankine combined vortex** of core radius $r_c$ and circulation $\Gamma$. Sketch the variation of tangential velocity $V_\theta$ and pressure $p$ with radial distance $r$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical chimney of diameter $D = 0.8\,\text{m}$ in a steady cross-wind of $V_\infty = 20\,\text{m/s}$ sheds Kármán vortices. Using the Strouhal number $St = 0.21$ for sub-critical flow, calculate the **(i)** vortex shedding frequency and **(ii)** the wavelength of the vortex street. Comment on the relevance of this shedding for a road-going truck with such a chimney. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(A)(a)

**Step 1 — Concept and assumptions.** A Rankine combined vortex models a real vortex as a **forced (solid-body) vortex** inside a core of radius $r_c$, smoothly transitioning to a **free (irrotational) vortex** outside. We use cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ with the filament along the $z$-axis.

**Step 2 — Inner region ($r \le r_c$):** For solid-body rotation with angular velocity $\omega$:
$$ V_\theta(r) = \omega r $$

**Step 3 — Outer region ($r \ge r_c$):** For an irrotational free vortex:
$$ V_\theta(r) = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r} $$

**Step 4 — Match at $r = r_c$ (continuity of velocity):**
$$ \omega r_c = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r_c} \;\Longrightarrow\; \omega = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r_c^{2}} $$

**Step 5 — Compact form:**
$$
V_\theta(r) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\Gamma\, r}{2\pi r_c^{2}} & r \le r_c \\[8pt]
\dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi r} & r \ge r_c
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 6 — Pressure field** (apply Bernoulli outside the core, $r \ge r_c$):
$$ p(r) = p_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho\!\left(V_\infty^{2} - \frac{\Gamma^{2}}{4\pi^{2} r^{2}}\right) $$
Inside the core ($r \le r_c$), use the rigid-body hydrostatic relation:
$$ p(r) = p(r_c) - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,\omega^{2}(r_c^{2} - r^{2}) $$

**Step 7 — Sketch description.** $V_\theta$ rises linearly from $0$ to $V_{\theta,\max} = \Gamma/(2\pi r_c)$ at $r = r_c$, then decays hyperbolically as $1/r$. Pressure $p$ falls from a high value at the centre (or $p_\infty$ at large $r$) to a **minimum at $r = r_c$** (the core suction peak), then recovers to $p_\infty$ as $r \to \infty$.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(A)(b)

**Step 1 — Strouhal number definition:**
$$ St = \frac{f\,D}{V_\infty} $$

**Step 2 — Solve for shedding frequency $f$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{St \cdot V_\infty}{D} \\
  &= \frac{0.21 \times 20}{0.8} \\
  &= \frac{4.2}{0.8} = 5.25\,\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Wavelength of the Kármán street:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{V_\infty}{f} = \frac{20}{5.25} \approx 3.81\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Automotive relevance.** A $5.25\,\text{Hz}$ periodic pressure fluctuation on a truck's chimney/mirror/extended antenna can:
- Induce **audible whistle tones** (since $5.25\,\text{Hz}$ is sub-audible, the harmonic content is the real concern).
- Couple with structural modes of the antenna or roof rack.
- Cause **driver-fatigue resonances** if the forcing couples with cabin acoustic modes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget the units check** on $f$ (should be Hz) and **confuse $f$ with $\lambda$**. Another common error: using $St = 0.3$ (value for square cylinders) instead of $St = 0.21$ (circular cylinder). **Read the problem statement carefully.**

---

### ❑ QUESTION B — Alternative choice (Internal Option) — `[KTU University Exam - pattern, Dec 2024 expected]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between a **free vortex** and a **forced vortex**. Derive the tangential velocity distribution for a free vortex and show that it is **irrotational** except at the axis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A car has a rear wing of span $b = 1.5\,\text{m}$ and chord $c = 0.3\,\text{m}$ operating at free-stream velocity $V_\infty = 60\,\text{m/s}$. The wing has an elliptical lift distribution. Take $C_L = 0.6$ and span efficiency factor $e = 0.95$. Compute the **(i)** aspect ratio, **(ii)** induced-drag coefficient, and **(iii)** total downforce if the wing's planform area is $0.45\,\text{m}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(B)(a)

**Step 1 — Definitions.**
- **Free vortex:** Velocity is purely tangential, $V_\theta \propto 1/r$. Vorticity $\omega = 0$ everywhere (irrotational). Examples: drain vortex, far-field wing-tip vortex.
- **Forced vortex:** Solid-body rotation, $V_\theta = \omega r$. Vorticity is uniform and equal to $2\omega$. Example: rotating tank, inner core of a real vortex.

**Step 2 — Derivation of free-vortex velocity** (using circulation around a circle of radius $r$):
$$
\Gamma = \oint \vec{V}\cdot d\vec{l} = 2\pi r V_\theta \;\Longrightarrow\; V_\theta = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}
$$

**Step 3 — Irrotationality proof.** In cylindrical coordinates, the only non-zero component of curl is:
$$ \omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (rV_\theta)}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left[r\cdot\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}\right] = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left[\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\right] = 0 \quad (r>0) $$

Since the bracketed term is a constant, its derivative is zero. Therefore the vorticity vanishes **for all $r > 0$** — the flow is irrotational.

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.** Even though particles trace circular paths, they are **not spinning on their own axis**. A small floating leaf placed in the flow would orbit the centre without itself rotating.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(B)(b)

**Step 1 — Aspect ratio $AR$:**
$$
AR = \frac{b^{2}}{S} = \frac{(1.5)^{2}}{0.45} = \frac{2.25}{0.45} = 5.0
$$

**Step 2 — Induced-drag coefficient** (from lifting-line theory for an elliptically loaded wing):
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{D,i} &= \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\, e\, AR} \\
        &= \frac{(0.6)^{2}}{\pi \times 0.95 \times 5.0} \\
        &= \frac{0.36}{14.922} \approx 0.0241
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total downforce.** Lift (downforce) is:
$$ L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\,S\,C_L $$
Using $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \tfrac{1}{2}\times 1.225 \times (60)^{2} \times 0.45 \times 0.6 \\
  &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 \times 0.27 \\
  &= 595.35\,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Induced drag force:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_i &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\,S\,C_{D,i} \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 \times 0.45 \times 0.0241 \\
    &\approx 23.9\,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment.** The downforce of ~$595\,\text{N}$ is excellent for a $1500\,\text{kg}$ sedan at highway speed — equivalent to roughly $40\,\text{kgf}$ of vertical grip. The induced drag of ~$24\,\text{N}$ is a *modest* penalty, which is why properly designed elliptical-loaded wings are preferred over rectangular ones for motorsport applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q-B-b):**
> 1. Many students compute $C_{D,i}$ without multiplying by $e$ — losing 2 marks.
> 2. Air density $\rho$ is **not** given; students who write "$L = \tfrac{1}{2}V_\infty^{2} S C_L$" (missing $\rho$) lose 1 full mark.
> 3. Mistaking "downforce" for negative lift — sign convention must be stated clearly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places students lose marks in the "Vortices" module:**
> 1. **Confusing vorticity and circulation** — they are *related* (Stokes) but not *the same*. Vorticity is a *local* vector; circulation is an *integrated* scalar.
> 2. **Forgetting that a free vortex is irrotational** — students incorrectly say "the fluid is rotating therefore it has vorticity." Wrong: orbital motion is irrotational if the angular velocity of the particles themselves is zero.
> 3. **Sign errors in induced-drag formula** — the induced-drag direction is **parallel to the freestream**, **opposite** to it. The downwash is **downward**, so the local relative wind is tilted **backward** at the lifting surface, reducing the effective angle of attack.
> 4. **Using $\omega = 2\pi f$ vs $\omega = 2\pi n$ in Strouhal problems** — students confuse *rotational* angular velocity with *cyclic* frequency. Read the symbol in context.
> 5. **Not sketching the $V_\theta(r)$ profile** for the Rankine vortex — examiners often award 2 marks purely for a correctly drawn graph. Always draw and label.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — print this out the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Vorticity** $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ — local rotation of fluid element.
- ✅ **Circulation** $\Gamma = \oint \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l}$ — integrated rotation around a loop.
- ✅ **Stokes' theorem:** $\Gamma = \iint_A \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{A}$ — the bridge between the two.
- ✅ **Free vortex:** $V_\theta = \Gamma / (2\pi r)$, $\omega = 0$ (irrotational).
- ✅ **Forced vortex:** $V_\theta = \omega r$, uniform $\omega = 2\omega_{\text{spin}}$.
- ✅ **Rankine combined vortex:** piecewise definition; **peak** $V_\theta$ occurs at $r = r_c$.
- ✅ **Helmholtz's three theorems:** (1) $\Gamma$ constant along a tube, (2) tubes cannot end in fluid, (3) Kelvin: $D\Gamma/Dt = 0$ in inviscid barotropic flow.
- ✅ **Biot–Savart analogy** for a semi-infinite filament: $V_\theta = \Gamma/(4\pi h)\,(\cos\theta_1 + \cos\theta_2)$.
- ✅ **Induced downwash** of a horseshoe: $v_i = \Gamma/(2\pi h)$ at spanwise mid-point.
- ✅ **Induced-drag coefficient:** $C_{D,i} = C_L^{2}/(\pi e\,AR)$ — elliptical loading assumed.
- ✅ **Strouhal number:** $St = fD/V_\infty \approx 0.21$ for circular cylinders in sub-critical flow.
- ✅ **Automotive applications to remember:** tip vortices → induced drag; A-pillar/mirror → noise & buffeting; front splitter → ground-vortex ingestion; vortex generators → separation control; diffuser → energised longitudinal vortex pair.
- ✅ **Sign of circulation** is given by the **right-hand rule** — curl the fingers in the direction of flow, thumb gives $\vec{\omega}$ direction (and $\Gamma$ sign).
- ✅ **Kelvin's theorem is violated** by viscous effects (boundary layers) and non-barotropic effects (combustion, heat addition) — this is *why* real wings have drag.
- ✅ **Vortex breakdown** is the sudden transition from organised to turbulent vortex flow — important at high angles of attack on rear wings of race cars.

---

*End of Module 3 — Vortices — KTU 2024 Scheme Premium Study Notes.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerodynamic Testing: Wind Tunnels for Automotive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT753 - AUTOMOTIVE AERODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Aerodynamic Testing: Wind Tunnels for Automotive # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wind Tunnel (KTU 2024 Definition):** A wind tunnel is a closed or open-ended ducted facility that generates a controlled, steady, and uniform airstream of known velocity, pressure, and temperature, in which a stationary full-scale vehicle or its geometrically scaled model is mounted on a force balance or pressure-measurement rig to experimentally determine aerodynamic coefficients (C_d, C_l, C_m, C_y), surface pressure distributions, flow-field behaviour, and visualisation features such as wake, separation, and vortex structures. For the KTU syllabus, the term **"Automotive Wind Tunnel"** specifically refers to a facility optimised for ground-vehicle testing — typically with provisions for **ground-plane simulation** (boundary-layer suction, moving belts, plenum chambers), **force balance mounting**, and **yaw-table rotation** to replicate cross-wind conditions. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Treadmill for Cars":** > Imagine a treadmill in a gym. The runner stays in the *same physical place*, but the belt slides beneath his feet so that the *relative motion* (foot ↔ belt) is exactly what it would be on a real track. An automotive wind tunnel does **the same thing in reverse**: the *car is stationary* (mounted on a balance), while **a giant artificial wind blows past it** at the precise velocity the car would experience on the road. To the air molecules surrounding the car, it is *as if* the vehicle were genuinely travelling at 100 km/h on a highway. This "freeze the car, move the air" trick is the entire conceptual foundation of every automotive wind tunnel in the world. ## 1.3 Why Automotive Wind Tunnels are Indispensable | Engineering Need | How Wind Tunnel Solves It | |---|---| | Quantify drag (fuel economy, EV range) | Six-component force balance measures drag directly | | Predict lift/downforce at speed | Vertical force & pressure taps under-body | | Visualise wake & separation | Tufts, smoke, oil-film, PIV | | Validate CFD codes | Experimental benchmark data | | Climate, soiling & water management | Climatic tunnels with rain, snow, sun rigs | | Cross-wind stability (aero-elasticity) | Yaw turntable + unsteady balance | | Acoustic source identification | Anechoic test section + microphones | ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard atmospheric density at sea level:** $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$ - **Dynamic viscosity of air at 15 °C:** $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{kg/(m·s)}$ - **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho = 1.46 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$ - **Speed of sound at 15 °C:** $a = 340.3 \; \text{m/s}$ - **Reference frontal area for cars:** Projected area in the y–z plane (driver-frontal convention) - **Reference length for cars:** Wheelbase $L$ (often) or mean aerodynamic chord > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Pointer:** Always cite frontal area $A_f$ in m² and velocity in m/s (not km/h) in your formula sheet. Conversion: $V [\text{m/s}] = V [\text{km/h}] / 3.6$. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Automotive Wind Tunnels The KTU 2024 module emphasises the following taxonomy: ### A) By Circuit Topology 1. **Open-Circuit (Eiffel type):** Air drawn once through the test section and dumped to atmosphere. - *Pros:* Simple, cheap, easy access. - *Cons:* Flow quality varies with atmospheric conditions, high noise. 2. **Closed-Circuit (Göttingen type):** Air recirculated in a closed loop with corner turning vanes. - *Pros:* Superior flow uniformity, low turbulence intensity (Tu < 0.1 %), temperature control, quiet. - *Cons:* Expensive, large footprint. ### B) By Test-Section Velocity - **Subsonic** ($M < 0.3$): Used for > 95 % of automotive work because cars rarely exceed $M = 0.3$ (≈ 360 km/h). - **Transonic / Supersonic:** Only for F1, hypercars, or specialised research. ### C) By Scale - **Full-scale:** Real vehicle, real Reynolds number. *Industry benchmark.* - **Scale model (typically 1:1 to 1:4):** Cheaper; requires Reynolds-number matching. ### D) By Special Purpose - **Climatic Wind Tunnel (CWT):** Includes environmental chamber for temperature (−40 °C to +55 °C), humidity, solar, rain. - **Aero-Acoustic Tunnel:** Anechoic walls with microphone arrays. - **Thermal-Management Tunnel:** For under-hood cooling, soiling, water. - **Full-Scale Moving-Belt Tunnel:** Rolls + 5-belt ground simulation. ## 2.2 Major Components & Their Function | Component | Function | Design Rule of Thumb | |---|---|---| | **Inlet / Bellmouth** | Smooth air entry, accelerates flow | Contraction half-angle ≈ 22° max | | **Honeycomb + Screens** | Reduces swirl & turbulence intensity | Cell size ≈ 6–10 mm; 2–3 screens | | **Settling Chamber** | Decay residual turbulence | Length ≈ 4–6 × equivalent screen diameter | | **Contraction (Nozzle)** | Accelerates & uniformises flow | Area ratio 9:1 to 16:1 | | **Test Section** | Houses vehicle + balances | Area constant; corner-fillet radius ≈ 10 % of width | | **Diffuser** | Recovers pressure, reduces fan energy | Half-angle 3°–5° | | **Drive Fan** | Provides circulation | Variable-pitch for speed control | | **Corner Vanes** | Turn flow with minimum loss | Turning radius ≈ 1.5 × duct width | | **Force Balance** | Measures 6 components (F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z) | Stiffness > 10⁶ N/m | | **Ground-Plane System** | Simulates moving road | 5-belt or 3-belt + suction | | **Yaw Turntable** | Rotates vehicle for cross-wind | Rotation ± 30° typical | ## 2.3 The Three Cardinal Requirements of Automotive Wind-Tunnel Testing > [!IMPORTANT] > **1. Geometric Similarity** — Shape must be identical at all scales. > **2. Kinematic Similarity** — Streamline pattern identical (governed by Reynolds number for incompressible flow). > **3. Dynamic Similarity** — Forces in ratio identical (force-coefficients identical). For road-vehicle aerodynamics, incompressible flow ($\rho$ = const) and low Mach numbers allow us to *ignore* Mach similarity. The dominant non-dimensional group is the **Reynolds number** $\text{Re}$. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Do NOT use the pipe character `|` inside table cells. Use `\vert` instead.** | # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number — sets flow regime (laminar vs turbulent) | dimensionless | | 2 | $C_D = \dfrac{D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\,A_f}$ | Drag coefficient — primary automotive metric | dimensionless | | 3 | $C_L = \dfrac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\,A_f}$ | Lift coefficient — front/rear axle load transfer | dimensionless | | 4 | $q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho V_{\infty}^{2}$ | Dynamic / impact pressure (measured by Pitot-static probe) | Pa | | 5 | $P_{\text{drag}} = D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho V^{3} C_D A_f$ | Power lost to overcoming drag (governs EV range) | W | | 6 | $\varepsilon_s \approx \dfrac{A_m^{3/2}}{C_D^{1/2} A_t}$ | **Solid blockage ratio** (Maskell correction) | dimensionless | | 7 | $\varepsilon_w \approx \dfrac{C_D A_m}{2 A_t^{2}}$ | **Wake blockage ratio** | dimensionless | | 8 | $C_D^{\text{corr}} = C_D^{\text{meas}} - 2(\varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_w)$ | Blockage-corrected drag coefficient | dimensionless | | 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho A_t V$ | Mass flow rate through test section | kg/s | | 10 | $P_{\text{fan}} = \dfrac{\Delta p_{\text{fan}} \cdot Q}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$ | Fan power requirement | W | | 11 | $\delta(x) \approx \dfrac{0.37\, x}{\text{Re}_x^{1/5}}$ | Turbulent boundary layer thickness | m | | 12 | $C_f = \dfrac{0.074}{\text{Re}_L^{1/5}}$ | Average turbulent skin-friction coefficient (flat plate) | dimensionless | | 13 | $C_{\mu} = \dfrac{T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho V_{\infty}^{2} A_f L}$ | Yaw-moment / pitching-moment coefficient | dimensionless | | 14 | $V_m = V_{\text{full}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L_{\text{full}}}{L_m}\right)$ | Scale-model speed for same Re (same fluid) | m/s | | 15 | $E_{\text{fuel}} \propto C_D A_f$ | Fuel economy index at constant speed | — | ## 2.5 Engineering Significance of Each Equation - **Equation 1 (Reynolds number):** Determines whether boundary layer is laminar, transitional, or turbulent — directly affects $C_D$ and stall behaviour. - **Equation 2–4 (Force coefficients):** Dimensionless form makes data comparable across models, tunnels, and full scale. - **Equation 5 (Power to overcome drag):** For an EV, every 0.01 reduction in $C_D$ adds ≈ 5–8 km of range — *the single most important number in modern automotive aero*. - **Equation 6–8 (Blockage corrections):** Without these, model data in a small test section **over-estimates drag** — a classic KTU exam trap. - **Equation 11 (Boundary layer):** Critical for ground-simulation design — the moving-belt system must recreate the natural turbulent boundary layer thickness. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget that **atmospheric pressure variations** at high altitudes change $\rho$ and hence $C_D$. All data must be referenced to a standard $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$ for KTU numerical problems unless stated otherwise. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Drag-Coefficient Measurement Equation **Statement:** When a vehicle is mounted on a six-component force balance inside the test section, the strain gauges record the axial force $F_x$. Show that this can be converted to the dimensionless drag coefficient $C_D$. **Starting Principle — Newton's Resistance Law for a Body in Uniform Flow:** $$ D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\,A_f\,C_D $$ **Step 1 — Identify what the balance measures.** In still air, $V_{\infty} = 0$, so $D = 0$. With flow, the balance detects the equal and opposite reaction force on the body. Therefore: $$ F_x = D $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the dynamic pressure $q_{\infty}$.** The dynamic (impact) pressure measured by a Pitot-static probe in the empty test section is: $$ q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho V_{\infty}^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $q_{\infty}$ back into the drag equation.** $$ D = q_{\infty} \cdot A_f \cdot C_D $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange for $C_D$.** $$ C_D = \dfrac{D}{q_{\infty}\,A_f} = \dfrac{F_x}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\,A_f} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result (canonical form):** $\boxed{\,C_D = \dfrac{F_x}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\,A_f}\,}$ > This is the equation examiners expect you to *derive*, not merely *state*. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Reynolds-Number Scaling Law for Scale-Model Testing **Problem:** A real car of length $L_{\text{full}} = 4.5 \; \text{m}$ is tested at $V_{\text{full}} = 108 \; \text{km/h} = 30 \; \text{m/s}$. A $1:5$ scale model is built. Find $V_m$ for **dynamic similarity** if the **same air** is used. **Step 1 — Write the equality of Reynolds numbers for similarity.** $$ \text{Re}_m = \text{Re}_{\text{full}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the definition $\text{Re} = VL/\nu$.** $$ \dfrac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \dfrac{V_{\text{full}}\,L_{\text{full}}}{\nu_{\text{full}}} $$ **Step 3 — Since the same fluid is used, $\nu_m = \nu_{\text{full}}$. Cancel them.** $$ V_m L_m = V_{\text{full}}\,L_{\text{full}} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $V_m$.** $$ V_m = V_{\text{full}} \cdot \dfrac{L_{\text{full}}}{L_m} $$ **Step 5 — Insert numbers. $L_m = L_{\text{full}}/5$.** $$ V_m = 30 \cdot \dfrac{4.5}{0.9} = 30 \cdot 5 = 150 \; \text{m/s} = 540 \; \text{km/h} $$ **Step 6 — Check the Mach number.** $$ M_m = \dfrac{V_m}{a} = \dfrac{150}{340.3} \approx 0.44 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical Caveat:** $M_m = 0.44$ is in the **transonic regime**, where compressibility effects (which do NOT exist for the real car at $M \approx 0.09$) become important. **Dynamic similarity is therefore violated.** This is precisely why full-scale testing is preferred for road vehicles whenever possible. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Solid-Blockage Correction (Maskell's Approach) **Physics:** When a model sits in a finite test section, the streamlines are squeezed around it, locally accelerating the flow. The measured $C_D$ therefore appears **higher** than the free-space value. **Step 1 — Define the solid blockage ratio.** The model's frontal area $A_m$ obstructs the test-section area $A_t$: $$ \varepsilon_s = \dfrac{A_m}{A_t} \quad \text{(geometric blockage)} $$ **Step 2 — Account for the drag-induced wake contraction (Maskell).** Maskell showed that the effective area of the blockage is increased by a factor dependent on $C_D$: $$ \varepsilon_s^{\text{eff}} \approx k\,\varepsilon_s \cdot \sqrt{C_D} \quad \text{where } k \approx \mathcal{O}(1) $$ **Step 3 — The corrected free-stream velocity is the apparent test-section velocity multiplied by the blockage factor:** $$ V_{\infty}^{\text{corr}} = V_t (1 + \varepsilon_s^{\text{eff}}) $$ **Step 4 — Substituting into the drag-coefficient formula and using a first-order Taylor expansion $(1 + \varepsilon_s)^{-2} \approx 1 - 2\varepsilon_s$ gives:** $$ C_D^{\text{corr}} \approx C_D^{\text{meas}} - 2\,\varepsilon_s $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** $\boxed{\,C_D^{\text{corr}} = C_D^{\text{meas}} - 2\,\varepsilon_s\,}$ for solid blockage alone. > Combined with wake blockage $\varepsilon_w$, the full Maskell correction is: > $$ \boxed{\,C_D^{\text{corr}} = C_D^{\text{meas}} - 2(\varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_w)\,} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Fan Power Requirement **Step 1 — Volume flow rate through the test section.** $$ Q = A_t \cdot V_t $$ **Step 2 — Pressure rise across the fan equals total losses (diffuser, contraction, corner vanes, model drag):** $$ \Delta p_{\text{fan}} = \sum \text{losses} $$ **Step 3 — Hydraulic (shaft) power required.** $$ P_{\text{fan}} = \dfrac{\Delta p_{\text{fan}}\,Q}{\eta_{\text{fan}}} $$ **Step 4 — Combining $Q$ and $\Delta p$ in terms of test-section velocity:** $$ \boxed{\,P_{\text{fan}} = \dfrac{\Delta p_{\text{fan}} \cdot A_t \cdot V_t}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}\,} $$ For a typical automotive tunnel running at $V_t = 50 \; \text{m/s}$ with $A_t = 20 \; \text{m}^{2}$, $\Delta p = 1500 \; \text{Pa}$ and $\eta = 0.85$: $$ P_{\text{fan}} \approx \dfrac{1500 \times 20 \times 50}{0.85} \approx 1.76 \; \text{MW} $$ This illustrates why full-scale automotive wind tunnels consume **megawatts** of electrical power. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem (3-step):** A sedan with frontal area $A_f = 2.2 \; \text{m}^{2}$ and wheelbase $L = 2.7 \; \text{m}$ is tested in a subsonic full-scale wind tunnel at $V = 130 \; \text{km/h}$. The drag force on the balance reads $F_x = 380 \; \text{N}$. Take $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$. **(a) Calculate the drag coefficient.** Convert velocity: $$ V = \dfrac{130}{3.6} = 36.11 \; \text{m/s} $$ Dynamic pressure: $$ q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(36.11)^{2} = 798.7 \; \text{Pa} $$ Drag coefficient: $$ C_D = \dfrac{F_x}{q_{\infty}\,A_f} = \dfrac{380}{798.7 \times 2.2} = 0.216 $$ **(b) Find the power needed to overcome aerodynamic drag at this speed.** $$ P_{\text{drag}} = F_x \cdot V = 380 \times 36.11 = 13.72 \; \text{kW} $$ **(c) If $V$ is increased to 180 km/h, what is the new $F_x$ (assume $C_D$ constant)?** $$ V_2 = \dfrac{180}{3.6} = 50 \; \text{m/s} $$ $$ q_{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(50)^{2} = 1531.25 \; \text{Pa} $$ $$ F_{x,2} = C_D \cdot q_2 \cdot A_f = 0.216 \times 1531.25 \times 2.2 = 727.7 \; \text{N} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** Doubling speed raises $q$ by factor of 4, hence force by factor of 4. $380 \times 4 = 1520$ N would be naïve; here it grew only ≈ 1.92× because speed went from 130 → 180 km/h, not from 90 → 180 km/h. Use the *exact* ratio: > $$ \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{V_1^{2}} = \left(\dfrac{50}{36.11}\right)^{2} = 1.917 $$ **Bonus (KTU examiner favourite):** Verify Reynolds-number independence. $$ \text{Re}_L = \dfrac{VL}{\nu} = \dfrac{36.11 \times 2.7}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.68 \times 10^{6} $$ This is in the **post-critical (turbulent) regime**, where $C_D$ is approximately constant — justifying the assumption in part (c). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Closed-Circuit Subsonic Automotive Wind Tunnel ```mermaid flowchart LR inletA[Inlet Bellmouth] --> condB[Flow Conditioner
Honeycomb plus Screens] condB --> settleC[Settling Chamber] settleC --> contractD[Contraction Nozzle
Area Ratio 9 to 1] contractD --> testE[Test Section
Vehicle on Balance] testE --> diffuserF[Diffuser
3 to 5 Degree Half Angle] diffuserF --> driveG[Drive Fan
Variable Pitch Blades] driveG --> returnH[Return Loop
Corner Turning Vanes] returnH --> inletA testE --> balanceJ[Six Component
Force Balance] testE --> pressK[Pressure Taps
Pitot Static Probes] testE --> visualL[Flow Visualization
Tufts Smoke Oil] testE --> yawM[Yaw Turntable
Plus or Minus 30 deg] testE --> groundN[Ground Simulation
Moving Belt plus Suction] ``` ## 4.2 Ground-Simulation System Architecture (Five-Belt Configuration) ```mermaid flowchart TB vehicleA[Vehicle Underbody
Boundary Layer] --> frontB[Front Center Belt] frontB --> vehC[Test Vehicle
Force Balance] vehC --> rearD[Rear Center Belt] rearD --> sideE[Left Side Belt] sideE --> sideF[Right Side Belt] sideF --> plenumG[Underbody Plenum
Suction Slot] plenumG --> tunnelH[Main Tunnel Loop] vehC --> pitotI[Pitot Probe
above Car] vehC --> balanceJ[Wheel Force Balance
Slip Ring Type] plenumG --> suctionK[Blower plus
Flow Control Valve] ``` ## 4.3 Measurement-System Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB sensorA[Pitot Static Probes] --> daqA[Pressure Scanner
64 Channel] sensorB[Surface Pressure Taps] --> daqB[Multiplexer Pressure Module] sensorC[Force Balance Strain Gauges] --> daqC[Bridge Amplifier] sensorD[Wheel Torque Meter] --> daqC sensorE[Hot Wire Anemometer] --> daqD[Anemometer Bridge] daqA --> procA[Data Acquisition
PC based DAS] daqB --> procA daqC --> procA daqD --> procA procA --> calA[Calibration Matrix
Cross Coupling Removal] calA --> outputA[Forces Moments
Coefficients Cp Distribution] procA --> visualA[Flow Visualisation
Software Overlay] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram of a Typical Test Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA[Step 1 Vehicle Prep
Wheels aligned Tyre placard] --> stepB[Step 2 Boundary Layer
Check at Empty Tunnel] stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Mount on Balance
Zero Tare] stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Set Velocity
Sweep 30 to 60 m per s] stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Record Forces
At each Setpoint] stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Apply Yaw
Minus 20 to Plus 20 deg] stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Blockage and
Buoyancy Corrections] stepG --> stepH[Step 8 Final C_D C_L
C_M Curves] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** Each box is a *functional block*, not a literal drawing. In the KTU board exam, you may reproduce a simplified sketch of any one of these diagrams and earn full marks if your labelling is correct. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q1a]` **"Define the term 'wind tunnel' as applied to automotive testing. List any four essential components."** **Model Answer (≈ 90 words):** > An automotive wind tunnel is a controlled-flow facility in which a stationary vehicle or its scale model is exposed to a uniform airstream of known velocity, density and turbulence intensity to measure aerodynamic forces, moments and flow features. > > Four essential components are: > 1. **Contraction nozzle** — accelerates and uniformises the flow. > 2. **Test section** — houses the vehicle. > 3. **Force balance** — measures six-component forces and moments. > 4. **Diffuser & drive fan** — recover pressure and circulate the air. *(Examiner's note: A neat sketch with labelled parts earns bonus impression even in a 3-mark item.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Q1b]` **"Differentiate between open-circuit and closed-circuit wind tunnels. State one advantage of each."** **Model Answer:** | Criterion | Open-Circuit (Eiffel) | Closed-Circuit (Göttingen) | |---|---|---| | Air-flow path | Single pass, dumped to atmosphere | Continuous recirculation | | Cost & footprint | Low | High | | Flow uniformity | Modest | Excellent (Tu < 0.1 %) | | Environmental control | Poor (affected by ambient) | Excellent (controlled T, ρ) | | Noise level | High | Low | - *Open-circuit advantage:* Simple construction, low capital cost, easy vehicle access. - *Closed-circuit advantage:* Superior flow quality and constant test conditions independent of ambient weather. --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice) --- ### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q2]` **(a) With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel used for full-scale automotive testing. List any six major components and their functions.** **[7 Marks]** **Solution:** A closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel recirculates air through a closed loop, allowing precise control over velocity, temperature and turbulence intensity. The drive fan (typically a variable-pitch axial fan) draws air from the return loop and forces it through a series of conditioning elements before it enters the test section where the vehicle is mounted on a six-component force balance. After passing the model, the air is decelerated in a diffuser that recovers static pressure, then routed through corner turning vanes back to the inlet. **Six major components and their functions:** | Component | Function | |---|---| | 1. Inlet bellmouth | Smooth, loss-free entry of air into the circuit | | 2. Honeycomb + screens | Eliminates swirl and reduces turbulence intensity | | 3. Settling chamber | Provides flow straightening length | | 4. Contraction (9:1) | Accelerates flow to test-section velocity with uniform profile | | 5. Test section | Houses vehicle; mounted on force balance; fitted with moving belt | | 6. Diffuser (3°–5°) | Recovers static pressure, reducing fan power demand | | 7. Drive fan + motor | Provides circulation; variable pitch for speed control | | 8. Corner vanes | Turn flow through 90° with minimum loss | **Neat-sketch description (to be drawn on answer sheet):** Closed rectangular loop drawn in plan view → label air-flow direction → mark (i) bellmouth, (ii) honeycomb, (iii) contraction, (iv) test section (vehicle inside), (v) diffuser, (vi) fan, (vii) corner vanes. --- **(b) A 1:5 scale model of a passenger car is to be tested in a wind tunnel using the same air as the prototype. The full-scale car travels at 120 km/h. Determine the model test velocity required for dynamic similarity. Comment on the practical limitations of this test.** **[7 Marks]** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert full-scale velocity.** $$ V_{\text{full}} = \dfrac{120}{3.6} = 33.33 \; \text{m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Write Reynolds-number equality for dynamic similarity.** $$ \dfrac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \dfrac{V_{\text{full}} L_{\text{full}}}{\nu_{\text{full}}} $$ **Step 3 — Same fluid ⇒ $\nu_m = \nu_{\text{full}}$. Cancel.** $$ V_m L_m = V_{\text{full}} L_{\text{full}} $$ **Step 4 — With $L_m = L_{\text{full}}/5$.** $$ V_m = V_{\text{full}} \cdot \dfrac{L_{\text{full}}}{L_m} = 33.33 \times 5 = 166.67 \; \text{m/s} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to km/h.** $$ V_m = 166.67 \times 3.6 \approx 600 \; \text{km/h} $$ **Step 6 — Check Mach number.** $$ M_m = \dfrac{V_m}{a} = \dfrac{166.67}{340.3} \approx 0.49 $$ **Practical limitations:** 1. **Compressibility violation:** $M_m = 0.49$ lies in the transonic regime, where shock formation (absent in the real car at $M_{\text{full}} \approx 0.098$) invalidates dynamic similarity. 2. **Tunnel-power demand:** Achieving $V = 167 \; \text{m/s}$ requires multi-megawatt drive fans — uneconomical. 3. **Boundary-layer transition:** Critical $\text{Re}_{\text{crit}}$ may differ between model and prototype, altering separation behaviour. 4. **Ground simulation:** The model's small ground clearance makes moving-belt fidelity difficult. 5. **Cooling system fidelity:** Engine-cooling airflow at model scale does not replicate full-scale heat-exchanger behaviour. **Conclusion:** For road vehicles, full-scale testing is strongly preferred; scale models are used only for concept studies or when Re-matching is sacrificed and a *calibrated* correction is applied. --- ### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Q3]` **(a) Explain with a sketch the concept of ground simulation in automotive wind tunnels. Compare the "single-belt" and "five-belt" systems.** **[7 Marks]** **Solution:** **Concept of ground simulation:** A real car driving on a road experiences a moving ground beneath its wheels. The boundary layer that forms on the road is ingested under the vehicle, strongly influencing under-hood flow, diffuser performance, and wake structure. In a wind tunnel the vehicle is stationary, so without compensation the ground plane is also stationary, producing a *stationary* boundary layer that does not exist in reality. **Ground simulation** is the technique of replicating the moving-road boundary-layer condition using mechanical belts, suction slots and plenums. **Single-Belt (3-belt) System:** - A wide central belt covers the wheelbase region; two narrow belts cover the wheel tracks. - Driven at the tunnel-air velocity to match road speed. - Simpler, cheaper, but produces a thick boundary layer beneath the floor → over-estimates underbody flow separation. **Five-Belt System:** - One centre belt (wheelbase region) + two front-axle belts + two rear-axle belts. - Plus lateral suction slot ahead of the front wheels to bleed off the leading-edge stagnation boundary layer. - A large underbody plenum removes air ingested at the front, replicating open-road flow. - Higher fidelity; used in modern full-scale facilities (e.g., FKFS Stuttgart, MIRA). **Comparison Table:** | Feature | 3-Belt | 5-Belt | |---|---|---| | Boundary-layer thickness under floor | Larger (less accurate) | Smaller, closer to road | | Cost | Lower | Higher | | Maintenance | Easier | Complex belt tracking | | Application | Concept work | Production-grade full-scale testing | --- **(b) A car with frontal area $A_f = 2.0 \; \text{m}^{2}$ is tested in a full-scale wind tunnel. At $V_1 = 100 \; \text{km/h}$ the drag force is $F_{x,1} = 280 \; \text{N}$. At $V_2 = 150 \; \text{km/h}$ the drag force is $F_{x,2} = 612 \; \text{N}$. Calculate the drag coefficient at each speed, comment on whether $C_D$ is constant, and compute the power required to overcome drag at $V_2$. Take $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$.** **[7 Marks]** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert velocities to SI.** $$ V_1 = \dfrac{100}{3.6} = 27.78 \; \text{m/s}, \quad V_2 = \dfrac{150}{3.6} = 41.67 \; \text{m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Dynamic pressures.** $$ q_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(27.78)^2 = 472.4 \; \text{Pa} $$ $$ q_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(41.67)^2 = 1063.0 \; \text{Pa} $$ **Step 3 — Drag coefficients.** $$ C_{D,1} = \dfrac{F_{x,1}}{q_1\,A_f} = \dfrac{280}{472.4 \times 2.0} = 0.296 $$ $$ C_{D,2} = \dfrac{F_{x,2}}{q_2\,A_f} = \dfrac{612}{1063.0 \times 2.0} = 0.288 $$ **Step 4 — Comment on constancy.** $$ \dfrac{C_{D,2}}{C_{D,1}} = \dfrac{0.288}{0.296} \approx 0.973 $$ $$ \Delta C_D \approx -2.7\% $$ Within typical KTU experimental uncertainty (≈ 1–3 %), $C_D$ is essentially **constant**, confirming that the flow is in the *post-critical turbulent regime* — a common assumption for production cars. **Step 5 — Power at $V_2$.** $$ P_{\text{drag},2} = F_{x,2} \cdot V_2 = 612 \times 41.67 = 25{,}503 \; \text{W} \approx 25.5 \; \text{kW} $$ Alternative check using $C_D$: $$ P_{\text{drag},2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{3} C_{D,2} A_f = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(41.67)^3 (0.288)(2.0) \approx 25.5 \; \text{kW} \;\checkmark $$ --- ## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on Wind-Tunnel questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s** in numerical problems. (3-mark question – full marks lost.) > 2. **Stating Reynolds-number similarity without checking Mach similarity.** For cars, $M < 0.3$ is mandatory. Mention it explicitly. > 3. **Ignoring blockage correction.** When a small model occupies > 5 % of the test-section area, examiners *will* deduct marks if you do not mention Maskell's correction. > 4. **Drawing flow direction wrongly** on the closed-loop sketch (clockwise vs anti-clockwise). Be consistent. > 5. **Stating $C_D$ without specifying reference area.** Always write: "$C_D$ based on frontal area $A_f$". > 6. **Mixing up $\mu$ and $\nu$.** Use $\nu$ when $V$ is in m/s and $L$ in m, because $\text{Re} = VL/\nu$ is dimensionless. > 7. **Neglecting boundary-layer transition.** Real cars often have partial laminar–turbulent transition; mention it for full marks. > 8. **Confusing lift and downforce.** $C_L > 0$ = lift (unwanted); $C_L < 0$ = downforce (desired for sports cars). State sign conventions. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **Wind tunnel = controlled-airflow facility** for stationary vehicle or model testing; primary purpose is $C_D$, $C_L$, $C_M$, and flow-visualisation data. - **Closed-circuit > Open-circuit** in flow quality; closed tunnels are the industry standard. - **Reference area for cars = frontal area $A_f$** (projected area in the y–z plane). - **Reference length = wheelbase $L$** (sometimes used for $\text{Re}_L$). - **Canonical drag equation:** $C_D = F_x / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f)$. - **Power lost to drag:** $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^3 C_D A_f \propto V^3$ (the cubic in $V$ is why fuel economy plummets above 80 km/h). - **Blockage corrections are mandatory** for models occupying > 5 % of test-section area; use Maskell's formula $C_D^{\text{corr}} = C_D^{\text{meas}} - 2(\varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_w)$. - **Re-matching for scale models:** $V_m = V_{\text{full}} \cdot (L_{\text{full}}/L_m)$ with same fluid; check $M < 0.3$. - **Ground simulation:** 5-belt + suction + plenum is the modern benchmark; without it, underbody flow is misrepresented. - **Six-component balance:** measures $F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z$. - **Flow visualisation tools:** tufts, smoke, oil-film, laser-Doppler, PIV. - **Wind-tunnel power:** megawatts for full-scale facilities ($V_t \approx 50$ m/s, $A_t \approx 20$ m²). - **Critical Reynolds number** for car-like bodies: $\text{Re}_{\text{crit}} \approx 3 \times 10^{5}$ (boundary-layer transition). - **Standard sea-level air:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. - **Always cite frontal area, fluid density, and velocity in SI units** in your final numerical answer. - **Typical modern sedan $C_D$:** 0.24–0.30; sports car: 0.28–0.35; heavy truck: 0.55–0.75.

topics.title: Principles of Wind Tunnel Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT753 - AUTOMOTIVE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Wind Tunnel Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Wind Tunnel? (KTU Board-Standard Definition)

A **Wind Tunnel** is a specially designed enclosed duct system in which a working fluid (almost always atmospheric air) is forced to flow past a stationary test object (in our context, an **automobile model** or full-scale vehicle) under precisely controlled and measurable conditions. The purpose is to experimentally determine the aerodynamic forces, moments, pressure distributions, and flow-field characteristics acting on the body without the prohibitive cost and danger of free-flight or full-scale road testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Examiner-Approved Wording):**
> *"A wind tunnel is a controlled-testing apparatus that produces a regulated airflow of known velocity, pressure, and turbulence level over a test model, allowing measurement of aerodynamic quantities (lift, drag, side-force, rolling, pitching, yawing moments) using balances and pressure transducers."*

Mathematically, the wind tunnel simulates the **relative motion** between the vehicle and the surrounding air:

$$V_{\text{relative}} = V_{\text{vehicle}} - V_{\text{air}}$$

In road conditions, a car moving at **120 km/h** through still air experiences the same relative flow as a stationary car in a tunnel with air moving at **120 km/h** — this is the **Galilean equivalence** that makes wind tunnels feasible.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Kitchen Fan & Paper Strip" Model

Imagine standing in a **long kitchen corridor** with a powerful exhaust fan at one end pulling air through. If you hold a **paper airplane** (your car) stationary inside that airstream, the air rushing past it creates the same lift on the wings and drag on the fuselage as if the airplane were flying at that speed through the sky.

- The **fan** = the wind tunnel's drive system (motor + propeller/fan)
- The **corridor** = the wind tunnel's settling chamber + contraction + test section
- The **paper airplane** = the full-scale or scale-model vehicle
- The **instruments on your hand** (force, pressure sensors) = the **force balance** and **pressure scanner**

A real automotive wind tunnel is essentially an **industrial-scale, instrumented version of this kitchen-fan concept** — but engineered with extraordinary precision (some tunnels cost upwards of **₹100 crore**, e.g., the **Natrax facility in India**).

## 1.3 Why Wind Tunnels are Indispensable in Automotive Engineering

| Road Test (Real-World) | Wind Tunnel Test |
|---|---|
| Highly variable ambient conditions (wind gusts, temperature) | Precisely controlled flow conditions |
| Cannot isolate individual parameters | One variable at a time can be changed |
| Driver safety risk at high speeds | Safe static testing |
| Expensive and time-consuming per data point | Repeatable, fast, low marginal cost |
| Difficult to measure pressure on body surface | Hundreds of pressure taps easily accessible |
| Cannot visualise flow field directly | Schlieren, PIV, smoke-wire visualization |

## 1.4 Classification of Wind Tunnels — Quick Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification Framework (Memorize This Table):**

| Criterion | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **By Circuit** | Open-circuit (Eiffel / Göttingen type) | Closed-circuit (return-type) |
| **By Speed** | Subsonic (M < 0.3) | Transonic / Supersonic |
| **By Test Section** | Open (free-jet) | Closed (wall-bounded) |
| **By Purpose** | Full-scale automotive | Scale-model R&D |
| **By Pressure** | Atmospheric (blow-down) | Variable density / Vacuum |

For automotive work, **low-speed, closed-circuit, closed-wall test sections** are the industry standard (e.g., **MIRA, Pininfarina, Porsche, Mercedes-Benz, Tata Motors, Mahindra, Maruti Suzuki**).

## 1.5 The Three Golden Requirements of an Ideal Wind Tunnel

1. **Flow Quality:** Uniform, low-turbulence, parallel streamlines at the test section entry.
2. **Geometric Similarity:** The model must be a true geometric replica of the prototype (scaled).
3. **Dynamic Similarity:** Reynolds number (and Mach number, where relevant) of the model test must match the prototype. For automotive flows, this primarily means **Reynolds Number Matching**.

$$Re_{\text{model}} = Re_{\text{prototype}}$$
$$\frac{\rho V L}{\mu}\bigg|_{\text{model}} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}\bigg|_{\text{prototype}}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Components of a Wind Tunnel — Systematic Breakdown

A modern low-speed automotive wind tunnel consists of **8 functional subsystems**. Understanding each is essential for KTU's Part-B questions.

### (i) Contraction Nozzle (or Convergent Section)
- A smoothly converging duct that accelerates flow and reduces turbulence intensity.
- Shape: **3rd-order to 5th-order polynomial profile** or **Witozinsky / cubic profile**.
- Contraction Ratio (CR):

$$CR = \frac{A_{\text{settling chamber}}}{A_{\text{test section}}}$$

**Typical automotive tunnel CR = 6 : 1 to 10 : 1**

- Higher CR ⇒ lower turbulence, but larger physical length.

### (ii) Test Section
- The straight, constant-area working section where the model is mounted.
- Length: typically **2.5 to 3 times** the model length to allow flow establishment.
- Cross-section: rectangular or elliptical; **floor** (with or without moving belt) for ground simulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ground Simulation Belt:** Modern automotive tunnels use a **moving-belt floor** moving at the same velocity as the oncoming air to simulate the relative motion between the road and the tyres — this is called the **5-belt system** or **flat-belt system**. Without it, the boundary layer under the car is grossly incorrect.

### (iii) Diffuser
- A gradually expanding duct downstream of the test section.
- Reduces velocity ⇒ recovers **static pressure** (kinetic energy recovery).
- Half-angle typically **2.5° to 5°** to prevent flow separation.
- Pressure recovery ratio:

$$C_p = \frac{P_{\text{test section}} - P_{\text{diffuser exit}}}{P_{\text{total upstream}}}$$

### (iv) Drive System (Fan + Motor)
- **Axial-flow fans** (multi-stage) or **centrifugal blowers**.
- Power requirement (KTU favourite derivation — see Section 3):

$$P_{\text{fan}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_{\text{test}} V_{\text{test}}^3 \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$$

where $\eta_{\text{fan}}$ is fan efficiency (typically **0.7 to 0.85**).

### (v) Corner Vanes (Turning Vanes)
- Installed in the four corners of a closed-circuit tunnel.
- Redirect flow by 90° with minimal loss.
- Typically **circular-arc or airfoil-shaped** profiles.

### (vi) Settling Chamber & Honeycomb
- Contains **honeycomb** (cellular flow straightener) and **screens** (anti-turbulence mesh).
- Reduces lateral turbulence intensity from ~5–10% down to **< 0.1%**.
- Each screen imposes a small pressure drop:

$$\Delta P_{\text{screen}} = K \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2, \quad K \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0$$

### (vii) Heat Exchanger
- Maintains air temperature (and hence density, viscosity) at a constant value.
- Critical because $C_D$ measurements are sensitive to **Re drift**.

### (viii) Measuring & Data Acquisition
- Multi-component **force balances** (strain-gauge type).
- **Pressure scanners** (64-, 128-, 256-channel electronic transducers).
- Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV), Laser Doppler Velocimetry (LDV).

## 2.2 Open-Circuit vs Closed-Circuit Tunnels

| Parameter | Open-Circuit (Eiffel) | Closed-Circuit (Return) |
|---|---|---|
| Layout | Suction / blower at one end | Continuous loop with corners |
| Power consumption | Higher (no pressure recovery) | Lower (re-circulation re-uses energy) |
| Flow quality | Moderate | Very high |
| Construction cost | Lower | Higher |
| Noise level | Higher (motor near test section) | Lower |
| Typical users | Universities, light aircraft | Automotive OEMs, aerospace |

## 2.3 Similarity Laws — The Heart of Wind Tunnel Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an automotive model test, **Reynolds Number similarity** is the governing criterion (since the flow is highly incompressible, M < 0.3).

### Reynolds Number:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

### Matching Condition:

$$Re_{\text{model}} = Re_{\text{prototype}}$$
$$V_m L_m = V_p L_p \quad (\text{for same air})$$
$$V_m = V_p \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} = V_p \cdot \lambda^{-1}$$

where $\lambda = L_m / L_p$ is the **scale factor**.

**Example:** A 1:4 scale model of a car ($L_m = L_p/4$) tested at the same $V_p = 120$ km/h would require $V_m = 480$ km/h (impractical!) — so engineers either increase tunnel pressure (CFD-style **pressurised tunnels**) or use a **cryogenic tunnel** (NFoFD concept used by some OEMs).

## 2.4 Force and Moment Measurement — The Balance

The primary measured quantity is **aerodynamic drag**:

$$F_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \cdot C_D$$

where $S$ is frontal area. The balance measures **all six components**:

| Component | Symbol | Vehicle Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Drag (x-axis) | $F_D$ | Aerodynamic Resistance |
| Side-force (y-axis) | $F_S$ | Crosswind |
| Lift (z-axis) | $F_L$ | Downforce / Lift |
| Roll moment (about x) | $M_x$ | – |
| Pitch moment (about y) | $M_y$ | – |
| Yaw moment (about z) | $M_z$ | – |

## 2.5 Wind Tunnel Corrections

> [!NOTE]
> Real wind tunnels are not perfect — finite test-section size, wall boundary layers, support struts all introduce errors. **KTU 2024 examiners love this topic.**

### (a) Blockage Correction (Solid Blockage)
When a model occupies a finite fraction of the test-section cross-section, the **effective flow area decreases**, accelerating the flow artificially:

$$\epsilon_{sb} = \tau \cdot \frac{V_{\text{blocked}}}{V_{\infty}} \cdot \left(\frac{C_{D,\text{measured}}}{S/A_{\text{test}}}\right)^{0.5}$$

A simpler first-order form:

$$C_{D,\text{corrected}} = C_{D,\text{measured}} \left(1 - k \cdot \frac{S_{\text{model}}}{S_{\text{test}}}\right)$$

where $k$ is a constant depending on body shape (typically **1.5 to 3.0**).

### (b) Wall Interference (Boundary Blockage)
The growing boundary layer on the test-section walls effectively reduces the cross-section, similar to solid blockage but distributed.

### (c) Support Strut Interference
The model is held by a **strut, sting, or strut-pylon** from below, which has its own drag and adds a momentum defect.

### (d) Buoyancy Correction
If the air in the test section is slightly warmer or cooler than the surroundings, a net vertical buoyancy force exists.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Table — High-Frequency KTU Question Source.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho V L / \mu = V L / \nu$ | – | Matching parameter |
| 2 | Mach number | $M = V / a$ | – | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ |
| 3 | Drag force | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D$ | N | – |
| 4 | Lift force | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$ | N | – |
| 5 | Dynamic pressure | $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Pa | – |
| 6 | Fan power | $P = q_\infty A V / \eta_f$ | W | – |
| 7 | Contraction ratio | $CR = A_s / A_t$ | – | $A_s$ settling, $A_t$ test |
| 8 | Turbulence intensity | $Tu = u' / V$ | % | $< 0.1\%$ ideal |
| 9 | Blockage | $\epsilon_{sb}$ | % | $< 5\%$ acceptable |
| 10 | Pressure coeff. | $C_p = (p - p_\infty)/q_\infty$ | – | – |
| 11 | Screen loss coeff. | $K = \Delta P / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2)$ | – | $K \approx 1.5$–2.0 |
| 12 | Continuity | $\rho V A = \text{const}$ | kg/s | Incompressible |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Drag coefficient calibration:** Modern cars target $C_D \approx 0.22$–$0.30$.
- **Cooling airflow:** Radiator, brake, and battery (EV) inlet design.
- **Wind-noise prediction:** Aero-acoustic wind tunnels (anechoic chambers) measure microphone data.
- **Aero-elastic testing:** Side mirror flutter, antenna resonance.
- **Crosswind sensitivity:** Important for trucks and high-CG vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fan Power Required to Drive the Wind Tunnel

This is a **standard KTU 14-mark derivation**, often asked with subtle variations.

### Setup
Air of density $\rho$ flows steadily through the test section of area $A_t$ at velocity $V_t$. The fan must overcome all losses in the circuit.

### Step 1 — Mass flow rate
By continuity at the test section:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_t \cdot V_t$$

### Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate
By definition:

$$Q = A_t \cdot V_t$$

### Step 3 — Ideal power to impart kinetic energy to the air
The kinetic energy added per unit time equals $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m} V_t^2$:

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V_t^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A_t V_t \cdot V_t^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_t V_t^3$$

### Step 4 — Account for fan efficiency $\eta_f$
The motor must supply more power than the air receives (because the fan itself is not 100% efficient):

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{ideal}}}{\eta_f} = \frac{\rho A_t V_t^3}{2\eta_f}$$

### Step 5 — Final form
Using $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_t^2$ (dynamic pressure):

$$\boxed{P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{q_\infty A_t V_t}{\eta_f}}$$

### Why this matters
For a tunnel with $A_t = 20$ m², $V_t = 50$ m/s (≈180 km/h), $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\eta_f = 0.8$:

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{0.5 \times 1.225 \times 20 \times 50^3}{0.8} \approx 1.91 \text{ MW}$$

That is **2 MW of electrical power** — explaining why automotive wind tunnels are enormous capital investments.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Number Matching for Scale Models

### Given
Prototype car: length $L_p$, free-stream velocity $V_p$, ambient air.
Scale model: length $L_m = L_p/n$ (i.e., scale ratio $1:n$).

### Step 1 — Match Reynolds number
For the same working fluid (air at same T, P):

$$Re_p = Re_m$$
$$\frac{\rho V_p L_p}{\mu} = \frac{\rho V_m L_m}{\mu}$$

### Step 2 — Simplify (same $\rho$, $\mu$)
$$V_p L_p = V_m L_m$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $V_m$
$$V_m = V_p \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} = V_p \cdot n$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion
$$\boxed{V_m = n \cdot V_p}$$

The model must be tested at **$n$ times** the prototype speed.

### Numerical Example
Prototype car length $L_p = 4.5$ m, road speed $V_p = 30$ m/s (≈108 km/h).
Scale model $1:3$ (so $n = 3$, $L_m = 1.5$ m).

$$V_m = 3 \times 30 = 90 \text{ m/s} = 324 \text{ km/h}$$

This is **impractical** for a 1:3 model — leading to the use of **pressurised** or **cryogenic** tunnels.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Recovery in the Diffuser

### Step 1 — Continuity in the diffuser
$$\rho V_1 A_1 = \rho V_2 A_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

### Step 2 — Bernoulli (no friction assumption first)
$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$

### Step 3 — Static pressure rise in ideal diffuser
$$P_2 - P_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$

### Step 4 — Express in terms of dynamic pressure at test section
$$q_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2$$
$$P_2 - P_1 = q_1 \left(1 - \left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2\right)$$

### Step 5 — Real diffuser with efficiency $\eta_d$
$$\boxed{(P_2 - P_1)_{\text{real}} = \eta_d \cdot q_1 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2\right]}$$

Typically $\eta_d = 0.7$ to $0.9$ depending on diffuser divergence angle and wall friction.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Blockage Ratio & Solid Blockage Correction (Maskell's Method)

### Step 1 — Define blockage ratio
$$\beta = \frac{S_m}{S_t} = \frac{\text{frontal area of model}}{\text{test section area}}$$

### Step 2 — Solid blockage factor (Maskell form)
For a 3-D body of frontal area $S_m$ in a closed test section of area $S_t$:

$$\epsilon_{sb} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{S_m}{C_t \cdot S_t^{0.5}} \cdot \left(\frac{C_D \cdot S_m}{S_t}\right)^{0.5}$$

where $C_t$ is a model-dependent constant.

### Step 3 — Simplified engineering form (linearised)
For low blockage ($\beta < 0.05$):

$$C_{D,\text{corrected}} = C_{D,\text{measured}} \cdot (1 - k\beta)$$

### Step 4 — Typical value
For streamlined automotive bodies, $k \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$.
For bluff bodies (SUV, truck), $k \approx 2.5$ to $3.5$.

### Step 5 — Practical result
$$\boxed{\text{% Error in } C_D \approx 100 \cdot k\beta \approx 100 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.05 = 10\%}$$

Hence the KTU rule: **blockage must be kept below 5%** for acceptable accuracy.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Honeycomb + Screen Turbulence Reduction

### Step 1 — Initial turbulence intensity
Without honeycomb/screen: $Tu_0 \approx 5\%$.

### Step 2 — Effect of a single screen
A screen of mesh $M$ and wire diameter $d$ has a loss coefficient:

$$K = \left(1 - \beta_s\right)^2 + \left(1 - \beta_s^2\right) \cdot F(\text{Re}_{wire})$$

For the simplest engineering estimate:

$$\frac{Tu_{\text{after}}}{Tu_{\text{before}}} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + K}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{n_{\text{screens}}}}$$

### Step 3 — Stacking $n$ identical screens
Each screen multiplies turbulence reduction:

$$Tu_n \approx \frac{Tu_0}{(1+K)^{n/2}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical example
$Tu_0 = 5\%$, $K = 1.6$, $n = 2$:

$$Tu_2 = \frac{0.05}{(1+1.6)^{2/2}} = \frac{0.05}{2.6} \approx 1.92\%$$

Adding a honeycomb before the screens reduces this further to **< 0.1%** — the desired automotive wind tunnel quality.

## 3.6 Summary Matrix — "What Derivation Does KTU Love?"

| Derivation Topic | Frequency | Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Fan power | High | 5–7 |
| Reynolds number matching | Very High | 5–7 |
| Pressure recovery in diffuser | Medium | 4–5 |
| Blockage correction | High | 5–6 |
| Screen/honeycomb effect | Low–Medium | 3–4 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Circuit Wind Tunnel Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Intake Section] --> B[Settling Chamber]
    B --> C[Honeycomb Flow Straightener]
    C --> D[Anti Turbulence Screens]
    D --> E[Contraction Nozzle]
    E --> F[Test Section with Model and Balance]
    F --> G[Diffuser]
    G --> H[Corner Vanes Turn 1]
    H --> I[Fan and Drive Motor]
    I --> J[Corner Vanes Turn 2]
    J --> K[Heat Exchanger]
    K --> L[Corner Vanes Turn 3]
    L --> M[Corner Vanes Turn 4]
    M --> A
    F --> N[Data Acquisition and Force Pressure System]
    N --> O[Computer and Visualization]
```


## 4.5 Decision Tree — Choosing the Correct Wind Tunnel Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need Full Scale Vehicle Test?} -->|Yes| A1[Closed Circuit Full Scale Tunnel]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{High Re Matching Required?}

    Q2 -->|No Standard Re| Q3{High Pressure Recovery Needed?}

```
