```mermaid
    K -- Yes --> M[Post Processing: Forces, Streamlines, Cp]
    M --> N[Correlation with Wind Tunnel Data]
    N --> O{Acceptable Match?}
    O -- No --> P[Refine Mesh or Turbulence Model]
    P --> E
    O -- Yes --> Q[Design Sign Off for Manufacture]
    Q --> R[Track Testing and Validation]
```

**Workflow Description:**
- **A → B**: STL or Parasolid files are imported and de-featured (removing bolts, small fillets that do not affect flow).
- **C**: Domain size typically extends 10L upstream, 20L downstream, 5L laterally.
- **D–F**: Surface mesh ~2–5 mm; volume mesh ~5–10 mm near body, growing to 50–100 mm at far-field; prism layers ensure $y^+ \leq 1$.
- **G**: Inlet = velocity, Outlet = pressure, Car body = no-slip wall, Ground = moving wall (velocity = car speed), Wheels = rotating reference frame.
- **H**: Solver choice depends on accuracy needs vs. computational budget.
- **J**: Residuals drop 4–6 orders of magnitude for force convergence.
- **N–P**: CFD results are correlated with wind tunnel using standard FIA correlation factors.

### 4.2 F1 Car Aerodynamic Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRONT_END
        FW[Front Wing Assembly]
        FEND[F-End Plates]
        FBIB[Front Barge Boards and Bib]
    end
    subgraph MIDDLE
        SF[Side Pod Undercut]
        FENDER[Barge Boards]
        SIDEPOD[Side Pod Inlets]
    end
    subgraph REAR
        FLOOR[Underfloor and Venturi Tunnels]
        DIFF[Diffuser]
        RW[Rear Wing]
        RWEND[Rear Wing End Plates]
        BEAM[Beam Wing]
        DRS[DRS Actuator]
    end
    FW --> FLOOR
    FEND --> SIDEPOD
    FBIB --> FLOOR
    SF --> FLOOR
    FENDER --> FLOOR
    SIDEPOD --> FLOOR
    FLOOR --> DIFF
    DIFF --> RW
    RW --> BEAM
    DRS --> RW
    RWEND --> DIFF
```

**Component-by-Component Aerodynamic Function:**

1. **Front Wing (FW)** — Generates ~25–30% of total downforce; also conditions flow toward the rest of the car. The **Y-250 vortex** sheds off the inboard section and energizes the floor.
2. **F-End Plates and Bargeboards** — Direct tire wake away from the floor and sidepod undercut.
3. **Sidepod Inlets** — Feed cooling air to radiators; also shape side-pod pressure field.
4. **Underfloor and Venturi Tunnels (2022+ regs)** — Generate **ground-effect downforce** via low-pressure suction; can contribute 40–50% of total downforce.
5. **Diffuser** — Expands flow exiting the floor, accelerating it and reducing pressure beneath the car.
6. **Rear Wing (RW)** — Generates the highest local downforce at the rear axle.
7. **Beam Wing** — Smaller horizontal element below the main plane; balances drag-downforce tradeoff.
8. **DRS (Drag Reduction System)** — Flattens the rear wing's upper element to reduce drag by ~10–15% on straights, opening a 10–15 km/h speed advantage.

### 4.3 Y-Plus Mesh Quality Reference

| Region | Target $y^+$ | Layer Count | First Cell Height |
|---|---|---|---|
| Body panels (resolved BL) | $\leq 1$ | 15–25 | ~ 0.01 mm |
| Wheels and tires | $\leq 1$ | 12–20 | ~ 0.02 mm |
| Floor and diffuser | $\leq 1$ | 20–30 | ~ 0.008 mm |
| Wing elements (resolved BL) | $\leq 1$ | 18–25 | ~ 0.01 mm |
| Far-field (skip BL inflation) | n/a | 0 | n/a |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). List the three main discretization techniques used in CFD for solving the Navier–Stokes equations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) is a branch of fluid mechanics that uses numerical methods and algorithms to solve and analyze problems involving fluid flows. Computers are used to perform the calculations required to simulate the free-stream flow of liquids and gases, and their interaction with surfaces defined by boundary conditions. For automotive applications, CFD predicts pressure distribution, velocity fields, drag, lift, and other aerodynamic quantities on vehicles.

**Three Discretization Techniques (1 mark):**
1. **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** — Approximates derivatives with Taylor series expansions.
2. **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** — Conserves flux across cell faces; industry standard for F1.
3. **Finite Element Method (FEM)** — Uses variational/Galerkin formulations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the Reynolds number? Why is its value significant for F1 racing car aerodynamics?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
The Reynolds number is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow. It is defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \frac{U L}{\nu}$$

where $U$ is the free-stream velocity, $L$ is the characteristic length, $\rho$ is density, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity.

**Significance for F1 (1.5 marks):**
For an F1 car at top speed (~100 m/s) with a wheelbase of 3.6 m, the Reynolds number is on the order of $Re \sim 2.4 \times 10^7$. Since $Re \gg 5 \times 10^5$, the boundary layer is **fully turbulent** over the entire car. This means:
- CFD solvers **must** use turbulence models (RANS, DES, or LES); direct numerical simulation is impossible.
- Flow separation behavior is dominated by turbulent mixing.
- Wind tunnel tests must be run at the same Reynolds number for accurate correlation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the formula and forget the **physical interpretation**. Marks are split as 1.5 (definition + formula) and 1.5 (F1 significance). Skipping the F1 context costs 50% of the marks.

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** *Derive the second-order central difference approximation for the second spatial derivative $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$ using Taylor series expansion. State the order of accuracy. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the FVM (Finite Volume Method) approach used in CFD with a neat sketch. How is conservation ensured at the cell faces? Mention its advantages over FDM for F1 aerodynamic simulations. (7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1: Taylor Series Expansion Forward**

We expand $u$ at node $i+1$ around node $i$:

$$u_{i+1} = u_i + \Delta x \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2!} \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{3!} \left( \frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3} \right)_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{4!} \left( \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} \right)_i + \dots$$

**Step 2: Taylor Series Expansion Backward**

Similarly, expanding $u$ at node $i-1$:

$$u_{i-1} = u_i - \Delta x \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2!} \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{3!} \left( \frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3} \right)_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{4!} \left( \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} \right)_i - \dots$$

**Step 3: Adding the Two Expansions**

When we add the two series, all **odd** derivatives cancel out:

$$u_{i+1} + u_{i-1} = 2 u_i + (\Delta x)^2 \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{12} \left( \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} \right)_i + \dots$$

**Step 4: Isolating the Second Derivative**

Rearranging:

$$\left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i = \frac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2} - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{12} \left( \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} \right)_i + \dots$$

**Step 5: Truncation Error**

The leading truncation error term is $O((\Delta x)^2)$, hence the approximation is **second-order accurate**:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \approx \frac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2} + O((\Delta x)^2)}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**FVM Concept (3 marks):**
The Finite Volume Method divides the computational domain into a finite number of small control volumes (cells). The governing PDEs (continuity, momentum, energy) are integrated over each control volume. Applying the **divergence theorem**, volume integrals of divergence terms are converted into **surface integrals** of fluxes across cell faces:

$$\int_{V} \nabla \cdot \vec{F} \ dV = \oint_{S} \vec{F} \cdot \hat{n} \ dS$$

This guarantees that the flux leaving one cell equals the flux entering the adjacent cell, ensuring **exact conservation** at the discrete level.

**Sketch of an FVM Cell (2 marks):**
A typical 2D FVM cell shows the centroid (P) surrounded by four neighbors (N, S, E, W) connected via faces where fluxes are computed. The value at the centroid represents the cell-averaged quantity.

**Conservation at Cell Faces (1 mark):**
Flux through the east face of cell P equals the flux through the west face of cell E, ensuring global mass, momentum, and energy conservation throughout the domain.

**Advantages Over FDM for F1 (1 mark):**
- Handles complex F1 geometries (curved wings, slatted floors) easily using arbitrary polyhedral meshes.
- Guaranteed conservation makes force balance (lift + drag + side force) physically meaningful.
- Compatible with unstructured meshes needed for tight packaging around wings and wheels.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.A):** For part (a), students often write the final formula without **deriving** the Taylor expansions — this loses up to 4 of 7 marks. For part (b), do NOT confuse FVM with FEM; FVM uses the **integral conservation form**, not the variational form. Drawing a clear labeled cell diagram earns 2 full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** *With the aid of neat sketches, explain the major aerodynamic components of a modern Formula 1 car (post-2022 regulations) and their functions. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 1:2 scale model of an F1 car is tested in a wind tunnel at 60 m/s. The full-scale car travels at 90 m/s. Calculate the Reynolds number for both cases and comment on dynamic similarity. (Take air properties at standard conditions: $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³, $\mu$ = 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s; characteristic length L = 3.6 m for the full-scale car.) (7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

The following components and their functions must be described (1 mark each + 2 marks for the sketch and coherence):

1. **Front Wing Assembly** — Primary downforce generator at front axle; conditions the wake that flows over the rest of the car. Generates the Y-250 vortex that seals the floor.

2. **Front Bargeboards and Bib** — Directs tire wake away from the floor; the **bib** (undertray) prevents air ingress under the leading edge of the floor.

3. **Sidepods with Undercut** — The undercut creates a low-pressure region that pulls high-energy air under the floor; sidepod shape also manages flow over the rear tires.

4. **Underfloor Venturi Tunnels** — Since 2022, F1 cars use **ground-effect tunnels**; venturi acceleration under the car creates massive suction downforce (40–50% of total).

5. **Diffuser** — Expands the underfloor flow back to atmospheric pressure, recovering energy and preventing flow separation at the rear of the floor.

6. **Rear Wing with DRS** — Generates ~30% of total downforce; the DRS flap opens on straights to reduce drag.

7. **Beam Wing** — Small horizontal element below the rear wing main plane; balances the rear downforce and tunes the diffuser exit flow.

8. **Wheel Covers and Wheel Wake Control** — Reduce the energy of turbulent wakes shed from rotating tires, which otherwise destroys downstream downforce.

A labeled side-view sketch of an F1 car showing the locations of all these components should accompany the answer.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Full scale: $U_1 = 90$ m/s, $L_1 = 3.6$ m
- Model scale: $U_2 = 60$ m/s, $L_2 = 1.8$ m (since 1:2 scale)
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s

**Step 1: Kinematic Viscosity**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{1.81 \times 10^{-5}}{1.225} = 1.478 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Reynolds Number — Full Scale**

$$Re_1 = \frac{U_1 L_1}{\nu} = \frac{90 \times 3.6}{1.478 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{324}{1.478 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.193 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 3: Reynolds Number — Model**

$$Re_2 = \frac{U_2 L_2}{\nu} = \frac{60 \times 1.8}{1.478 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{108}{1.478 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.308 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 4: Ratio of Reynolds Numbers**

$$\frac{Re_1}{Re_2} = \frac{2.193 \times 10^7}{7.308 \times 10^6} = 3.00$$

So $Re_1 = 3 \times Re_2$.

**Step 5: Comment on Dynamic Similarity**

For **dynamic similarity** between the model and the prototype, the Reynolds numbers must be equal: $Re_1 = Re_2$. Since $Re_1 = 3 \times Re_2$, dynamic similarity is **NOT** achieved. The model experiences a proportionally lower Reynolds number, which means:
- The boundary layer on the model is **relatively thicker**.
- Flow separation behavior will differ.
- Pressure distribution and forces will not scale linearly.

In F1 wind tunnel testing, this discrepancy is corrected using **boundary layer tripping** (forcing transition to turbulence using dots or zigzag tape) and applying the FIA-mandated **Reynolds number correlation factor** to CFD results.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.B):** Many students compute only $Re_1$ and forget $Re_2$, then can't comment on dynamic similarity — losing 4+ marks. The conclusion must explicitly state **whether** similarity is achieved and the **engineering consequence**. Always show units in your intermediate steps.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **CFD** = numerical simulation of fluid flow; core of modern F1 aerodynamic development.
- **Three discretization methods**: FDM (simple, structured grids), FVM (conservative, industry standard), FEM (variational, more common in structures).
- **Governing equations**: Continuity ($\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$) + Navier–Stokes (momentum conservation).
- **Key dimensionless numbers** in F1 aerodynamics: $Re \sim 10^6$–$10^7$, $M \sim 0.26$–$0.30$, $C_L \sim 3$–$4$, $C_D \sim 0.7$–$1.0$ (DRS closed vs. open).
- **FTCS scheme** for 1D convection-diffusion: $u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - C(u_i^n - u_{i-1}^n) + d(u_{i+1}^n - 2u_i^n + u_{i-1}^n)$, where $C = c \Delta t / \Delta x$ (Courant) and $d = \nu \Delta t / (\Delta x)^2$ (diffusion number).
- **Courant condition** for stability: $C \leq 1$ (convective CFL).
- **Second-order central difference** for $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$: $\frac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2}$ with truncation error $O((\Delta x)^2)$.
- **Turbulence models**: RANS (cheapest, time-averaged), DES (industry standard for F1), LES (most accurate, most expensive).
- **F1 aero components**: Front wing, bargeboards, sidepods, underfloor venturi tunnels, diffuser, rear wing, DRS, beam wing.
- **2022 reg revolution**: Return of **ground-effect** floors generating ~40–50% of total downforce.
- **Wind tunnel–CFD correlation**: FIA mandates cross-validation; Reynolds number mismatch must be addressed via tripping and correlation factors.
- **Wall resolution**: Aim for $y^+ \leq 1$ with prism layers for accurate boundary layer prediction.
- **Standard air properties at sea level**: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $a = 343$ m/s.
- **CFD workflow**: Geometry → Mesh → Boundary Conditions → Solve → Post-process → Validate → Iterate.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **CFD = "Cure For Delay"** (in F1 development cycles).
> - **FVM = "Flux over Volume Method"** (conservation is the key).
> - **CFL = "Can't Flow too Large"** (keep Courant ≤ 1).
> - **$Re$ high → turbulent → use RANS/DES, never DNS**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sports Car
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT753 - AUTOMOTIVE AERODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sports Car # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sports Car (Aerodynamic Definition):** A *sports car* is a high-performance, two-seater (or 2+2) passenger automobile specifically engineered for superior dynamic performance, characterized by a low ride-height, high power-to-weight ratio (typically > 150 kW/tonne), and a body shape optimized through **aerodynamic sculpting** to simultaneously minimize **drag** while maximizing **downforce** ($C_L$) — a combination distinct from sedan/hatchback vehicles where comfort and fuel economy dominate the design brief. In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, a sports car is treated as a **rolling aerodynamic testbed** — the same physics that govern a Formula 1 (F1) car govern it, but the constraints are relaxed (street legality, comfort, manufacturability). Sports cars occupy the aerodynamic regime known as **performance-class ground vehicles**, with typical parameters: | Parameter | Typical Sports Car Range | |---|---| | Drag Coefficient ($C_D$) | **0.28 – 0.38** | | Lift Coefficient ($C_L$, inverted, i.e., downforce) | **0.20 – 1.20** (race-spec) | | Frontal Area ($A$) | **1.8 – 2.2 m²** | | Top Speed ($V_{max}$) | **280 – 440 km/h** | | 0–100 km/h Time | **2.5 – 3.8 s** | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Peregrine Falcon vs. The Albatross > [!NOTE] > **Think of a sports car like a peregrine falcon in a nose-dive:** It is built for *vertical force generation* (downforce) at the cost of some forward efficiency. A family sedan is the *albatross* — it glides for fuel economy with minimal downward force. The peregrine falcon strikes prey at > 320 km/h with astonishing control — its feathers generate enormous *lift* (in flight) or *force* into the dive without tumbling. A sports car does the same with its rear wing, diffuser, and underbody venturi tunnels: it forces the car *into* the road, multiplying tire grip exactly when the falcon's feathers multiply its dive precision. A road car, by contrast, ignores this entirely because tires on a road do not break traction at 120 km/h. ## 1.3 Why Sports Cars Are Aerodynamically Unique Unlike a commercial vehicle, a sports car is designed around **four aerodynamic priorities**, ranked in KTU 2024 syllabus order: 1. **Downforce generation** ($C_L > 0$ acting *downward*) — to push tires into the asphalt. 2. **Drag minimization** ($C_D$ as low as practical) — to maximize top speed and acceleration. 3. **Aerodynamic balance** (front:rear downforce ratio ≈ 40:60 to 50:50) — for high-speed stability. 4. **Cooling airflow management** — for brakes, engine, and (in hybrid/electric) battery packs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The "downforce vs. drag" trade-off is the **central dilemma** in sports car aerodynamics. Every additional Newton of downforce costs roughly **3–5 Newtons of extra drag** in poorly designed systems; a master-class designer (Adrian Newey, Gordon Murray) drives this ratio toward **L/D > 3.5** (lift-to-drag, where lift here is *downforce*). ## 1.4 Standard Reference Constants Used Throughout The following constants appear in every sports-car aerodynamic calculation and must be memorized: - **Air density at sea level, ISA:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Kinematic viscosity of air:** $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ - **Speed of sound at sea level:** $a = 340.3 \text{ m/s}$ - **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Reference frontal area convention:** $A$ measured as the orthogonal projection of the car onto a vertical plane perpendicular to the direction of travel. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Forces That Define a Sports Car A moving sports car experiences two aerodynamic forces that dominate its performance envelope: ### 2.1.1 Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_D$) The **drag force** is the component of aerodynamic force parallel to the freestream velocity. It opposes motion and consumes engine power. $$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_D\,A$$ **Logic step-by-step:** - $\rho$ → how much "air mass" the car must shove aside per second. - $V^2$ → kinetic energy flux scales with velocity squared (this is why a Bugatti Chiron needs **1,600 kW** at 420 km/h but only **~75 kW** at 100 km/h). - $C_D$ → body-shape efficiency (a magic number determined by wind-tunnel/CFD testing). - $A$ → frontal cross-section (smaller is better, but constrained by cabin space). ### 2.1.2 Aerodynamic Downforce (Negative Lift) ($F_L$) The **downforce** is the *downward* component of aerodynamic force. By convention in automotive work, a **positive lift coefficient** is reported but interpreted as **downforce** when the resultant vector points *into* the road. $$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_L\,A$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign convention clarification (KTU standard):** In aerospace, $+C_L$ = upward lift. In automotive, engineers report $C_L$ as a positive number when the force pushes the car **downward** (i.e., "negative lift" in aerospace terms). Always read the problem statement carefully — KTU questions occasionally flip this. ### 2.1.3 The Power Penalty of Drag To maintain a steady velocity $V$, the engine must continuously supply power to overcome drag: $$P_D = F_D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{3}\,C_D\,A$$ **Why this matters:** Drag power scales with the **cube of velocity**. Doubling the speed requires **eight times** the engine power purely for aero — which is why a 200 km/h cruise is "easy" but a 400 km/h top speed is "exponentially expensive." ## 2.2 The Aerodynamic Efficiency Ratio (L/D) The single most quoted metric for any sports car is its **lift-to-drag ratio**, where lift here is interpreted as *downforce*: $$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C_L}{C_D}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** A higher L/D means the car generates more downforce per unit of drag penalty. This is the figure-of-merit used by KTU board questions comparing two cars, or evaluating wing/diffuser designs. | Sports Car Class | Typical $C_L/C_D$ | Comment | |---|---|---| | Grand Tourer (e.g., Aston Martin DB11) | **0.6 – 1.0** | Comfort-biased; low wing | | Mid-engine Supercar (e.g., Ferrari 296 GTB) | **1.5 – 2.5** | Active aero, modest wings | | Track-focused Hypercar (e.g., Aston Valkyrie) | **3.0 – 4.5** | Ground effect + giant wing | | Le Mans Hypercar (LMH class) | **4.0 – 6.0** | Wing + full underbody | | Formula 1 Car (2024 spec) | **~6.5 – 8.0** | Ground-effect floor + DRS | ## 2.3 Ground Effect — The Secret Weapon A sports car with a **flat underbody** and a **rear diffuser** acts like an inverted airfoil between the road and the chassis. Air accelerates under the car (Venturi effect), pressure drops, and the car is **sucked downward**. The simplified ground-effect downforce, treating the underbody as a venturi: $$F_{GE} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,(C_{L,\,\text{wing}} + C_{L,\,\text{floor}})\cdot A_{\text{ref}}$$ For a venturi-shaped underbody: $$\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,(V_{\text{throat}}^{2} - V_{\infty}^{2})$$ where $V_{\text{throat}} > V_{\infty}$ due to the constriction. ## 2.4 Aerodynamic Balance & Slipstream The **aero balance** is the percentage of total downforce acting on the **front axle**: $$\text{Aero Balance}_{F} = \frac{F_{L,\,\text{front}}}{F_{L,\,\text{front}} + F_{L,\,\text{rear}}} \times 100\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** For a sports car to be stable under braking, the front aero balance must *shift forward* as speed increases — this is why many modern sports cars (McLaren, Porsche) use **active rear wings** that flatten at high speed to reduce rear downforce and prevent oversteer. **Slipstream (drafting) factor:** A trailing car experiences reduced drag: $$C_{D,\,\text{trailing}} = C_{D,\,\text{lead}} \cdot (1 - k)$$ where $k \approx 0.25 - 0.40$ at typical F1 following distances (~1 car length). ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_D A$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N | | 2 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_L A$ | Aerodynamic downforce (positive = down) | N | | 3 | $P_D = F_D V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{3} C_D A$ | Power required to overcome drag | W | | 4 | $\frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D}$ | Aerodynamic efficiency ratio | dimensionless | | 5 | $F_{\text{net vertical}} = mg + F_L$ | Effective weight on tires at speed | N | | 6 | $\mu (mg + F_L) = \tfrac{m v^{2}}{R}$ | Maximum cornering speed (cylindrical) | m/s | | 7 | $\text{Aero Balance}_{F} = \frac{F_{L,F}}{F_{L,F} + F_{L,R}}$ | Front downforce fraction | % | | 8 | $a_{\text{lat,\,max}} = \mu g + \frac{\mu F_L}{m}$ | Max lateral acceleration with aero | m/s² | | 9 | $\Delta p_{\text{venturi}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho(V_{\text{throat}}^{2} - V_{\infty}^{2})$ | Underbody pressure drop | Pa | | 10 | $V_{\text{crit,\,stall}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 W}{\rho\, C_{L,\,\text{max}}\, A}}$ | Stall speed of an inverted airfoil | m/s | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **F1 (motorsport):** Every gram of downforce is worth more than every gram of power reduction — that's why teams spend **$15M+ per season** on aero development. - **Hypercar manufacturers (Koenigsegg, Hennessey, Bugatti):** Use active aero + ground effect to balance top-speed runs (low $C_L$) with track mode (max $C_L$). - **Electric sports cars (Rimac Nevera, Pininfarina Battista):** Aero also cools **battery packs** while generating downforce — a unique multi-functional requirement. - **Road car homologation:** A sports car sold to the public must pass pedestrian-safety regulations, so the front wing and splitter are **blunted or breakaway**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Problem A — Downforce and Drag of a Mid-Engine Supercar > **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):** A mid-engine sports car has the following data: > - Mass $m = 1450 \text{ kg}$ > - Frontal area $A = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$ > - Drag coefficient $C_D = 0.32$ > - Downforce coefficient $C_L = 0.45$ > - Air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > > **Calculate:** (i) Drag force at $V = 200 \text{ km/h}$. (ii) Downforce at the same speed. (iii) Aerodynamic efficiency L/D. (iv) Effective vertical load on each tire at this speed (assume 4 equal tires). ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Unit conversion of velocity.** $$V = 200 \text{ km/h} = 200 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 55.56 \text{ m/s}$$ *Conversion logic:* 1 km = 1000 m, 1 h = 3600 s, so 200 km/h = 200·(1000/3600) m/s. **Step 2 — Compute velocity-squared head.** $$V^{2} = (55.56)^{2} = 3086.4 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute dynamic pressure.** $$q = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 3086.4 = 1890.4 \text{ Pa}$$ *Logic:* Dynamic pressure is the "kinetic energy per unit volume" of the oncoming air and is the master variable in all quasi-steady aero forces. **Step 4 — Compute drag force.** $$F_D = q \cdot C_D \cdot A = 1890.4 \times 0.32 \times 2.0 = 1209.9 \text{ N}$$ *Logic:* This is the force the engine must push against at 200 km/h — about the weight of a small motorcycle. **Step 5 — Compute downforce.** $$F_L = q \cdot C_L \cdot A = 1890.4 \times 0.45 \times 2.0 = 1701.4 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6 — Aerodynamic efficiency L/D.** $$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right) = \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{0.45}{0.32} = 1.406$$ *Interpretation:* For every 1 N of drag, the car earns 1.41 N of downforce — a respectable but not exceptional ratio (track-focused cars exceed 4). **Step 7 — Static weight on tires.** $$W = m \cdot g = 1450 \times 9.81 = 14224.5 \text{ N}$$ **Step 8 — Effective (aero + static) vertical load on all 4 tires combined.** $$N_{\text{total}} = W + F_L = 14224.5 + 1701.4 = 15925.9 \text{ N}$$ **Step 9 — Load per tire (4 equal tires).** $$N_{\text{tire}} = \frac{N_{\text{total}}}{4} = \frac{15925.9}{4} = 3981.5 \text{ N} \approx 406 \text{ kgf}$$ *Final answer boxed:* $$\boxed{F_D = 1209.9 \text{ N}, \quad F_L = 1701.4 \text{ N}, \quad L/D = 1.406, \quad N_{\text{tire}} \approx 406 \text{ kgf}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Problem B — Power Required to Reach Top Speed > **Problem Statement:** Using the same car, find the **engine power** required to overcome drag at top speed. Also find how much the engine power grows if the top speed is increased by 25%. ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Recall the drag-power equation.** $$P_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{3}\,C_D\,A$$ **Step 2 — Compute $V^3$ at 200 km/h.** $$V^{3} = (55.56)^{3} = 171{,}489 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{3}$$ **Step 3 — Compute power.** $$P_D = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 171{,}489 \times 0.32 \times 2.0$$ $$P_D = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 171{,}489 \times 0.64 = 67{,}221 \text{ W} \approx 67.2 \text{ kW}$$ *Logic:* This is only ~91 hp — far less than the ~700 hp a real supercar produces. The "extra" power accelerates the car (overcomes inertia and rolling resistance) and pushes it past 200 km/h. **Step 4 — New velocity after 25% increase.** $$V' = 1.25 \times 55.56 = 69.44 \text{ m/s} \quad (\equiv 250 \text{ km/h})$$ **Step 5 — Velocity cubed at new speed.** $$(V')^{3} = (69.44)^{3} = 334{,}897 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{3}$$ **Step 6 — New drag power.** $$P'_D = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 334{,}897 \times 0.32 \times 2.0$$ $$P'_D = 131{,}324 \text{ W} \approx 131.3 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 7 — Compute the ratio.** $$\frac{P'_D}{P_D} = \frac{131.3}{67.2} = 1.953$$ *Logic check:* Since $P \propto V^3$, the theoretical ratio is $(1.25)^3 = 1.953$ — **perfect match**, confirming the cubic law. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** To go 25% faster, the engine must supply **95.3% more power** purely for aero drag. This is why top-speed records (Koenigsegg Jesko Absolut: 531 km/h target) require $C_D < 0.28$ AND frontal area below 2.0 m² AND a flat underbody. --- ## 3.3 Problem C — Maximum Cornering Speed with and without Downforce > **Problem Statement:** The same car (m = 1450 kg) negotiates a flat curve of radius $R = 100 \text{ m}$. Tire-road friction coefficient $\mu = 1.0$ (high-performance semi-slicks). Compute the maximum cornering speed: (a) ignoring downforce, (b) including downforce at $C_L = 0.45$. ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Force balance at the verge of sliding.** The maximum lateral force the tires can supply equals $\mu$ times the normal force. For circular motion: $$\mu\,(mg + F_L) = \frac{m\,V^{2}}{R}$$ *Logic:* The centripetal requirement $\tfrac{mV^2}{R}$ must not exceed the maximum friction $\mu N$. We substitute $N = mg + F_L$ (downforce pushes the car harder into the road, increasing $N$). **Step 2 — Rearrange for $V^2$.** $$V^{2} = \mu\,R\,\left(g + \frac{F_L}{m}\right)$$ $$V = \sqrt{\mu\,R\,\left(g + \frac{F_L}{m}\right)}$$ **Step 3 — Case (a): Ignoring downforce ($F_L = 0$).** $$V_a = \sqrt{\mu\,R\,g} = \sqrt{1.0 \times 100 \times 9.81}$$ $$V_a = \sqrt{981} = 31.32 \text{ m/s} = 112.8 \text{ km/h}$$ **Step 4 — Case (b): Including downforce at the cornering speed.** We must solve this **self-consistently** because $F_L$ depends on $V$: $$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,V^{2}\,C_L\,A = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times V^{2} \times 0.45 \times 2.0 = 0.55125\,V^{2}$$ Substitute: $$V^{2} = \mu\,R\,g + \mu\,R\,\frac{F_L}{m} = 981 + 1.0 \times 100 \times \frac{0.55125\,V^{2}}{1450}$$ $$V^{2} = 981 + 0.03802\,V^{2}$$ $$V^{2}(1 - 0.03802) = 981$$ $$V^{2}(0.96198) = 981$$ $$V^{2} = 1019.7$$ $$V_b = 31.93 \text{ m/s} = 114.95 \text{ km/h}$$ **Step 5 — Percentage gain.** $$\Delta V\% = \frac{31.93 - 31.32}{31.32} \times 100 = 1.95\%$$ *Logic check:* At low $C_L$ and modest speeds, aero gain is marginal. The aero benefit scales with $V^2$, so it dominates at much higher speeds or with $C_L > 1$ (race-spec). --- ## 3.4 Problem D — Ground Effect (Underbody Venturi) > **Problem Statement:** A sports car has a venturi-shaped underbody. The throat constricts the underbody flow from $h_1 = 0.18 \text{ m}$ (entrance clearance) to $h_2 = 0.04 \text{ m}$ (throat). Freestream velocity $V_{\infty} = 60 \text{ m/s}$. Treating the underbody as a 1-D, incompressible, mass-conserving channel, find $V_{\text{throat}}$ and the pressure drop across the throat (assume $p_{\infty} = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$). ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Continuity equation (1-D mass conservation).** For an incompressible flow of constant width $w$: $$\rho\,V_1\,h_1\,w = \rho\,V_{\text{throat}}\,h_2\,w$$ $$V_{\text{throat}} = V_1 \cdot \frac{h_1}{h_2}$$ *Logic:* Same mass flow per second, smaller cross-section → higher velocity. **Step 2 — Approximate entrance velocity from freestream (Bernoulli between far upstream and entrance).** Assuming the underbody entrance velocity equals freestream: $V_1 \approx V_{\infty} = 60 \text{ m/s}$. **Step 3 — Compute throat velocity.** $$V_{\text{throat}} = 60 \times \frac{0.18}{0.04} = 60 \times 4.5 = 270 \text{ m/s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Reality check:** $V_{\text{throat}} = 270 \text{ m/s}$ is subsonic (Mach 0.79), so 1-D incompressible Bernoulli is borderline valid. In real cars, the diffuser is **angle-limited** (≤ ~7°) to prevent flow separation. If separation occurs, the venturi stalls and downforce collapses — known as **"porpoising"** in F1 (2022 rule era). **Step 4 — Apply Bernoulli along the streamline between freestream and throat.** $$p_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} = p_{\text{throat}} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\text{throat}}^{2}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $p_{\text{throat}}$.** $$p_{\text{throat}} = p_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\left(V_{\infty}^{2} - V_{\text{throat}}^{2}\right)$$ $$p_{\text{throat}} = 101{,}325 + 0.5 \times 1.225 \times \left(60^{2} - 270^{2}\right)$$ $$p_{\text{throat}} = 101{,}325 + 0.6125 \times (3600 - 72{,}900)$$ $$p_{\text{throat}} = 101{,}325 + 0.6125 \times (-69{,}300)$$ $$p_{\text{throat}} = 101{,}325 - 42{,}446 = 58{,}879 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 6 — Pressure drop.** $$\Delta p = p_{\infty} - p_{\text{throat}} = 101{,}325 - 58{,}879 = 42{,}446 \text{ Pa}$$ *Interpretation:* A suction of 42.4 kPa acts on the entire underbody. For a $2.0 \text{ m} \times 4.5 \text{ m} = 9.0 \text{ m}^2$ underbody (a generous estimate): $$F_{GE} \approx \Delta p \cdot A_{\text{under}} = 42{,}446 \times 9.0 = 382{,}014 \text{ N} \approx 38.9 \text{ tonnes}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** In an exam, students often forget that **downforce** is calculated from pressure integrated over the *underbody area*, not the *frontal area*. Use $A_{\text{underbody}}$ for ground effect, but $A_{\text{frontal}}$ for drag/downforce from wings. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sports Car Aero Subsystems The following Mermaid diagram maps how air interacts with the major aero components of a sports car, from freestream ingestion to wake generation. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Freestream Air
Rho = 1.225 kg per m3
V inf]:::input B1[Front Splitter
Yaw Stability]:::comp B2[Front Bumper Intakes
Brake Cooling]:::comp B3[Bonnet Vents
Engine Bay Heat Rejection]:::comp B4[Side Cooling Intakes
Mid Engine Radiators]:::comp B5[Side Skirts
Seal Underbody Flow]:::comp B6[Flat Underbody
Accelerates Air via Venturi]:::comp B7[Rear Diffuser
Expands and Recovers Flow]:::comp B8[Rear Wing
Primary Downforce Generator]:::comp B9[DRS or Active Flap
Reduces CD on Straights]:::comp B10[Rear Bumper Outlets
Wake Management]:::comp C1[Wake Region
Low Pressure Turbulence]:::output D1[Aero Balance Controller
ECU Active Aero Logic]:::ctrl A1 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A1 --> B3 B1 --> B4 B2 --> B6 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 B5 --> B6 B6 --> B7 B7 --> B8 B8 --> B9 B9 --> B10 B10 --> C1 D1 -. Active control signal .-> B8 D1 -. Active control signal .-> B9 D1 -. Active control signal .-> B7 classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000 classDef comp fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000 classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Blue node = incoming airflow. - Green nodes = individual aero components. - Red node = wake (energy lost). - Yellow node = the central ECU controlling active aero (DRS, active wing, ride height). ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Force & Energy Flow on a Sports Car ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Engine
Power Output Pe]:::src P2[Drivetrain Losses
eta dt = 0.90]:::loss P3[Tyre Rolling Resistance
mu r]:::loss P4[Aerodynamic Drag
F sub D]:::loss P5[Climbing Resistance
mg sin theta]:::loss P6[Kinetic Energy
0.5 m V squared]:::store P7[Downforce on Tyres
F sub L]:::aero P8[Lateral Grip Force
mu times N]:::grip P9[Vertical Load per Tyre
mg plus F sub L over 4]:::aero P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P2 --> P4 P2 --> P5 P2 --> P6 P7 --> P9 P9 --> P8 classDef src fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000 classDef loss fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000 classDef store fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b,color:#000 classDef aero fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000 classDef grip fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** The block diagram shows the dual role of aero on a sports car: 1. **Aero is a loss** — drag steals engine power (red path). 2. **Aero is a benefit** — downforce multiplies tire grip (green path). The art of sports-car aero design is **maximizing the green path while minimizing the red path.** ## 4.3 State Machine — Active Rear Wing Modes ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> LowDrag LowDrag --> HighDownforce : Brake detected OR
High steering angle HighDownforce --> MidDownforce : Steady state cruise MidDownforce --> LowDrag : Straight line V greater than 150 km per h LowDrag --> LowDrag : DRS flap open HighDownforce --> HighDownforce : Wing at max attack angle MidDownforce --> HighDownforce : Cornering OR Heavy braking ``` **Logic:** - **Low Drag mode** — wing flat or DRS open, used on straights for top speed. - **Mid Downforce** — moderate flap angle, used in mixed corners. - **High Downforce** — full attack angle, used under heavy braking or in tight hairpins. This is essentially how the McLaren P1, Porsche 918, and Ferrari SF90 Stradale manage their active wings. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PEMUT753] > **Define the term "lift-to-drag ratio" as applied to a sports car. Why is this ratio critical for the design of high-performance vehicles? A certain sports car has $C_D = 0.30$ and generates a downforce coefficient $C_L = 1.20$ at racing trim. Compute the L/D ratio and comment on its performance class.** **Model Answer (3-mark layout):** The **lift-to-drag ratio (L/D)** is the ratio of the downforce coefficient $C_L$ to the drag coefficient $C_D$ of a vehicle: $$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D}$$ It quantifies how efficiently a vehicle generates downforce per unit of drag penalty. Higher L/D means the car corners harder without losing straight-line speed — a critical trade-off in performance design. **Calculation:** $$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{1.20}{0.30} = 4.0$$ A ratio of 4.0 places the car in the **track-focused hypercar / Le Mans prototype** class (compare with grand tourers at 1.0 and F1 cars at 6.5–8.0). The vehicle can corner with significant grip advantage at only a moderate drag cost — suitable for circuit use. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT753] > **Explain the phenomenon of "ground effect" in a sports car. How does the venturi-shaped underbody contribute to downforce? State the Bernoulli-based relationship used in your explanation.** **Model Answer (3-mark layout):** **Ground effect** refers to the additional downforce generated by a sports car due to the constricted airflow between its underbody and the road surface. As the car moves, air is accelerated under the chassis (especially through a venturi-shaped tunnel), reducing static pressure below atmospheric. **Bernoulli's equation** between the freestream and the underbody throat: $$p_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} = p_{\text{throat}} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\text{throat}}^{2}$$ The low-pressure region under the car produces a **net downward suction force**, supplementing the downforce from the rear wing. This is why modern hypercars (Aston Martin Valkyrie, McLaren Solus GT) feature heavily sculpted underbodies — they can produce **more downforce with less drag** than a wing alone. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question B — Choice (A) — Comprehensive Design Problem [14 Marks] > **A track-focused sports car has the following specifications:** > - Mass $m = 1400 \text{ kg}$ > - Frontal area $A = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$ > - $C_D = 0.36$ at racing trim (with wing) > - $C_L = 0.85$ at racing trim (downforce positive) > - $C_D = 0.28$ at top-speed trim (wing in low-drag mode) > - $C_L = 0.15$ at top-speed trim > - Air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > > **(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the drag force, downforce, and the power required to maintain a velocity of $V = 72 \text{ m/s}$ (≈ 260 km/h) in racing trim. Also find the L/D ratio and effective weight per tire (4 equal tires). > > **(b) [7 marks]** If the same car switches to top-speed trim at the same velocity, calculate the new drag and downforce. By what percentage does the drag decrease? If the engine delivers a constant 400 kW at the wheels, find the maximum steady-state velocity in each trim (neglect rolling resistance and assume flat road). --- ### Solution to Question B(A) #### Part (a) — Racing Trim at 72 m/s [7 marks] **Step 1 — Velocity squared:** $$V^{2} = 72^{2} = 5184 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Dynamic pressure head:** $$q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5184 = 3175.2 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Drag force (racing trim):** $$F_{D,R} = q \cdot C_D \cdot A = 3175.2 \times 0.36 \times 2.0 = 2286.1 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Downforce (racing trim):** $$F_{L,R} = q \cdot C_L \cdot A = 3175.2 \times 0.85 \times 2.0 = 5397.8 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5 — L/D ratio:** $$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_R = \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{0.85}{0.36} = 2.36$$ **Step 6 — Power required at 72 m/s:** $$P_D = F_D \cdot V = 2286.1 \times 72 = 164{,}599 \text{ W} \approx 164.6 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 7 — Static weight:** $$W = mg = 1400 \times 9.81 = 13{,}734 \text{ N}$$ **Step 8 — Total vertical load:** $$N_{\text{total}} = W + F_{L,R} = 13{,}734 + 5397.8 = 19{,}131.8 \text{ N}$$ **Step 9 — Load per tire:** $$N_{\text{tire}} = \frac{19{,}131.8}{4} = 4783.0 \text{ N} \approx 487.6 \text{ kgf}$$ $$\boxed{F_{D,R} = 2286 \text{ N}, \quad F_{L,R} = 5398 \text{ N}, \quad P_D = 164.6 \text{ kW}, \quad L/D = 2.36, \quad N_{\text{tire}} \approx 488 \text{ kgf}}$$ #### Part (b) — Top-Speed Trim and Maximum Velocity [7 marks] **Step 1 — Drag force (top-speed trim) at 72 m/s:** $$F_{D,T} = q \cdot C_D \cdot A = 3175.2 \times 0.28 \times 2.0 = 1778.1 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Downforce (top-speed trim):** $$F_{L,T} = 3175.2 \times 0.15 \times 2.0 = 952.6 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Percentage drag reduction:** $$\%\Delta F_D = \frac{F_{D,R} - F_{D,T}}{F_{D,R}} \times 100 = \frac{2286.1 - 1778.1}{2286.1} \times 100$$ $$\%\Delta F_D = \frac{508}{2286.1} \times 100 = 22.2\%$$ **Step 4 — Maximum velocity in racing trim:** At terminal velocity, the engine power equals drag power: $$P_{\text{engine}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\max}^{3} C_D A$$ $$V_{\max,R} = \left(\frac{2 P_{\text{engine}}}{\rho\,C_D\,A}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{2 \times 400{,}000}{1.225 \times 0.36 \times 2.0}\right)^{1/3}$$ $$V_{\max,R} = \left(\frac{800{,}000}{0.882}\right)^{1/3} = \left(907{,}120\right)^{1/3} = 96.55 \text{ m/s} = 347.6 \text{ km/h}$$ **Step 5 — Maximum velocity in top-speed trim:** $$V_{\max,T} = \left(\frac{2 \times 400{,}000}{1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.0}\right)^{1/3}$$ $$V_{\max,T} = \left(\frac{800{,}000}{0.686}\right)^{1/3} = (1{,}166{,}472)^{1/3} = 105.27 \text{ m/s} = 378.97 \text{ km/h}$$ **Step 6 — Speed gain from switching trim:** $$\Delta V = 378.97 - 347.6 = 31.4 \text{ km/h} \quad (\approx 9\% \text{ faster})$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Sports Car Aero Problems:** > 1. **Unit conversion trap:** Students often forget to convert km/h to m/s before cubing. KTU examiners award 0 marks for $V$ plugged in as km/h. > 2. **L/D vs. C_L/C_D confusion:** Both are correct only when the reference area $A$ is the same. Always state which $A$ you used. > 3. **Downforce sign:** Never write $F_L$ as "negative lift" or use a negative sign — KTU automotive convention is positive = downforce. > 4. **Cubic-root calculator mistake:** When finding $V_{\max}$ from $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^3 C_D A$, students often cube the wrong side. Re-derive carefully. --- ### Question B — Choice (B) — Conceptual + Analytical [14 Marks] > **(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a labeled block diagram, explain the four main aerodynamic components of a typical mid-engine sports car (front splitter, flat underbody, rear diffuser, rear wing). Describe how each contributes to downforce or drag reduction. > > **(b) [7 marks]** A sports car has mass 1500 kg and is fitted with a rear wing of planform area $S = 1.5 \text{ m}^2$. The wing operates as an inverted airfoil with lift coefficient $C_{L,\text{wing}} = 1.4$ at angle of attack $\alpha = 12°$. At a velocity of $V = 80 \text{ m/s}$ (288 km/h), compute: > - (i) The downforce produced by the wing alone. > - (ii) The induced drag of the wing, assuming an Oswald efficiency factor $e = 0.85$ and aspect ratio $AR = 4$. > - (iii) The L/D ratio of the wing alone. > - (iv) The total drag power if the wing-induced drag is added to a body drag of 250 N. > Use $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. --- ### Solution to Question B(B) #### Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation [7 marks] The four aerodynamic components work synergistically: 1. **Front Splitter** — A horizontal lip at the front of the car. Generates a small front-axle downforce by acting as a **low-aspect-ratio inverted airfoil**. More importantly, it deflects high-energy air *around* the car rather than under it, preventing the underbody pressure from "leaking" forward. Reduces front-end lift by 30–50% in well-designed systems. 2. **Flat Underbody** — A sealed, smooth surface between the front splitter and the rear diffuser. Acts as the **lower wall of a venturi channel**. Combined with the diffuser, it accelerates the underbody air, dropping its pressure (Bernoulli) and creating strong downforce with minimal drag penalty. 3. **Rear Diffuser** — A flared section at the rear underbody that **gradually expands** the constricted underbody flow back to ambient pressure. Its function is to *recover* pressure smoothly, preventing flow separation that would otherwise kill downforce. Angle-limited to ≤ 7° to avoid stall. The diffuser is the **most efficient** downforce-generating component per unit of drag. 4. **Rear Wing** — An inverted airfoil mounted above the rear deck. Provides high downforce by virtue of high $C_L$, but at the cost of substantial drag (especially **induced drag** proportional to $C_L^2$). Often **active** (multi-element, DRS) to switch between low-drag and high-downforce modes. **Block diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[Front Splitter]:::c1 F2[Flat Underbody]:::c1 F3[Rear Diffuser]:::c1 F4[Rear Wing]:::c2 F1 -->|Directs flow| F2 F2 -->|Accelerates flow| F3 F3 -->|Recovers pressure| OUT[Low Pressure Wake]:::out F4 -->|High lift but draggy| OUT classDef c1 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000 classDef c2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03,color:#000 classDef out fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000 ``` #### Part (b) — Rear Wing Quantitative Analysis [7 marks] **Step 1 — Velocity squared:** $$V^{2} = 80^{2} = 6400 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Dynamic pressure:** $$q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6400 = 3920 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3 — (i) Downforce from the wing:** $$F_{L,\text{wing}} = q \cdot C_{L,\text{wing}} \cdot S = 3920 \times 1.4 \times 1.5 = 8232 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — (ii) Induced drag coefficient of the wing:** The induced drag coefficient of a finite wing is: $$C_{D,i} = \frac{C_{L}^{2}}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}$$ Substituting: $$C_{D,i} = \frac{(1.4)^{2}}{\pi \times 0.85 \times 4} = \frac{1.96}{10.681} = 0.1835$$ **Step 5 — Induced drag force:** $$F_{D,i} = q \cdot C_{D,i} \cdot S = 3920 \times 0.1835 \times 1.5 = 1078.9 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6 — (iii) Wing L/D ratio:** $$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\text{wing}} = \frac{C_L}{C_{D,i}} = \frac{1.4}{0.1835} = 7.63$$ **Step 7 — (iv) Total drag power:** Total drag = body drag + induced drag: $$F_{D,\text{total}} = 250 + 1078.9 = 1328.9 \text{ N}$$ Power required: $$P_{\text{total}} = F_{D,\text{total}} \cdot V = 1328.9 \times 80 = 106{,}312 \text{ W} \approx 106.3 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{F_{L,\text{wing}} = 8232 \text{ N}, \quad F_{D,i} = 1079 \text{ N}, \quad L/D_{\text{wing}} = 7.63, \quad P_{\text{total}} = 106.3 \text{ kW}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Wing Analysis Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $C_L$ for the wing with $C_L$ for the whole car:** The wing L/D is computed using **wing planform area** $S$, not the car's frontal area $A$. Mixing them up is the #1 mistake. > 2. **Induced-drag formula trap:** $C_{D,i} = C_L^2 / (\pi e AR)$ applies only to a **3D finite wing**. Do not apply it to the entire car unless you justify with an effective $AR$. > 3. **Forgetting to add body drag:** The wing's drag is on top of the body's drag; the total system drag is **additive**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Sports Car Aerodynamics** **Core Concepts (must know verbatim):** - ☐ Sports car = performance-class vehicle optimized for **downforce + low drag + balanced aero**. - ☐ Drag force: $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_D A$ - ☐ Downforce force: $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_L A$ (sign convention: positive = down) - ☐ Drag power: $P_D = F_D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{3} C_D A$ → **cubic in velocity**. - ☐ Aerodynamic efficiency (L/D) = $C_L/C_D$ → higher is better. - ☐ Ground effect: venturi underbody → low pressure → suction downforce. - ☐ Aerodynamic balance: front:rear downforce ratio for stability. - ☐ Active aero: DRS, multi-element wings, ride-height control. **Numerical / Computational Skills:** - ☐ Convert km/h → m/s by multiplying by 1000/3600 = 0.2778. - ☐ Dynamic pressure $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$ in Pa (N/m²). - ☐ Maximum cornering speed: $V = \sqrt{\mu R(g + F_L/m)}$ — must solve self-consistently if $F_L$ depends on $V$. - ☐ Terminal velocity from engine power: $V_{\max} = \left(2P / \rho C_D A\right)^{1/3}$. - ☐ Induced drag: $C_{D,i} = C_L^{2} / (\pi e AR)$. - ☐ Bernoulli for venturi: $p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} = p_{\text{throat}} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\text{throat}}^{2}$. **Critical Pitfalls (lose marks if missed):** - ☐ Always state the **sign convention** for $C_L$ in your solution. - ☐ Use **wing planform area** $S$ for wing calculations; use **frontal area** $A$ for whole-car drag/downforce. - ☐ Watch the difference between "**lift**" (aerospace) and "**downforce**" (automotive). - ☐ Don't confuse aerodynamic balance (% front) with weight distribution (mass-based). - ☐ Bernoulli applies only to **inviscid, incompressible, steady flow along a streamline** — flag when violated. **Comparison Numbers to Memorize (KTU 2024 frequently asked):** - ☐ Family sedan: $C_D \approx 0.30$, $C_L \approx 0$ (neutral lift) - ☐ Sports car: $C_D \approx 0.28 - 0.38$, $C_L \approx 0.20 - 1.20$ - ☐ F1 car: $C_D \approx 0.85 - 1.00$, $C_L \approx 3.0 - 4.0$ (massive!) - ☐ Standard air density: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - ☐ Standard gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Application Linkages:** - ☐ Sports car aero feeds into **Module 5 (Race Car Aerodynamics)** — same physics, more extreme. - ☐ Ground-effect phenomenon connects to **Module 3 (Boundary Layer & Flow Control)**. - ☐ Active aero links to **Module 6 (Modern Vehicle Control Systems)**. - ☐ Drag-cube-law ($P \propto V^3$) connects to **Module 2 (Energy & Power Requirements)**.

topics.title: SUVs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT753 - AUTOMOTIVE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SUVs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SUVs — Aerodynamic Characteristics & Design Considerations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sport Utility Vehicle (SUV)** is a category of motor vehicle that combines elements of road-passenger cars with features borrowed from off-road vehicles, characterized by a **high ground clearance**, **large frontal area**, **bluff (box-like) body geometry**, and elevated center of gravity. From an aerodynamic standpoint, SUVs are classified as **bluff bodies** — bodies whose geometric proportions result in **massive flow separation** even at moderate Reynolds numbers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** SUVs are studied as a *contrast case* to low-drag passenger cars. They exhibit inherently poor aerodynamic performance due to their boxy geometry but represent one of the **fastest-growing automotive segments globally**, making their aerodynamic optimization critical for fuel economy and emissions compliance.

**Standard Metrics Used in SUV Aerodynamics:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical SUV Range | Units |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|-------|
| Drag Coefficient | $C_D$ | **0.35 – 0.45** | dimensionless |
| Frontal Area | $A$ | **2.5 – 3.2** | m² |
| Lift Coefficient | $C_L$ | **0.30 – 0.55** | dimensionless |
| Ground Clearance | $h_{gc}$ | **180 – 220** | mm |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Moving Billboard"

Imagine pushing a **large wooden wardrobe** through still air versus pushing a **slim office chair**. The wardrobe (SUV) acts like a moving billboard — air slams into its broad, flat front face, cannot follow the abrupt roofline, and breaks away (separates) immediately, creating a **large turbulent wake** behind. The chair (sedan) is shaped so air glides smoothly around it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** An SUV's *frontal area* is roughly **40–50% larger** than a sedan's, and its *drag coefficient* is roughly **40–60% higher**. When these two factors multiply in the drag equation, an SUV can suffer **2× to 3× the aerodynamic drag** of a comparable sedan at the same speed.

This is why SUV manufacturers invest heavily in subtle body shaping, underbody panels, and active aerodynamic devices — every **0.01 reduction in $C_D$** is equivalent to removing several kilograms of mass from the vehicle in terms of fuel economy benefit.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Why SUVs Are Aerodynamically Challenging

SUVs suffer from poor aerodynamics due to **three concurrent geometric factors**:

1. **Large Frontal Area ($A$)** — The tall, broad front face presents a large obstruction to incoming airflow. Since drag force scales linearly with $A$, this alone inflates drag.

2. **High Drag Coefficient ($C_D$)** — The near-vertical windshield, flat roof, and abruptly truncated rear create **large adverse pressure gradients**, forcing premature boundary layer separation. This produces a massive low-pressure wake region that "pulls" the vehicle backward.

3. **Elevated Ride Height** — More air flows *underneath* the vehicle, churning through the drivetrain, suspension, and exhaust components. This underbody flow contributes significantly to **parasitic drag**.

## 2.2 The Bluff-Body Wake Structure

For a typical SUV traveling at highway speed, the wake consists of:

- **Recirculation zone** behind the rear vertical face
- **Shear layers** rolling up from roof edges and C-pillars
- **Vortex shedding** from the roof trailing edge (similar to a flat-plate normal flow)
- **Base pressure deficit** creating suction drag

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-essential** for KTU Module 4 questions on SUVs.

### Core Aerodynamic Force Equations

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---------|------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Aerodynamic Drag Force** | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_D\,A$ | $F_D$ [N], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $V$ [m/s], $C_D$ [—], $A$ [m²] | Total resistance force opposing motion |
| **Aerodynamic Lift Force** | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_L\,A$ | $F_L$ [N], $C_L$ [—] | Vertical force (positive = lift, negative = downforce) |
| **Side Force** | $F_S = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_{S\beta}\,\beta\,A$ | $F_S$ [N], $C_{S\beta}$ [1/rad], $\beta$ [rad] | Crosswind-induced lateral force |
| **Yaw Moment** | $M_Z = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_{M\beta}\,\beta\,A\,\ell$ | $M_Z$ [N·m], $C_{M\beta}$ [1/rad], $\ell$ [m] | Rotational tendency in crosswind |
| **Power to Overcome Drag** | $P_D = F_D \cdot V$ | $P_D$ [W] | Engine power consumed by drag |
| **Horsepower Loss to Drag** | $HP_{loss} = \dfrac{F_D \cdot V}{745.7}$ | $HP_{loss}$ [hp] | Equivalent mechanical power loss |
| **Drag Area (Metric)** | $D \cdot A = C_D \cdot A$ | [m²] | Combined drag metric for fleet comparison |
| **Reynolds Number (Vehicle)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\,V\,\ell}{\mu}$ | $Re$ [—], $\ell$ [m], $\mu$ [Pa·s] | Flow regime indicator (typical SUV: $Re \approx 1.5\times10^{7}$) |

### SUV-Specific Empirical Correlations

| Correlation | Formula | Application |
|-------------|---------|-------------|
| **Approximate $C_D$ from Form Factor** | $C_D \approx 2\,\left(\dfrac{A_{max}}{A}\right)\,(1-\sigma)^{3/2}$ | Bluff-body drag estimation |
| **Fuel Penalty Coefficient** | $\Delta \dot{m}_f \propto C_D \cdot A \cdot V^{2}$ | Linking aerodynamics to fuel consumption |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Range Extension:** For electric SUVs (e.g., Tesla Model X), every **0.01 drop in $C_D$** can extend range by approximately **5–8 km** per charge.
- **Emissions Compliance:** OEMs target $C_D \cdot A \leq 0.70$ m² for SUVs to meet upcoming EU and Bharat Stage-VII $\text{CO}_2$ norms.
- **High-Speed Stability:** SUVs experience greater lift-induced **porpoising** and **crosswind sensitivity** due to elevated $C_L$ and side force derivatives.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comparative Drag Analysis: SUV vs Sedan

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 4. We derive the percentage difference in drag force between a typical SUV and a comparable sedan.

### Given Data

| Parameter | Sedan | SUV |
|-----------|-------|-----|
| Frontal Area $A$ | **2.10 m²** | **2.90 m²** |
| Drag Coefficient $C_D$ | **0.28** | **0.40** |
| Air Density $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Velocity $V$ | **30 m/s (108 km/h)** | **30 m/s** |

### Step 1 — Drag Force on the Sedan

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{D,\text{sedan}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_{D,\text{sedan}}\,A_{\text{sedan}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{D,\text{sedan}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times (1.225) \times (30)^{2} \times (0.28) \times (2.10) \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 0.28 \times 2.10 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 0.588 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 529.2 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 648.27 \\[4pt]
F_{D,\text{sedan}} &\approx 324.14 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Drag Force on the SUV

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{D,\text{SUV}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_{D,\text{SUV}}\,A_{\text{SUV}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{D,\text{SUV}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times (1.225) \times (30)^{2} \times (0.40) \times (2.90) \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 0.40 \times 2.90 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 1.16 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1044 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1278.9 \\[4pt]
F_{D,\text{SUV}} &\approx 639.45 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Percentage Increase in Drag

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\Delta F_D &= \dfrac{F_{D,\text{SUV}} - F_{D,\text{sedan}}}{F_{D,\text{sedan}}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\Delta F_D &= \dfrac{639.45 - 324.14}{324.14} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{315.31}{324.14} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\%\,\Delta F_D &\approx 97.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The SUV experiences **nearly double (≈97%) the aerodynamic drag** of the sedan under identical conditions, purely due to its geometric disadvantage — despite both being powered at the same cruising speed.

### Step 4 — Power Required to Overcome Drag

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{D,\text{sedan}} &= F_{D,\text{sedan}} \times V = 324.14 \times 30 = 9724.2 \text{ W} \approx 13.04 \text{ hp}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{D,\text{SUV}} &= F_{D,\text{SUV}} \times V = 639.45 \times 30 = 19183.5 \text{ W} \approx 25.73 \text{ hp}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** The SUV consumes **~12.7 hp more** than the sedan purely to overcome air resistance at 108 km/h. Over a 500 km highway drive, this translates to **roughly 0.6 – 1.0 litres of additional fuel**.

---

## 3.2 SUV-Specific Aerodynamic Design Features

| Feature | Function | Drag Reduction |
|---------|----------|----------------|
| **Front Air Dam** | Reduces air entering underbody | $\Delta C_D \approx 0.01 - 0.02$ |
| **Rear Roof Spoiler** | Delays roof separation | $\Delta C_D \approx 0.02 - 0.04$ |
| **Underbody Panels** | Smooths underbody flow | $\Delta C_D \approx 0.03 - 0.05$ |
| **Active Grille Shutters** | Closes grille at low cooling demand | $\Delta C_D \approx 0.01 - 0.03$ |
| **Rear Diffuser** | Accelerates underbody flow, recovers pressure | $\Delta C_D \approx 0.02 - 0.04$ |
| **Wheel Arch Liners** | Reduces wheel-well turbulence | $\Delta C_D \approx 0.01 - 0.02$ |
| **Side Mirrors / Camera Pods** | Replaces draggy mirrors | $\Delta C_D \approx 0.02 - 0.05$ |

---

## 3.3 Crosswind Sensitivity Derivation

For an SUV, the **side force coefficient derivative** $C_{S\beta}$ is typically **50–80% higher** than that of a sedan. The yaw moment derivative $C_{M\beta}$ determines whether the vehicle *tends to weathervane into the wind* (stable) or *weathervane away* (unstable — dangerous).

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_Z &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_{M\beta}\,\beta\,A\,\ell
\end{aligned}
$$

For a typical SUV with $\ell = 2.8$ m, $A = 2.9$ m², $C_{M\beta} = 0.15$ /rad, $V = 25$ m/s, $\beta = 10^{\circ} = 0.1745$ rad:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_Z &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (25)^{2} \times 0.15 \times 0.1745 \times 2.9 \times 2.8 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 625 \times 0.15 \times 0.1745 \times 8.12 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 625 \times 0.2125 \\[4pt]
M_Z &\approx 81.4 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many forget to convert the yaw angle from **degrees to radians** before substitution. Always multiply degrees by $\pi/180$.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Explain why SUVs have a higher drag coefficient than sedans. Mention typical $C_D$ values.**

**Model Answer:**

SUVs have a higher drag coefficient than sedans primarily due to their **bluff-body geometry**. The following factors contribute:

1. **Near-vertical windshield** — Creates a strong adverse pressure gradient right after the A-pillar, causing early flow separation.
2. **Flat, blunt roof** — Promotes massive separation at the rear trailing edge, forming a large low-pressure wake.
3. **Tall and broad frontal area** — Increases stagnation pressure and frontal pressure drag.
4. **Minimal rear tapering** — Unlike fastback sedans, SUVs cannot smoothly converge the flow at the rear.
5. **Open underbody cavities** — The high ground clearance exposes the drivetrain and suspension to turbulent underbody flow.

**Typical $C_D$ Values:**

| Vehicle Type | $C_D$ Range |
|--------------|-------------|
| Modern Sedan | **0.22 – 0.28** |
| Hatchback | **0.30 – 0.35** |
| SUV | **0.35 – 0.45** |
| Box Truck | **0.55 – 0.70** |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**List any four aerodynamic modifications used in modern SUVs to reduce drag.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Rear Roof Spoilers** — Delay flow separation at the roof trailing edge, reducing wake size.
2. **Underbody Panel Fairings** — Smoothen underbody airflow and reduce parasitic drag.
3. **Active Grille Shutters** — Close the radiator grille at low cooling loads to reduce frontal blockage.
4. **Side Camera Pods Replacing Mirrors** — Eliminate the draggy mirror pods, often saving 0.02–0.05 in $C_D$.
5. *(Bonus)* **Rear Diffusers** — Accelerate underbody flow and recover base pressure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: **Module Internal Choice** — answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) and (b) for **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **wake structure behind a typical SUV** and discuss how it contributes to **pressure drag**.

**(b)** A mid-size SUV with frontal area $A = 2.85$ m² and drag coefficient $C_D = 0.38$ is travelling at a constant speed of **120 km/h** in still air ($\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³). Calculate:
   1. The **aerodynamic drag force** in Newtons.
   2. The **power required to overcome drag** in kW and hp.
   3. The **fuel consumption rate** in litres/hour if the engine-propeller efficiency is 28% and fuel calorific value is **44 MJ/kg** with density **0.745 kg/L**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The wake structure behind a typical SUV consists of:

- **Recirculation Zone** — Located directly behind the rear vertical face, this region contains slow-moving, low-pressure fluid that "pulls" the vehicle backward (base drag).
- **Shear Layers** — Formed at the roof trailing edge and C-pillar; these layers roll up into **trailing vortices** that further enlarge the wake.
- **Base Pressure Deficit** — Pressure in the wake is significantly below ambient, creating a net rearward force.

**Diagram (described):** Vertical SUV silhouette with arrows showing inflow from the front, separation at the roof edge, a large recirculation bubble behind the tailgate, and vortices at the C-pillars.

**Contribution to Pressure Drag:** Approximately **55–60% of total drag** on an SUV is pressure drag, of which the majority originates from the **base pressure deficit** in the wake. Form drag dominates over skin friction drag because the SUV's bluff shape creates flow separation almost immediately.

**Marking Key:**
- Sketch with proper labels: **3 marks**
- Explanation of wake components: **2 marks**
- Discussion of pressure drag contribution: **2 marks**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $A = 2.85$ m²
- $C_D = 0.38$
- $V = 120 \text{ km/h} = 33.33 \text{ m/s}$
- $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Aerodynamic Drag Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_D &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_D\,A \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.20 \times (33.33)^{2} \times 0.38 \times 2.85 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.20 \times 1110.89 \times 1.083 \\[4pt]
&\approx 721.7 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Power to Overcome Drag:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= F_D \times V = 721.7 \times 33.33 \\[4pt]
&\approx 24056 \text{ W} \approx 24.06 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to horsepower:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HP_{loss} &= \dfrac{24056}{745.7} \approx 32.26 \text{ hp}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fuel Consumption Rate:**

Engine power required:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{engine} &= \dfrac{P_D}{\eta} = \dfrac{24.06}{0.28} \approx 85.93 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Heat energy required from fuel per second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= P_{engine} = 85.93 \text{ kJ/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Mass flow rate of fuel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_f &= \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\text{CV}} = \dfrac{85.93}{44000} \approx 1.953 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to litres per hour:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_f &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{\rho_{fuel}} = \dfrac{1.953 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600}{0.745} \\[4pt]
&\approx \dfrac{7.031}{0.745} \approx 9.44 \text{ L/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion:** $120$ km/h must be converted to m/s by multiplying by $1000/3600 = 5/18$. Many students incorrectly use 120 directly, yielding answers off by a factor of ~3.6.
> 2. **Engine efficiency** must be applied AFTER computing mechanical drag power, not before.
> 3. **Fuel density** (kg/L) must be inverted correctly when converting from kg/s to L/h.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **aerodynamic challenges specific to SUVs**, including high lift, crosswind sensitivity, and cooling demands. How do modern SUVs mitigate these issues?

**(b)** An electric SUV has the following parameters: $C_D = 0.29$ (after optimization), $A = 2.70$ m², battery capacity = **85 kWh**, rolling resistance coefficient $C_r = 0.012$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt} = 0.88$. Calculate the **range in km** at a constant cruising speed of **90 km/h** on a flat road. Use $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³ and vehicle mass $m = 2300$ kg.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Key Aerodynamic Challenges of SUVs:**

1. **High Lift Generation:**
   - The flat upper surface and roof shape generate positive lift ($C_L \approx 0.30-0.55$).
   - This reduces front-axle grip, causing **porpoising** and reduced high-speed stability.
   - *Mitigation:* Add rear roof spoilers that act as small airfoils, generating downforce.

2. **Crosswind Sensitivity:**
   - Tall body and large side area produce large side-force and yaw-moment derivatives.
   - *Mitigation:* Active rear winglets, side curtains, and ESP (Electronic Stability Program) intervention.

3. **Cooling Demands:**
   - Larger engines, towing loads, and off-road usage produce more heat.
   - SUVs need larger grilles and radiators — but open grilles increase drag.
   - *Mitigation:* **Active Grille Shutters** that open only when cooling is demanded.

4. **Underbody Turbulence:**
   - High ground clearance means more exposed mechanical components.
   - *Mitigation:* Underbody panels and rear diffusers.

**Marking Key:**
- Identification of 3+ challenges: **3 marks**
- Mitigation strategies: **3 marks**
- Clarity and flow: **1 mark**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $V = 90$ km/h = **25 m/s**
- $C_D = 0.29$, $A = 2.70$ m², $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³
- $m = 2300$ kg, $C_r = 0.012$
- $\eta_{dt} = 0.88$, $E_{batt} = 85$ kWh = $85 \times 3600$ kJ = **306,000 kJ**

**Step 1 — Aerodynamic Drag Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_D &= 0.5 \times 1.20 \times (25)^{2} \times 0.29 \times 2.70 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.20 \times 625 \times 0.783 \\[4pt]
&\approx 293.6 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rolling Resistance Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_r &= C_r \times m \times g = 0.012 \times 2300 \times 9.81 \\[4pt]
&\approx 270.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Tractive Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= F_D + F_r = 293.6 + 270.8 = 564.4 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Power Demand at Wheels:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel} &= F_{total} \times V = 564.4 \times 25 = 14110 \text{ W} \approx 14.11 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Power Demand from Battery:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{batt} &= \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{dt}} = \dfrac{14.11}{0.88} \approx 16.03 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Cruising Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{batt}} = \dfrac{85}{16.03} \approx 5.30 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= V \times t = 90 \text{ km/h} \times 5.30 \text{ h} \approx 477 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to include **rolling resistance** — many students compute only the drag power, overestimating range.
> 2. Drivetrain efficiency is applied **at the battery-to-wheel interface**, not on drag power alone.
> 3. Battery energy must be in consistent units (kWh vs. kJ) before dividing by power in kW.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SUVs (Module 4)**

- **SUVs are bluff bodies** — high drag due to early flow separation at roof trailing edge.
- **Typical $C_D$ range: 0.35 – 0.45**; frontal area: 2.5 – 3.2 m².
- **Drag force equation:** $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_D\,A$ — both $C_D$ and $A$ are inherently large for SUVs.
- **Approximately 55–60% of SUV drag is pressure drag**, dominated by base pressure deficit in the wake.
- **Power to overcome drag:** $P_D = F_D \cdot V$ — doubles when speed doubles (cubic relationship).
- **Modern SUV aero features:** underbody panels, active grille shutters, rear spoilers, diffusers, camera pods.
- **Crosswind sensitivity:** Higher $C_{S\beta}$ and $C_{M\beta}$ derivatives → ESP is critical.
- **Lift coefficient $C_L$ of 0.30–0.55** creates stability issues; rear spoilers generate downforce.
- **EV relevance:** Reducing $C_D$ directly extends battery range — every **0.01 drop** ≈ **5–8 km** extra range.
- **Yaw angle $\beta$ must always be in radians** in moment calculations: multiply degrees by $\pi/180$.
- **Drag area metric** $C_D \cdot A$ is the industry-standard for comparing vehicle aerodynamic efficiency.
- **Fuel consumption scales linearly** with $C_D \cdot A$ at constant speed (assuming fixed engine efficiency).
- **Active aerodynamics** (movable flaps, grille shutters) become especially valuable on SUVs because their drag budget is large.
